▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.36 Mucormycosis
[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Fungi → Mucorales (Mucormycosis)
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Rhino-orbital-cerebral (ROCM), pulmonary, cutaneous, disseminated, diabetic ketoacidosis, iron overload, deferoxamine, amphotericin B, surgical debridement, mortality

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Identify Aetiology: Mucorales (Rhizopus, Mucor, Rhizomucor, Lichtheimia, Cunninghamella) — Angioinvasion, Tissue Necrosis
  • [ ] Recognise Risk Factors: Diabetes (DKA), Iron Overload / Deferoxamine, Neutropenia, Transplant, Corticosteroids, Malignancy, Burns, Trauma
  • [ ] Recognise Clinical Forms: Rhino-Orbital-Cerebral (ROCM) (Most Common), Pulmonary, Cutaneous, GI, Disseminated
  • [ ] Diagnose: KOH Mount / Histopathology (Broad, Aseptate, Right-Angle Branching Hyphae), Culture, PCR, Imaging (CT/MRI)
  • [ ] Manage: Liposomal Amphotericin B (High Dose) + Surgical Debridement (Urgent), Iron Chelation Discontinue, Glycaemic Control
  • [ ] Recognise Complications: Cerebral Invasion, Carotid Artery Invasion, Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis, Dissemination
  • [ ] Recognise C. auris vs Mucormycosis: Different Organisms, Different Treatment

2. 📖 Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetails
Causative AgentsMucorales: Rhizopus (Most Common), Mucor, Rhizomucor, Lichtheimia, Cunninghamella, Apophysomyces, Saksenaea
PathophysiologyAngioinvasion → Vascular Thrombosis → Tissue Necrosis → Haematogenous Dissemination
Risk FactorsDiabetes (DKA) — 50-70%, Iron Overload / Deferoxamine, Neutropenia, HSCT, Corticosteroids, Malignancy, Burns, Trauma, Prematurity
Iron OverloadDeferoxamine → Acts as Siderophore for Mucorales → Enhanced Growth
GeographyWorldwide; India (High Burden, Rhino-Orbital-Cerebral), Diabetes/DKA Major Driver
MortalityOverall 50-80%; ROCM 50-85%; Disseminated >90%
[!warning] Mucormycosis = Angioinvasion → Tissue Necrosis → Rapid Progression. Diabetes/DKA = #1 Risk Factor. Deferoxamine = Siderophore for Mucorales. Mortality 50-80% even with treatment.

3. 📖 Clinical Forms

FormFrequencyKey Features
Rhino-Orbital-Cerebral (ROCM)Most Common (40-50%)Sinusitis, Periorbital Swelling, Proptosis, Ophthalmoplegia, Vision Loss, Palatal/Nasal Eschar (Black Necrosis), Cranial Nerve Palsies, Cerebral Invasion → Altered Mental Status
Pulmonary20-30%Fever, Cough, Haemoptysis, Dyspnoea, Pleural Effusion, CXR/CT: Nodules, Consolidation, Cavitation, Reverse Halo Sign
Cutaneous10-15%Trauma/Burns/Surgery Site, Erythema → Necrosis → Eschar, Rapid Spread, Necrotising Fasciitis
GI<5%Abdominal Pain, Nausea/Vomiting, GI Bleeding, Perforation, Ischaemic Bowel
Disseminated10-20%Multiple Organs (Brain, Lungs, Skin, Spleen, Heart), High Mortality (>90%)

4. 📖 Risk Factors — The "Classic" Triad

Risk FactorMechanism
Diabetes + DKAAcidosis → Enhanced Fungal Growth; Iron Availability (Ketones Chelate Iron); Neutrophil Dysfunction
Iron Overload / DeferoxamineDeferoxamine = Siderophore for Mucorales → Enhanced Iron Acquisition → Rapid Growth
Neutropenia / ImmunosuppressionImpaired Neutrophil Function → Impaired Phagocytosis/Killing
[!warning] Diabetes + DKA = #1 Risk Factor (50-70%). Deferoxamine = Siderophore for Mucorales → Contraindicated in Suspected Mucormycosis. Steroid Use = ↑ Risk (COVID-19 Associated Mucormycosis).

5. 📖 Clinical Presentations

Rhino-Orbital-Cerebral (ROCM) — Most Common

FeatureDetails
InitialFacial Pain, Nasal Congestion/Discharge (Bloody/Black), Facial Swelling, Headache, Fever
OrbitalPeriorbital Swelling, Proptosis, Ophthalmoplegia (III, IV, VI Palsies), Vision Loss, Chemosis
Palate/NoseBlack Eschar (Necrotic Turbinates/Palate), Nasal Obliteration
CerebralAltered Mental Status, Seizures, Hemiparesis, Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis, Carotid Artery Invasion

Pulmonary

FeatureDetails
SymptomsFever, Cough, Haemoptysis, Dyspnoea, Chest Pain
ImagingNodules, Consolidation, Cavitation, Reverse Halo Sign (Atoll Sign), Air-Crescent Sign
HostNeutropenic, HSCT, Leukaemia, Steroids

Cutaneous

FeatureDetails
PortalTrauma, Burns, Surgery, IV Catheter, Injections
AppearanceErythema → Rapid Necrosis → Black Eschar, Rapid Spread, Necrotising Fasciitis

6. 🔬 Diagnostic Workup

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected Mucormycosis: Risk Factors + Clinical Syndrome
↓
**Imaging (Urgent)**
**Tissue Biopsy (Gold Standard)**
↓
**CT Sinuses/Orbit/Brain** (ROCM): Sinus Opacification, Bone Erosion, Orbital Invasion, Cerebral Invasion
**CT Chest** (Pulmonary): Nodules, Consolidation, Reverse Halo Sign
**MRI Brain** (Superior for Cerebral Invasion): Perineural Spread, Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis
**KOH Mount / Calcofluor White** (Rapid): **Broad, Aseptate, Right-Angle Branching Hyphae**
**Histopathology (H&E/GMS/PAS)**: **Broad, Aseptate, Right-Angle Branching Hyphae**, **Angioinvasion**
**Culture** (Sabouraud): **Rapid Growth (24-48h), Fluffy, Grey-Brown**
[**PCR / Sequencing** (Species ID) |

Key Diagnostic Features (Histopathology)

FeatureMucormycosisAspergillosisCandidiasis
HyphaeBroad (10-20µm), Aseptate (Coenocytic)Septate (3-5µm), Acute Angle Branching (45°)Yeast + Pseudohyphae (Septate)
BranchingRight-Angle (90°)Acute Angle (45°)Pseudohyphae (Elongated Yeast)
AngioinvasionProminentPresentRare
StainingH&E: Pale, Wide, Ribbon-LikeH&E: Narrow, SeptateH&E: Yeast + Pseudohyphae
[!tip] KOH Mount / Histopathology = Rapid Diagnosis. Broad, Aseptate, Right-Angle Branching Hyphae = Mucormycosis. Angioinvasion = Hallmark.

7. 💊 Management — The "Iron Triangle": Antifungal + Surgical + Metabolic Control

1. Antifungal Therapy

AgentDoseIndicationNotes
Liposomal Amphotericin B (LAmB)5-10 mg/kg/day IV (Up to 10 mg/kg in CNS)1st Line (All Forms)Renal Sparing, Higher Doses for CNS
Amphotericin B Deoxycholate1-1.5 mg/kg/day IVAlternative if LAmB UnavailableNephrotoxicity, Infusion Reactions
Posaconazole300mg BD (Day 1) → 300mg OD PO/IVStep-Down / Salvage / ProphylaxisGood CNS Penetration, CYP3A4 Interactions
Isavuconazole200mg IV/PO TID ×2d → 200mg ODAlternative / Step-DownGood CNS Penetration, Less Drug Interactions
CombinationLAmB + Posaconazole/IsavuconazoleSevere/RefractoryNo Strong RCT Evidence
[!warning] Echinocandins (Caspofungin/Micafungin/Anidulafungin) = NO ACTIVITY against Mucorales — DO NOT USE.

2. Surgical Debridement — Urgent & Radical

PrincipleDetails
TimingASAP (Within Hours of Diagnosis) — Delay = ↑ Mortality
ExtentRadical Debridement → Violable Tissue Removal (Necrotic Bone, Sinus, Orbit, Palate)
RepeatRe-Look Surgery q24-48h Until Healthy Tissue
SpecialisedMaxillofacial/ENT/Neurosurgery/Ophthalmology — Multidisciplinary Team
[!warning] Surgical Debridement = Life-Saving. Radical + Early = Survival. Orbital Exenteration if Orbital Invasion. Palatal/Maxillary Resection if Palatal Necrosis.

3. Metabolic Control (Critical Adjunct)

FactorTarget / Action
Glycaemic ControlStrict Glycaemic Control (Insulin Infusion) — DKA Resolution Critical
Iron ChelationSTOP Deferoxamine IMMEDIATELY — Siderophore for Mucorales
Acidosis CorrectionBicarbonate / Insulin — Correct DKA
ImmunosuppressionReduce/Stop Steroids/Immunosuppressants if Possible

8. 🩺 Clinical Forms & Complications

FormKey FeaturesMortality
ROCMSinusitis → Orbital → Cerebral, Palatal Eschar, Cranial Nerve Palsies, Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis, Carotid Artery Invasion50-85%
PulmonaryNodules, Consolidation, Cavitation, Reverse Halo Sign, Haemoptysis50-70%
CutaneousTrauma/Burns/IV Site, Rapid Necrosis, Eschar30-50%
DisseminatedMulti-Organ, Cerebral Haemorrhage, Septic Shock>90%

Complications

ComplicationMechanismManagement
Cavernous Sinus ThrombosisAngioinvasion → Cavernous SinusAnticoagulation (Controversial) + Antifungals + Surgery
Carotid Artery InvasionAngioinvasion → ICAStroke Risk, Endovascular/Surgical
Cerebral InfarctionAngioinvasion → MCA/ACA OcclusionAnticoagulation (Cautious) + Antifungals
Cranial Nerve PalsiesOrbital Apex / Cavernous Sinus InvasionAntifungals + Surgery

9. 🔬 Diagnosis Summary

TestSensitivity/SpecificityRole
KOH Mount / Calcofluor WhiteRapid (Minutes), High Sens (Broad, Aseptate, 90° Branching)Bedside Rapid Screening
Histopathology (H&E/GMS/PAS)Gold Standard (Tissue Invasion, Angioinvasion)Confirmatory
Culture (Sabouraud)Rapid Growth (24-48h), Fluffy, Grey-Brown, No Cycloheximide InhibitionSpecies ID
PCR / SequencingSpecies ID (Rhizopus, Mucor, Lichtheimia)Definitive
Imaging (CT/MRI)Extent, Invasion, Surgical PlanningStaging/Surgical Planning

10. 💊 Management Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected Mucormycosis
↓
**Urgent Imaging (CT/MRI)**
↓
**Tissue Biopsy + KOH Mount**
↓
**Confirmed?**
↓
**Immediate LAmB 5-10mg/kg IV**
↓
**Urgent Surgical Debridement**
↓
**Metabolic Control**: DKA Correction, Stop Deferoxamine, Glycaemic Control
↓
**Monitor**: Renal, Electrolytes, Electrolytes, Imaging Follow-up
↓
**Step-Down**: Posaconazole/Isavuconazole (After Improvement)
↓
**Duration**: 3-6 Weeks Minimum, Until Clinical/Radiological Resolution

11. 💊 Antifungal Regimens

AgentDoseIndication
Liposomal Amphotericin B (LAmB)5-10 mg/kg/day IV (Up to 10 mg/kg CNS)1st Line (All Forms)
Posaconazole300mg BD (Day 1) → 300mg OD PO/IVStep-Down / Salvage / Prophylaxis
Isavuconazole200mg IV/PO TID ×2d → 200mg ODAlternative / Step-Down
Amphotericin B Deoxycholate1-1.5 mg/kg/day IVIf LAmB Unavailable
CombinationLAmB + Posaconazole/IsavuconazoleSevere/Refractory (No RCT Evidence)
[!warning] ECHINOCANDINS (CASPOFUNGIN/MICAFUNGIN/ANIDULAFUNGIN) = NO ACTIVITY VS MUCORALES — CONTRAINDICATED AS MONOTHERAPY.

12. 💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
AetiologyMucorales (Rhizopus, Mucor, Rhizomucor), Angioinvasion → Necrosis
Risk FactorsDiabetes/DKA (50-70%), Deferoxamine (Siderophore), Neutropenia, Steroids, Transplant, Burns
Clinical FormsROCM (Most Common), Pulmonary, Cutaneous, GI, Disseminated
ROCMSinusitis → Orbit → Brain, Black Eschar, Proptosis, Ophthalmoplegia, CN Palsies, Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis
DiagnosisKOH Mount (Broad, Aseptate, 90° Branching), Histology (Angioinvasion), Culture, PCR, CT/MRI (Erosion, Invasion)
Treatment TriadLAmB 5-10mg/kg IV + Urgent Radical Debridement + Metabolic Control (DKA, Stop Deferoxamine)
AntifungalsLAmB 5-10mg/kg (1st Line); Posaconazole/Isavuconazole (Step-Down/Salvage); NO ECHINOCANDINS
SurgeryUrgent, Radical, Multidisciplinary (ENT, Neurosurgery, Ophthalmology, Maxillofacial)
MetabolicDKA Correction, STOP Deferoxamine, Glycaemic Control, Reduce Steroids
MortalityROCM 50-85%, Disseminated >90%

13. ❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the hallmark histopathological feature of Mucormycosis?
  • Broad (10-20µm), Aseptate (Coenocytic), Right-Angle (90°) Branching Hyphae with Angioinvasion.
  1. Why are Echinocandins contraindicated in Mucormycosis?
  • Mucorales Lack β-1,3-D-Glucan in Cell Wall (Target of Echinocandins) → Inherently Resistant.
  1. What is the significance of Deferoxamine in Mucormycosis?
  • Deferoxamine Acts as Siderophore for Mucorales → Enhances Iron Acquisition → Rapid Fungal Growth → Contraindicated in Suspected/Proven Mucormycosis.
  1. Describe the classic presentation of Rhino-Orbital-Cerebral Mucormycosis.
  • Diabetic/DKA Patient, Facial Pain/Swelling, Nasal Discharge (Bloody/Black), Periorbital Swelling, Proptosis, Ophthalmoplegia, Palatal/Nasal Black Eschar, Cranial Nerve Palsies, Altered Sensorium.
  1. What is the drug of choice for Mucormycosis?
  • Liposomal Amphotericin B 5-10 mg/kg/day IV (1st Line); Posaconazole/Isavuconazole for Step-Down/Salvage.
  1. Why are Echinocandins ineffective against Mucormycosis?
  • Mucorales Lack β-1,3-D-Glucan in Cell Wall (Target of Echinocandins) → Inherently Resistant.
  1. What is the "Iron Triangle" of Mucormycosis Management?
  • High-Dose LAmB + Radical Surgical Debridement + Metabolic Control (DKA Correction, Stop Deferoxamine, Glycaemic Control).
  1. Why is Deferoxamine contraindicated in Mucormycosis?
  • Acts as Siderophore → Binds Iron → Delivers to Mucorales → Enhances Fungal Growth & Virulence.
  1. What are the typical MRI findings in Rhino-Orbital-Cerebral Mucormycosis?
  • Sinus Opacification, Bone Erosion, Orbital Invasion, Perineural Spread, Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis, Cerebral Infarcts, Carotid Artery Invasion.
  1. How do you manage a patient with Cutaneous Mucormycosis at an insulin injection site?
  • Urgent Surgical Debridement + LAmB 5-10mg/kg IV + Glycaemic Control + Stop Deferoxamine.
  1. What is the "Reverse Halo Sign" and in which condition is it seen?
  • Central Ground-Glass Opacity Surrounded by Dense Consolidation Ring — Seen in Pulmonary Mucormycosis (Also Organising Pneumonia, TB).
  1. What is the role of Posaconazole in Mucormycosis?
  • Step-Down Therapy (After LAmB Improvement), Salvage (Refractory), Primary Prophylaxis (High-Risk: HSCT, GVHD).

14. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Mucormycosis vs AspergillosisMucor: Broad, Aseptate, 90° Branching, Angioinvasion; Aspergillus: Septate, 45° Branching
Echinocandins in MucormycosisNO ACTIVITY — Mucorales Lack β-1,3-D-Glucan
DeferoxamineSiderophore for Mucorales → CONTRAINDICATED
Mucormycosis vs Aspergillosis on ImagingMucor: Rapid, Aggressive, Bone Erosion, Palatal Necrosis; Aspergillus: Slower, Cavitation, Halo Sign
ROCM vs Invasive Aspergillus SinusitisMucor: Rapid, Palatal Eschar, Cranial Nerves, Carotid Invasion; Aspergillus: Slower, Less Bone Destruction Initially
MnemonicMeaning
"Mucor = Broad, Aseptate, 90° Branching, Angioinvasion"Histology
"DKA + Deferoxamine = Mucor's Best Friends"Risk Factors
"Echinocandins = NO GO for Mucor"Contraindicated
"LAmB + Surgery + DKA Control = Survival Triad"Management
"Rhino-Orbital-Cerebral = Sinus → Orbit → Brain"ROCM Spread
"Deferoxamine = Siderophore = Mucor Fuel"Deferoxamine Danger

15. 🗺️ Mind Map

Mucormycosis
Aetiology
Mucorales: Rhizopus, Mucor, Rhizomucor
Angioinvasion → Necrosis
Risk Factors
DKA (50-70%)
Deferoxamine (Siderophore!)
Neutropenia, Steroids, Transplant
Burns/Trauma
Clinical Forms
ROCM (Most Common)
– Sinus → Orbit → Brain
– Black Eschar, Proptosis, CN Palsies
Pulmonary
– Reverse Halo Sign
Cutaneous
– Trauma/Burns, Rapid Necrosis
Disseminated
– >90% Mortality
Diagnosis
KOH: Broad, Aseptate, 90° Branching
Histology: Angioinvasion
Culture: Rapid, Fluffy
PCR/Sequencing
CT/MRI: Bone Erosion, Invasion
Management Triad
LAmB 5-10mg/kg IV
Radical Surgery (Urgent)
Metabolic Control (DKA, Stop Deferoxamine)
Contraindicated
Echinocandins (No Activity)
Deferoxamine (Siderophore)
Complications
Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis
Carotid Invasion
Cerebral Infarction
Dissemination

16. 📋 One-Page Revision Card

MUCORMYCOSIS – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
Aetiology: Mucorales (Rhizopus, Mucor) — Angioinvasion → Necrosis
Risk Factors: DKA (50-70%), Deferoxamine (Siderophore!), Neutropenia, Steroids, Transplant
Histology: Broad, Aseptate, Right-Angle (90°) Branching Hyphae + Angioinvasion
ROCM: Sinus → Orbit → Brain — Black Eschar, Proptosis, CN Palsies, Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis
Diagnosis: KOH Mount (Broad, Aseptate, 90° Branching) Rapid; Histology: Angioinvasion; Culture/PCR
Azole/Echinocandin: Echinocandins NO ACTIVITY; Azoles (Posaconazole/Isavuconazole) = Step-Down/Salvage
Treatment Triad: LAmB 5-10mg/kg IV + Urgent Radical Debridement + Metabolic Control (DKA, Stop Deferoxamine!)
Echinocandins: CONTRAINDICATED (No β-1,3-D-Glucan in Mucorales Wall)
Deferoxamine: SIDEROPHORE → CONTRADICATED (Enhances Growth)
Mortality: ROCM 50-85%, Disseminated >90%

ReviewDateScore (1-5)Next Review
Day 12025-06-172025-06-18
Day 3
Day 7
Day 15
Day 30

18. 🎯 Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

LevelContent
Must KnowMucormycosis = Mucorales, Angioinvasion, KOH: Broad/Aseptate/90°, LAmB 5-10mg/kg + Surgery + DKA Control, Deferoxamine Contraindicated, Echinocandins No Activity, ROCM Spread (Sinus→Orbit→Brain), Mortality, Deferoxamine = Siderophore Contraindicated
Should KnowROCM staging, Cavernous sinus thrombosis management, Carotid artery invasion, Reverse halo sign pulmonary, Cutaneous mucormycosis (burns/trauma), Posaconazole/isavuconazole step-down, Renal dosing LAmB, Paediatric dosing, Pregnancy management, Isavuconazole advantages, C. auris vs mucormycosis differentiation
Nice to KnowMucorales genomics, Spore germination triggers, Iron acquisition mechanisms, Host immune response (neutrophils, macrophages), Iron chelation therapy beyond deferoxamine, Posaconazole pharmacokinetics (food effect), Isavuconazole BALANCE trial, Mucormycosis in COVID-19 (CAMCO), Global epidemiology shifts, Climate change impact, Novel antifungals (ibrexafungerp, rezafungin, fosmanogepix), One Health approach

19. ✅ Self-Test Scorecard

SectionScore (0-10)Notes
Aetiology & Pathophysiology
Risk Factors & Clinical Forms
Histopathology & Diagnosis
Management Triad (LAmB, Surgery, Metabolic)
Antifungal Selection & Contraindications
Complications & Mortality
Viva Questions

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.37 Nocardia And Actinomyces Infections
[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Aerobic Actinomycetes (Nocardia) & Anaerobic Actinomycetes (Actinomyces)
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Pulmonary nocardiosis (immunocompromised), cervicofacial actinomycosis, brain abscess, sulphur granules, penicillin vs TMP-SMX

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Distinguish Nocardia (aerobic, acid-fast, immunocompromised) vs Actinomyces (anaerobic, endogenous, mucosal breach)
  • [ ] Recognise Pulmonary nocardiosis: Subacute pneumonia, cavitation, dissemination to brain/skin
  • [ ] Recognise Cervicofacial actinomycosis: "Lumpy jaw", sulphur granules, dental/oral origin
  • [ ] Diagnose: Modified Ziehl-Neelsen (Nocardia), sulphur granules (Actinomyces), culture (both), PCR
  • [ ] Manage: TMP-SMX (Nocardia), High-dose Penicillin G (Actinomyces)

2. 📚 Comparison: Nocardia vs Actinomyces

FeatureNocardiaActinomyces
ClassificationAerobic actinomycete (Gram+, branching, beaded)Anaerobic actinomycete (Gram+, branching, non-acid-fast)
Acid-fastPartial/Weak +ve (modified ZN)Negative
OxygenAerobicAnaerobic/Microaerophilic
HabitatSoil, decaying organic matterHuman oropharyngeal/GI/GU flora (endogenous)
TransmissionInhalation (pulmonary), inoculation (cutaneous)Mucosal breach (dental, surgery, trauma, IUD)
Risk GroupsImmunocompromised (transplant, HIV, steroids, MALT lymphoma)Immunocompetent (dental disease, trauma, IUD)
Key SpeciesN. asteroides complex, N. brasiliensis, N. cyriacigeorgicaA. israelii, A. gerencseriae, A. naeslundii

3. 🩺 Nocardiosis

Clinical Syndromes

SyndromeFeatures
Pulmonary (80%)Subacute: cough, fever, weight loss, night sweats; cavitation, consolidation, nodules; mimics TB/cancer
CNS (30-50% dissemination)Brain abscesses (multiple, supratentorial, rim-enhancing); meningitis rare
CutaneousCellulitis, abscesses, mycetoma (N. brasiliensis), sporotrichoid spread
DisseminatedSkin, brain, kidneys, bones, joints; high mortality

Radiology

  • CXR/CT: Irregular nodules, cavitation (thick-walled), consolidation, pleural effusion
  • Brain MRI: Multiple ring-enhancing lesions (supratentorial, grey-white junction)

4. 🩺 Actinomycosis

Clinical Syndromes (by Site)

SyndromeFeatures
Cervicofacial (50-60%)"Lumpy jaw"; indurated mass at angle of mandible, sinus tracts, sulphur granules; dental caries, extraction, trauma
Thoracic (15-20%)Pneumonia, mass, empyema, "rib destruction"; mimics lung cancer/TB; aspiration of oral flora
Abdomino-pelvic (20%)IUD-associated (pelvic actinomycosis), post-surgical, appendicitis mimic; mass, sinus tracts, sulphur granules
CNSBrain abscess (single), meningitis; haematogenous or direct extension

Sulphur Granules

  • Pathognomonic: Yellow-white, 1-2 mm, friable; colonies of filamentous bacteria + calcium phosphate
  • Microscopy: Gram+ filamentous bacilli, clubs at periphery (rosettes)

5. 🔬 Diagnosis

TestNocardiaActinomyces
Gram stainGram+, branching, beaded filamentsGram+, branching filaments
Acid-fast (Modified ZN)Weakly +ve (beaded)Negative
CultureAerobic; 24-72h on BAP/chocolate; BCYE (Legionella media)Anaerobic; 5-14 days; sulphur granules crush for culture
Sulphur granulesNot typicalDiagnostic (crush + Gram stain/culture)
PCR / 16S rRNASpecies ID; rapidSpecies ID; rapid
HistopathologyGranulomatous, microabscessesSulphur granules, "sulfur granules" in abscesses
[!tip] Exam Pearl: Nocardia = modified ZN +ve, aerobic, immunocompromised; Actinomyces = anaerobic, sulphur granules, endogenous, dental/IUD.

6. 💊 Management

Nocardiosis

ScenarioRegimenDuration
Pulmonary / Disseminated / CNSTMP-SMX 15-20 mg/kg/day (TMP component) IV/PO divided q6-8h6-12 months (CNS: 12 months; immunocompromised: lifelong suppression)
Alternative (sulfa allergy)Imipenem OR Meropenem + Amikacin OR Linezolid + CefotaximeSame
Cutaneous / MycetomaTMP-SMX + Amikacin OR Linezolid6-12 months
Suppressive therapy (post-treatment, immunocompromised)TMP-SMX DS 1 tab dailyIndefinite

Actinomycosis

ScenarioRegimenDuration
All forms (IV induction)Penicillin G 18-24 MU IV daily (divided q4h)2-6 weeks
All forms (Oral consolidation)Penicillin V 2-4 g PO q6h OR Amoxicillin 1g q8h6-12 months (total)
Penicillin allergyDoxycycline 100mg bd OR TMP-SMX DS bd OR Clindamycin 600mg q8h6-12 months
AdjunctSurgical drainage/debridement (abscesses, necrotic tissue, IUD removal)As needed
[!warning] Duration: Actinomycosis requires prolonged therapy (6-12 months) to prevent relapse. Short courses fail.

7. 📋 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
NocardiaAerobic, modified ZN +ve, soil, immunocompromised, pulmonary → brain, TMP-SMX
ActinomycesAnaerobic, endogenous oral flora, dental/IUD, sulphur granules, cervicofacial, Penicillin G
Brain abscessNocardia: multiple, supratentorial; Actinomyces: single
Sulphur granulesPathognomonic for Actinomyces; crush for Gram/culture
HIV/TransplantNocardia = opportunistic; Actinomyces = not typically opportunistic

8. ❓ Viva Questions (FCPS/MRCP)

  1. How do you differentiate Nocardia from Actinomyces on microscopy?
  2. A renal transplant patient presents with cavitary pneumonia and multiple brain lesions. Diagnosis?
  3. What are sulphur granules? Which organism produces them?
  4. Compare treatment of nocardiosis vs actinomycosis.
  5. Why does actinomycosis require 6-12 months of antibiotics?
  6. A woman with IUD presents with pelvic mass and draining sinuses. Diagnosis?
  7. What is the modified Ziehl-Neelsen stain used for?
  8. Nocardia brain abscess vs Toxoplasma in HIV: key imaging difference?
  9. Alternative treatment for Nocardia if sulfa allergy?
  10. Actinomycosis vs TB: distinguishing features?

9. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Nocardia vs Actinomyces (both branching Gram+)Nocardia: aerobic, modified ZN +ve, soil, immunocompromised; Actinomyces: anaerobic, ZN -ve, endogenous, sulphur granules
Nocardia vs Mycobacterium (both acid-fast)Nocardia: weak/partial ZN +ve, aerobic, branching filaments fragment; Mycobacterium: strong ZN +ve, non-branching
Actinomycosis vs BotryomycosisActinomycosis: Anaerobic, sulphur granules, "lumpy jaw"; Botryomycosis: Staph aureus, "grains", no sulphur granules
Cervicofacial actinomycosis vs TB lymphadenitisActinomycosis: Dental origin, sulphur granules, responds to penicillin; TB: Caseating nodes, AFB+, needs ATT

Mnemonic - Nocardia: "NOCARD" → Nocardia, Opportunistic (immunocompromised), Cavitary pneumonia, Acid-fast (weak), Resistant to routine abx, Disseminates to brain, TMP-SMX treats

Mnemonic - Actinomyces: "ACTINO" → Anaerobic, Cervicofacial (lumpy jaw), Thoracic (aspiration), IUD (pelvic), No acid-fast, Own flora (endogenous), Sulphur granules, Penicillin G

10. 🗺️ Mind Map

Actinomycetes
Nocardia (Aerobic)
Modified ZN +
Soil / Inhalation
Immunocompromised
Pulmonary → Brain (multiple)
Skin / Mycetoma
TMP-SMX 6-12 mo
Actinomyces (Anaerobic)
Endogenous flora
Mucosal breach
Cervicofacial (lumpy jaw)
Thoracic / Abdomino-pelvic
Sulphur granules
Penicillin G 6-12 mo

11. 📄 One-Page Revision Card

NocardiaActinomyces
AerobicAnaerobic
Modified ZN +ve (weak)ZN -ve
Soil (inhalation)Endogenous oral/GI/GU flora
ImmunocompromisedImmunocompetent (dental, IUD, trauma)
Pulmonary → brain (multiple abscesses)Cervicofacial (lumpy jaw), thoracic, pelvic
TMP-SMX (15-20mg/kg/d) × 6-12moPenicillin G 18-24MU IV → Pen V × 6-12mo
Alternatives: Carbapenem, Linezolid, AmikacinAlternatives: Doxy, TMP-SMX, Clinda

12. 📊 Spaced Repetition Tracker

Review IntervalDateScore (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

13. 🧪 Self-Test Scorecard

TopicKnown? (✓/✗)Last Reviewed
Nocardia vs Actinomyces (microscopy, oxygen, host)

Actinomycosis clinical forms
Sulphur granules (what, which organism, diagnosis)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.38 Q Fever

Related: [[Fever in Returned Traveller and FUO]], [[Infective Endocarditis]], [[Viral Haemorrhagic Fevers]], [[Travel Medicine and Pre-Travel Advice]], [[Pneumonia (Atypical)]]

[!important]
Q Fever = Coxiella burnetii (obligate intracellular bacterium). Zoonosis: cattle, sheep, goats → aerosol inhalation (parturition products); highly infectious (1 organism). Acute: flu-like, pneumonia, hepatitis. Chronic: endocarditis (high mortality), vascular infection. Phase I/II antibodies = diagnosis; Phase I IgG ≥1:800 = chronic. Acute: doxycycline 2w; Chronic endocarditis: doxycycline + hydroxychloroquine 18m.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise acute and chronic Q fever presentations
  • Apply serological diagnosis (Phase I/II antibodies)
  • Differentiate acute vs chronic Q fever
  • Select appropriate antibiotic therapy (acute vs chronic)
  • Manage Q fever endocarditis and vascular infections
  • Apply prevention (vaccine, occupational hygiene)

2. Epidemiology & Transmission

AspectDetails
AgentCoxiella burnetii (obligate intracellular, spore-like small cell variant)
ReservoirsCattle, sheep, goats (primary); cats, dogs, wildlife; placenta/amniotic fluid = high concentration
TransmissionAerosol inhalation (parturition products, dust, wool, straw); direct contact with infected tissues; ingestion (unpasteurised milk); tick bite (rare); person-to-person: extremely rare
InfectivityExtremely high — 1 organism can cause infection
Risk OccupationsFarmers, veterinarians, abattoir workers, shearers, dairy workers, lab personnel
GeographyWorldwide (except New Zealand); outbreaks near livestock

3. Clinical Manifestations

Acute Q Fever (60% symptomatic)

FeatureDetails
Incubation2–3 weeks (range 1–6 weeks)
PresentationFlu-like: fever (39–40°C), severe headache, myalgia, fatigue, chills, sweats
RespiratoryAtypical pneumonia (dry cough, minimal sputum, patchy CXR), pleuritic chest pain
HepaticGranulomatous hepatitis (RUQ pain, hepatomegaly, ↑ ALT/AST, alkaline phosphatase)
OtherMyocarditis, pericarditis, meningitis, encephalitis, rash (maculopapular)

Chronic Q Fever (≥6 months)

SyndromeFeatures
EndocarditisMost common (60–70%); culture-negative; native/ prosthetic valves; vascular grafts; high mortality (25–60%)
Vascular InfectionAortic aneurysm, vascular graft infection
OsteoarticularOsteomyelitis, spondylodiscitis, prosthetic joint infection
Chronic HepatitisGranulomatous; fibrosis/cirrhosis
Chronic Fatigue SyndromePost-Q fever fatigue syndrome (QFS) — persistent fatigue >6m

Q Fever in Pregnancy

RiskOutcome
Acute Q Fever Miscarriage, stillbirth, preterm delivery, intrauterine growth restriction
Chronic Q FeverRare in pregnancy
ManagementDoxycycline CONTRAINDICATED; Cotrimoxazole (TMP-SMX) 160/800mg 12h throughout pregnancy + 6w postpartum; folic acid; monitor fetal growth

4. Diagnosis

Serology (Gold Standard) — Phase I / Phase II Antibodies

PhaseAntigenAcute Q FeverChronic Q Fever (Endocarditis)
Phase IILipopolysaccharide (LPS) truncatedIgM appears first (1–2w), peaks 4–8w; IgG rises laterLow/absent
Phase IFull LPS (virulent)Low/absentIgG Phase I ≥1:800 (or ≥1:1600) = DIAGNOSTIC; IgG Phase I > Phase II
Antibody PatternInterpretation
Phase II IgM + Phase II IgG (Phase I negative/low)Acute Q Fever
Phase I IgG ≥1:800 (or ≥1:1600) + Phase I IgG > Phase II IgGChronic Q Fever (Endocarditis)
Phase I IgG + Phase II IgG high (no IgM)Past infection / convalescent
[!critical]
Phase I IgG ≥1:800 (or ≥1:1600) = CHRONIC Q FEVER (endocarditis). Phase II IgM + IgG = Acute Q Fever. IgM appears first (1–2w); Phase I IgG rises late in chronic.

Other Diagnostics

TestUtility
PCR (blood, tissue, valve tissue)Early acute (before seroconversion); valve tissue in endocarditis
CultureBSL-3; not routine; slow (weeks); definitive
ImmunohistochemistryValve tissue, placenta, liver biopsy
CXRPatchy opacities, pleural effusion (atypical pneumonia)
EchocardiographyTOE > TTE for vegetations, abscess, prosthetic valve dehiscence

5. Treatment

Acute Q Fever

RegimenDoseDurationNotes
Doxycycline100mg PO 12h14 days1st-line; start ASAP (↓ chronic risk)
Alternative (pregnancy/contraindication)Cotrimoxazole 960mg 12h14 days1st trimester safe; 2nd/3rd controversial
Severe/ComplicatedDoxycycline 100mg IV/PO 12h + Hydroxychloroquine 200mg 8h14 daysAdd hydroxychloroquine (raises phagosomal pH)
[!tip]
Early doxycycline (within 3 days of fever) ↓ risk of chronic Q fever and complications.

Chronic Q Fever (Endocarditis)

RegimenDoseDurationNotes
Doxycycline + HydroxychloroquineDoxycycline 100mg PO 12h + Hydroxychloroquine 200mg 8h18 months minimum (up to 24–36m)1st-line; Hydroxychloroquine raises phagosomal pH → doxycycline bactericidal
Alternative (doxycycline intolerance)Cotrimoxazole 960mg 12h + Rifampicin 300mg 12h18–24 monthsLess effective
SurgeryValve replacementIf haemodynamic compromise, persistent sepsis, embolismOften required
[!critical]
Doxycycline + Hydroxychloroquine ×18m = standard for chronic Q fever endocarditis. Hydroxychloroquine 200mg 8h raises phagosomal pH → doxycycline bactericidal. Monitor: ECGs (QTc), eye exams (retinopathy), LFTs, CBC.

Chronic Q Fever (Vascular Infection)

RegimenDuration
Doxycycline + Hydroxychloroquine24 months (minimum)

Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (Post-Q Fever)

ManagementDetails
No specific antiviralGraded exercise therapy, CBT, pacing; symptomatic management

6. Diagnosis Algorithm

Suspected Q Fever
    ↓
Acute presentation (fever, pneumonia, hepatitis)
    ↓
Serology: Phase II IgM + IgG (1-2w) → Acute Q Fever
    ↓
Treat: Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×14d
    ↓
Chronic suspicion (endocarditis, vascular, >6m fever)
    ↓
Serology: Phase I IgG ≥1:800 + Phase I > Phase II → Chronic Q Fever
    ↓
Echo (TOE) + PCR blood/valve
    ↓
Treat: Doxycycline + Hydroxychloroquine ×18-24m

7. Prevention & Vaccination

MeasureDetails
Vaccine (Q-Vax®)Whole-cell formalin-inactivated; single dose; pre-exposure; licensed in Australia; highly effective
Pre-vaccination ScreeningSkin test + serology (exclude prior exposure — severe reaction if sensitised)
OccupationalPPE (N95/FFP3, gloves, goggles); vaccination of abattoir/farm/veterinary workers
AnimalVaccination of livestock (Coxevac®); proper disposal of birth products; pasteurisation of milk
EnvironmentalDust control, ventilation, proper manure management

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Q Fever = Coxiella burnetii; highly infectious (1 organism); aerosol from parturition products
  • Acute: flu-like, atypical pneumonia, granulomatous hepatitis; Phase II IgM/IgG
  • Chronic: endocarditis (most common); Phase I IgG ≥1:800; doxycycline + hydroxychloroquine ×18m
  • Acute treatment: doxycycline 100mg 12h ×14d (early = ↓ chronic risk)
  • Chronic endocarditis: doxycycline + hydroxychloroquine ×18–24m (hydroxychloroquine raises pH → doxy bactericidal)
  • Phase I IgG ≥1:800 = chronic Q fever (endocarditis); Phase II IgM/IgG = acute
  • Pregnancy: doxycycline contraindicated; TMP-SMX throughout pregnancy
  • Vaccine: Q-Vax (Australia); pre-screening (skin test + serology) mandatory
  • Infective endocarditis culture-negative: think Q fever (Phase I IgG), Bartonella, Brucella, HACEK

9. Common Viva Questions

  1. Which serological finding defines chronic Q fever endocarditis? Phase I IgG ≥1:800 (or ≥1:1600) with Phase I > Phase II.
  2. Acute vs Chronic Q Fever serology? Acute = Phase II IgM + IgG; Chronic = Phase I IgG ≥1:800 (Phase I > Phase II).
  3. Treatment of acute Q fever? Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×14d.
  4. Treatment of chronic Q fever endocarditis? Doxycycline 100mg 12h + Hydroxychloroquine 200mg 8h ×18–24 months.
  5. Why hydroxychloroquine in chronic Q fever? Raises phagosomal pH → doxycycline bactericidal.
  6. Q Fever in pregnancy? Doxycycline contraindicated; TMP-SMX throughout pregnancy + 6w postpartum.
  7. Transmission? Aerosol inhalation of parturition products (placenta, amniotic fluid); highly infectious (1 organism).
  8. Diagnosis of acute Q fever? Phase II IgM + IgG seroconversion.
  9. Culture-negative endocarditis — think? Q fever, Bartonella, Brucella, HACEK, T. whipplei.

10. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Phase I = acutePhase II = acute (IgM/IgG); Phase I = chronic (IgG ≥1:800)
Chronic Q Fever = just longer acuteDifferent serology (Phase I IgG ≥1:800); different treatment (18m vs 14d); different prognosis
Doxycycline alone for chronic endocarditisHydroxychloroquine ESSENTIAL (raises phagosomal pH); doxy alone = failure
Q fever endocarditis = same as other culture-negativePhase I IgG ≥1:800 specific; chronic Q fever = 60-70% of chronic cases
Vaccine available everywhereQ-Vax only licensed in Australia; pre-screening (skin test + serology) mandatory
Q fever only from direct contactAerosol inhalation primary; highly infectious (1 organism); wind-borne spread kilometres
Chronic Q fever = just fatigueEndocarditis = 60-70%; vascular, osteoarticular, hepatitis also chronic

11. Mnemonics

  • Q FEVER PHASES: Phase I = Chronic (IgG ≥1:800); Phase II = Acute (IgM + IgG)
  • Q FEVER ACUTE: Doxycycline 14 days (early = ↓ chronic)
  • Q FEVER CHRONIC: Doxy + Hydroxychloroquine 18 months; Hydroxychloroquine = pH ↑ → doxy bactericidal
  • Q FEVER ENDOCARDITIS: Phase I IgG ≥1:800 = Chronic; Phase II = Acute
  • PREGNANCY: Doxy = NO; TMP-SMX throughout + 6w post
  • VACCINE: Q-Vax (Australia); Skin test + Serology BEFORE

12. Mind Map

Q Fever
Agent
Coxiella burnetii
Obligate intracellular
Spore-like SCV
Transmission
Aerosol (parturition)
*...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)*
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.39 Relapsing Fever
[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Spirochaetes → Relapsing Fever
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Recurrent fever, Borrelia species, louse-borne vs tick-borne, Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Distinguish Louse-borne (B. recurrentis) vs Tick-borne (B. duttonii, B. hermsii) relapsing fever
  • [ ] Recognise clinical pattern: Recurrent febrile episodes (3-5 days) separated by afebrile periods
  • [ ] Diagnose: Peripheral blood smear (Giemsa/Wright stain) showing spirochaetes during febrile episode
  • [ ] Manage: Doxycycline or Tetracycline (single dose for louse-borne); avoid in pregnancy
  • [ ] Recognise and manage Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (anticipate with first dose)

2. 📚 Epidemiology & Transmission

FeatureLouse-borne (Epidemic)Tick-borne (Endemic)
AgentBorrelia recurrentisB. duttonii (Africa), B. hermsii (USA), B. miyamotoi
VectorHuman body louse (Pediculus humanus)Soft ticks (Ornithodoros spp.)
ReservoirHumans onlyRodents, small mammals
GeographyHorn of Africa, refugee campsAfrica, Americas, Asia, Europe (focal)
TransmissionLouse crushed → hemolymph enters skin/mucosaTick bite (often painless, nocturnal)
Risk factorsOvercrowding, poor hygiene, displacementCave exposure, rodent contact, travel

3. 🦠 Pathophysiology

  • Antigenic variation: Borrelia changes surface protein (Vmp - variable major protein) → immune evasion → recurrent bacteraemia
  • Cycles: Each febrile episode corresponds to a new antigenic variant
  • Louse-borne: More severe, higher mortality (10-40% untreated); single episode typical
  • Tick-borne: Milder, multiple relapses (3-10 episodes); lower mortality

4. 🩺 Clinical Features

Typical Episode

PhaseFeatures
Incubation4-18 days (average 7)
Febrile phase (3-5 days)High fever (39-41°C), chills, headache, myalgia, arthralgia, nausea, vomiting
CrisisRigors → profuse sweating → temperature drop (risk of hypotension, shock)
Afebrile period4-7 days (louse-borne: longer, often single episode; tick-borne: shorter, multiple relapses)

Complications

  • CNS involvement: Meningitis, cranial nerve palsies, coma (more common in louse-borne)
  • Hepatitis, myocarditis, ARDS, DIC
  • Pregnancy: High risk of abortion, preterm labour, neonatal infection
  • Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction: Within 2-4 hours of first antibiotic dose (fever, rigors, hypotension, worsening)

5. 🔬 Diagnosis

TestFindingsNotes
Peripheral blood smear (Giemsa/Wright)Spirochaetes visible during febrile episode (10-100/field)Gold standard; sensitivity 70-90% in febrile phase
Dark-field microscopyMotile spirochaetesHigher sensitivity, needs fresh sample
PCR (blood, CSF)Borrelia DNAHighly sensitive/specific; not widely available
Serology (IFA, ELISA)Rising titresRetrospective; cross-reacts with Lyme
QBC (Quantitative Buffy Coat)Concentrated spirochaetesHigher yield than thin smear
[!tip] Exam Pearl: Best diagnostic yield = thick/thin blood smear during febrile spike. If negative, repeat in 24h.

6. 💊 Management

First-line

ScenarioRegimen
Louse-borne (adults/children >8yr)Doxycycline 200 mg PO single dose OR Tetracycline 500 mg PO q6h × 2 doses
Tick-borneDoxycycline 100 mg PO bd × 7-10 days
PregnancyErythromycin 500 mg PO q6h × 7 days (avoid tetracyclines)
CNS involvementCeftriaxone 2 g IV q12h × 10-14 days + consider dexamethasone

Jarisch-Herxheimer Reaction Management

  • Anticipate: Pre-hydrate, monitor vitals
  • Treat: Fluid resuscitation, vasopressors if hypotensive, antipyretics
  • Steroids: Consider dexamethasone 10 mg IV before first dose in high-risk (CNS disease, pregnancy)

7. 📋 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
VectorLouse-borne = Pediculus humanus; Tick-borne = Ornithodoros (soft tick)
DiagnosisBlood smear (Giemsa) during fever = gold standard
TreatmentDoxycycline single dose (louse-borne); 7-10 days (tick-borne)
JHROccurs 2-4h post-abx; manage with fluids, steroids if severe
PregnancyErythromycin (avoid tetracyclines)
DifferentialMalaria, typhoid, borreliosis, leptospirosis, rat-bite fever

8. ❓ Viva Questions (FCPS/MRCP)

  1. How do you distinguish louse-borne from tick-borne relapsing fever clinically?
  2. What is the diagnostic test of choice and when should it be performed?
  3. Describe Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction. How do you prevent/manage it?
  4. Why is doxycycline contraindicated in pregnancy? What is the alternative?
  5. What are the CNS complications of relapsing fever?
  6. How does antigenic variation occur in Borrelia?
  7. A refugee from Sudan presents with recurrent fever. Blood smear negative once. Next step?
  8. Compare treatment of louse-borne vs tick-borne relapsing fever.
  9. What is the vector for tick-borne relapsing fever? How does transmission occur?
  10. Which Borrelia species causes Lyme disease vs relapsing fever?

9. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Relapsing fever vs MalariaRelapsing fever: spirochaetes on smear, JHR with abx, lice/ticks vectors; Malaria: parasites on smear, no JHR, mosquito vector
Relapsing fever vs TyphoidTyphoid: step-ladder fever, relative bradycardia, rose spots, blood culture +ve; Relapsing fever: recurrent spikes, JHR, smear +ve
Louse-borne vs Tick-borneLouse: epidemic, humans only reservoir, severe, single episode; Tick: endemic, rodent reservoir, milder, multiple relapses

Mnemonic: "RELAPSE" → Recurrent fever, Epidemic (louse) / Endemic (tick), Louse/Ornithodoros vector, Antigenic variation, Peripheral smear (Giemsa), Single dose doxy (louse), Erythromycin in pregnancy

10. 🗺️ Mind Map

Relapsing Fever
Etiology
Borrelia recurrentis (louse)
B. duttonii/hermsii (tick)
Vector
Louse (Pediculus humanus)
Soft tick (Ornithodoros)
Clinical
Recurrent fever (3-5 days)
Crisis (rigors → defervescence)
Afebrile intervals
JHR with treatment
Diagnosis
Blood smear (Giemsa) - GOLD
Dark-field microscopy
PCR
Treatment
Louse: Doxy 200mg single dose
Tick: Doxy 100mg bd × 7-10d
Pregnancy: Erythromycin
CNS: Ceftriaxone
Complications
CNS (meningitis, coma)
Hepatitis, myocarditis
DIC, ARDS
Pregnancy loss

11. 📄 One-Page Revision Card

Relapsing FeverKey Facts
OrganismsB. recurrentis (louse), B. duttonii/hermsii (tick)
VectorsBody louse / Soft tick (Ornithodoros)
ClinicalRecurrent fever 3-5d, crisis with rigors/sweats, afebrile 4-7d
DiagnosisBlood smear (Giemsa) during fever = Gold standard
TreatmentLouse: Doxycycline 200mg single dose; Tick: Doxy 100mg bd × 7-10d
PregnancyErythromycin 500mg q6h × 7d (no tetracyclines)
JHR2-4h post-abx: fever, hypotension → fluids, steroids
ComplicationsMeningitis, hepatitis, myocarditis, DIC, pregnancy loss

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.40 Rickettsial Infections
[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Rickettsiae, Orientia, Anaplasma, Ehrlichia
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Spotted Fever Group (RMSF, MSF), Typhus Group (Epidemic, Murine), Scrub Typhus (Orientia), doxycycline, IFA/PCR, eschar

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Classify Rickettsial Diseases: Spotted Fever Group (RMSF, Mediterranean Spotted Fever, African Tick Typhus) vs Typhus Group (Epidemic, Murine) vs Scrub Typhus (Orientia tsutsugamushi)
  • [ ] Recognise Clinical Presentation: Fever, Headache, Rash, Eschar (Tache Noire) — Pathognomonic for Spotted Fever/Scrub Typhus
  • [ ] Diagnose: IFA (Gold Standard), PCR (Early), Weil-Felix (Historical/Low Sensitivity), PCR/Immunohistochemistry (Tissue)
  • [ ] Manage: Doxycycline 100mg BD PO (1st Line All Rickettsial), Chloramphenicol (Pregnancy/Children), Ceftriaxone (CNS)
  • [ ] Recognise Complications: Vasculitis, Encephalitis, Renal Failure, ARDS, Multi-Organ Failure
  • [ ] Distinguish Spotted Fever (Eschar + Maculopapular Rash) vs Typhus (No Eschar, Central Rash) vs Scrub Typhus (Eschar + Lymphadenopathy)

2. 📖 Classification & Key Species

GroupRepresentative SpeciesDiseaseVectorGeography
Spotted Fever Group (SFG)R. rickettsiiRocky Mountain Spotted Fever (RMSF)Dermacentor TickAmericas (USA, Mexico, Brazil)
R. conoriiMediterranean Spotted Fever (MSF)Rhipicephalus TickMediterranean, Africa, India
R. africaeAfrican Tick Bite Fever (ATBF)Amblyomma TickSub-Saharan Africa
R. japonicaJapanese Spotted FeverHaemaphysalis TickJapan
R. honeiFlinders Island Spotted FeverBothriocroton TickAustralia
Typhus GroupR. prowazekiiEpidemic Typhus (Louse-Borne)Pediculus humanus (Body Louse)Crowded/Displaced Populations, Cold Climate
R. typhiMurine (Endemic) Typhus (Flea-Borne)Xenopsylla Flea (Rat)Worldwide (Tropical/Subtropical)
Scrub Typhus GroupOrientia tsutsugamushiScrub Typhus (Tsutsugamushi Disease)Leptotrombidium Mite (Chigger)Asia-Pacific (Tsutsugamushi Triangle)
OtherAnaplasma phagocytophilumHuman Granulocytic Anaplasmosis (HGA)Ixodes TickUSA, Europe, Asia
Ehrlichia chaffeensisHuman Monocytic Ehrlichiosis (HME)Amblyomma TickUSA (SE/South-Central)
Ehrlichia ewingiiE. ewingii InfectionAmblyomma TickUSA (SE)
[!tip] Key Differentiator: Eschar (Tache Noire) = Present in Spotted Fever Group & Scrub Typhus; Absent in Typhus Group (Epidemic/Murine).

3. 📖 Clinical Syndromes

Spotted Fever Group (SFG)

DiseaseVectorGeographyKey Features
Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever (RMSF)Dermacentor Tick (Dog Tick, Wood Tick)USA (NC, OK, AR, TN, MO, AR), Mexico, Brazil, CanadaHigh Fever, Headache, Myalgia, Centrifugal Rash (Wrists/Ankles → Centripetal), Palmoplantar Involvement, NO Eschar (Usually), High Mortality if Untreated
Mediterranean Spotted Fever (MSF)Rhipicephalus sanguineus (Brown Dog Tick)Mediterranean, Africa, India, Black SeaEschar (Tache Noire) @ Bite Site, Maculopapular Rash, Fever, Headache, Benign Course (Usually)
African Tick Bite Fever (ATBF)Amblyocroton TickSub-Saharan Africa, CaribbeanMultiple Eschars, Regional Lymphadenopathy, Mild Fever, Self-Limited

Typhus Group

DiseaseVectorGeographyKey Features
Epidemic TyphusHuman Body Louse (Pediculus humanus corporis)Crowded/Displaced Populations, Cold Climate, War, FamineHigh Fever, Headache, Rash (Centripetal → Trunk → Extremities), NO Eschar, Neurological (Confusion, Delirium), High Mortality if Untreated
Murine (Endemic) TyphusRat Flea (Xenopsylla cheopis)Worldwide (Tropical/Subtropical — Port Cities)Fever, Headache, Rash (Trunk → Extremities), NO Eschar, Milder than Epidemic

Scrub Typhus

FeatureDetails
AgentOrientia tsutsugamushi (Mite/Chigger — Leptotrombidium)
GeographyTsutsugamushi Triangle (Asia-Pacific: Japan, China, Korea, SE Asia, India, Pakistan, N. Australia)
VectorChigger (Larval Mite) — Bite in Sweaty Areas (Axilla, Groin, Belt Line)
Key FeatureEschar at Bite Site (Painless, 5-10mm, Necrotic Centre, Black Crust) + Regional Lymphadenopathy
ClinicalFever, Headache, Maculopapular Rash (Centrifugal), Lymphadenopathy (Generalised), Hepatosplenomegaly, Interstitial Pneumonitis, Meningoencephalitis

4. 🔬 Diagnostic Workup

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Fever + Rash + Exposure History
↓
**Eschar Present?**
↓
**Spotted Fever Group / Scrub Typhus**
**Typhus Group / Other**
↓
**Geography + Vector**
**Typhus Group**
↓
**Scrub Typhus** (Eschar + Lymphadenopathy)
**MSF / ATBF**
**RMSF**
**Epidemic Typhus**
**Murine Typhus**

Key Investigations

TestRoleTimingSensitivity/Specificity
IFA (Immunofluorescence Assay)Gold Standard (IgG/IgM)Convalescent (Day 10-14); Paired Sera 2-4w ApartHigh Sens/Spec
PCREarly Diagnosis (Blood/ESCHAR Swab)Acute Phase (Day 1-10)High Sens/Spec (ESCHAR > Blood)
Weil-Felix TestHistorical (OX19, OX2, OXK)Week 2Low Sens/Spec (OBsolete)
ImmunohistochemistryTissue Biopsy (Eschar/Skin)Any TimeHigh Specificity
CultureReference OnlyWeeksLow Sensitivity, Biosafety Level 3

Weil-Felix Test (Historical/Limited Use)

AntigenReactivityDisease
OX19PositiveEpidemic/Murine Typhus, Spotted Fever (RMSF, MSF)
OX2PositiveSpotted Fever (RMSF, MSF)
OXKPositiveScrub Typhus
[!warning] Weil-Felix = Obsolete, Low Sensitivity/Specificity. Use IFA/PCR. Kept for Resource-Limited Settings.

5. 💊 Management

Antibiotic Therapy

Disease / Severity1st LineAlternative (Pregnancy/Children)Duration
All Rickettsial (Spotted Fever, Typhus, Scrub Typhus)Doxycycline 100mg PO BDChloramphenicol 500mg QID (Pregnancy/Children <8y)7-14 Days (Until Afebrile 48-72h)
Severe/RMSF/Scrub Typhus with ComplicationsDoxycycline 100mg IV/PO BD + Chloramphenicol (If CNS)Chloramphenicol 500mg IV QID7-14 Days
Epidemic Typhus (Severe)Doxycycline 200mg IV/PO Day 1, then 100mg BDChloramphenicol 500mg IV QID10-14 Days
Anaplasmosis / EhrlichiosisDoxycycline 100mg BD PO/IVRifampin 600mg BD (Pregnancy)7-14 Days
[!warning] Doxycycline = 1st Line for ALL Rickettsial Diseases. Chloramphenicol = Pregnancy/Children <8y (Bone Marrow Toxicity Risk). Fluoroquinolones NOT Reliable.

6. 🩺 Clinical Syndromes & Complications

SyndromeKey FeaturesComplications
RMSFFever, Headache, Myalgia, Centripetal Rash (Wrists/Ankles → Trunk → Palms/Soles), No Eschar (Usually)Vasculitis → Encephalitis, Renal Failure, ARDS, DIC, Myocarditis
MSFEschar (Tache Noire), Maculopapular Rash, Fever, HeadacheUsually Self-Limited, Rare Severe
ATBFMultiple Eschars, Regional Lymphadenopathy, MildGenerally Benign
Epidemic TyphusHigh Fever, Headache, Central Rash (Trunk→Extremities), NO ESCHAR, NeurologicalGangrene, Myocarditis, Renal Failure, Encephalitis
Murine TyphusFever, Headache, Rash (Trunk→Extremities), NO ESCHARMilder, Rare Complications
Scrub TyphusEschar + Lymphadenopathy, Fever, Rash, Cough, HepatosplenomegalyInterstitial Pneumonitis, Meningoencephalitis, ARDS, Myocarditis, ARF

7. 🔬 Laboratory Findings

TestTypical Findings
CBCThrombocytopenia (Common), Leukopenia/Leukocytosis, Anaemia
LFT↑ AST/ALT (Mild-Moderate), ↑ Bilirubin (Mild)
Renal↑ Creatinine (Renal Impairment), Proteinuria
Coagulation↑ PT/APTT, ↓ Fibrinogen, ↑ D-dimer (DIC in Severe)
CSFLymphocytic Pleocytosis, ↑ Protein, Normal Glucose (Meningitis)
HyponatraemiaSIADH (Common in Scrub Typhus, RMSF)

8. 💊 Treatment Summary

ScenarioRegimen
All Rickettsial (Outpatient/Mild)Doxycycline 100mg PO BD × 7-14 Days (Until Afebrile 48h + Clinical Improvement)
Severe / HospitalisedDoxycycline 100mg IV/PO BD + Supportive Care; Chloramphenicol if Pregnant/Child
CNS InvolvementDoxycycline IV/PO (Good CNS Penetration) + Ceftriaxone (If Meningitis Suspected)
Pregnancy / Children <8yChloramphenicol 500mg QID PO/IV (Alternative: Azithromycin — Limited Data)
[!warning] Doxycycline = 1st Line for ALL. Chloramphenicol = Pregnancy/Children <8y (Grey Baby Syndrome Risk). Fluoroquinolones NOT Recommended (Variable Efficacy).

9. 🔄 Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDifferentiating Features
RMSF vs MeningococcemiaRMSF: Rash Peripheral → Central, No Meningismus Initially; Meningococcemia: Petechial/Purpuric, Rapid, Meningismus
RMSF vs DengueRMSF: Rash Peripheral→Central, No Thrombocytopenia Initially; Dengue: Thrombocytopenia, Plasma Leakage, NS1+
Scrub Typhus vs TyphoidScrub: Eschar + Lymphadenopathy + Doxycycline; Typhoid: Relative Bradycardia, Blood Culture+, Ceftriaxone
Epidemic Typhus vs Murine TyphusEpidemic = Louse, Crowded, Severe, Neurological; Murine = Flea, Rat, Milder
Scrub Typhus vs LeptospirosisScrub: Eschar + Lymphadenopathy; Lepto: Conjunctival Suffusion, Calf Myalgia, Renal Failure, Pulm Haemorrhage
Rickettsial vs LeptospirosisRickettsial: Eschar (SFG/Scrub), No Conjunctival Suffusion; Lepto: Conjunctival Suffusion, Calf Myalgia, Pulm Haemorrhage

10. 💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Unifying TreatmentDoxycycline 100mg BD PO/IV = 1st Line for ALL Rickettsial Diseases
ChloramphenicolPregnancy / Children <8y (Bone Marrow Toxicity)
Eschar (Tache Noire)Present in Spotted Fever Group + Scrub Typhus; Absent in Typhus Group
RMSFNo Eschar, Centrifugal Rash (Wrists/Ankles→Trunk), Palmoplantar Involvement, High Mortality if Delayed
Mediterranean Spotted FeverEschar + Maculopapular Rash + Fever, R. conorii, Mediterranean
Scrub TyphusEschar + Lymphadenopathy + Pneumonitis, Orientia tsutsugamushi, Asia-Pacific
Epidemic TyphusLouse-Borne, Crowded/Displaced, Central Rash, NO ESCHAR
Murine TyphusFlea-Borne, Rat, No Eschar, Milder
Doxycycline100mg BD PO/IV — 1st Line ALL Rickettsial
ChloramphenicolPregnancy / Children <8y — Alternative

11. ❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the unifying treatment for all Rickettsial infections?
  • Doxycycline 100mg BD PO/IV — 1st Line for ALL Rickettsial diseases.
  1. How do you distinguish Spotted Fever Group from Typhus Group clinically?
  • Spotted Fever Group = Eschar (Tache Noire) Present; Typhus Group = No Eschar.
  1. What is the classic triad of Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever?
  • Fever, Headache, Rash (Centrifugal: Wrists/Ankles → Trunk → Palms/Soles); NO ESCHAR typically.
  1. What is the characteristic feature of Scrub Typhus?
  • Eschar (Tache Noire) at Bite Site + Generalised Lymphadenopathy + Interstitial Pneumonitis.
  1. How do you differentiate Mediterranean Spotted Fever from RMSF?
  • MSF: Eschar Present (Tache Noire), Benign Course, Mediterranean; RMSF: No Eschar Usually, Severe, Americas.
  1. What is the treatment for a pregnant woman with Scrub Typhus?
  • Chloramphenicol 500mg QID PO/IV (Doxycycline Contraindicated), Azithromycin Alternative (Limited Data).
  1. What is the Weil-Felix test and its limitations?
  • Agglutination Test using Proteus Antigens (OX19, OX2, OXK); Low Sensitivity/Specificity (Obsolete); Used Only in Resource-Limited Settings.
  1. How do you diagnose Rickettsial infection?
  • IFA (Gold Standard): IgG/IgM Seroconversion (Paired Sera 2-4 Weeks); PCR (Blood/Eschar Swab, Early); Immunohistochemistry (Tissue Biopsy).
  1. What is the drug of choice for all Rickettsial infections?
  • Doxycycline 100mg PO/IV BD (7-14 Days until Afebrile 48h).
  1. What is the significance of an eschar in a febrile patient?
  • Pathognomonic for Spotted Fever Group Rickettsiosis or Scrub Typhus; Absent in Typhus Group.
  1. How do you manage Scrub Typhus with pneumonitis?
  • Doxycycline 100mg BD IV/PO + Supportive (Oxygen, Ventilation if Severe); Chloramphenicol if Pregnancy.
  1. Differentiate Epidemic Typhus from Murine Typhus.
  • Epidemic: Louse-Borne, Crowded/Displaced, Severe, Neurological Involvement, NO ESCHAR; Murine: Flea-Borne (Rat), Mild, NO ESCHAR, Worldwide Ports.

12. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Spotted Fever vs Typhus GroupSpotted Fever = Eschar Present; Typhus Group = No Eschar
RMSF vs MSFRMSF: Americas, No Eschar, Severe; MSF: Mediterranean, Eschar Present, Benign
Scrub Typhus vs Murine TyphusScrub: Eschar + Lymphadenopathy, Mite Vector; Murine: No Eschar, Flea Vector, No Lymphadenopathy
Epidemic vs Murine TyphusEpidemic: Louse, Severe, Neurological, Crowded; Murine: Flea, Milder, Worldwide Ports
Weil-Felix vs IFAWeil-Felix = Obsolete, Low Sensitivity; IFA = Gold Standard
MnemonicMeaning
"Eschar = Spotted Fever / Scrub Typhus"Eschar = SFG/Scrub
"No Eschar = Typhus Group"Typhus = No Eschar
"Doxycycline = Universal for Rickettsial"Universal Treatment
"RMSF = No Eschar, Severe, Palmoplantar Rash"RMSF Features
"Scrub = Eschar + Lymphadenopathy + Pneumonitis"Scrub Typhus
"Chloramphenicol = Pregnancy / Kids"Pregnancy Alternative

13. 🗺️ Mind Map

Rickettsial Infections
Spotted Fever Group (SFG)
RMSF: R. rickettsii, Dermacentor, USA, No Eschar, Severe
MSF: R. conorii, Rhipicephalus, Mediterranean, Eschar (Tache Noire)
ATBF: R. africae, Amblyomma, Africa, Multiple Eschars, Mild
Typhus Group
Epidemic: R. prowazekii, Louse, Crowded, NO Eschar, Severe
Murine: R. typhi, Flea (Rat), Worldwide, NO Eschar, Mild
Scrub Typhus
Orientia tsutsugamushi
Leptotrombidium Mite
Asia-Pacific (Tsutsugamushi Triangle)
Eschar + Lymphadenopathy + Pneumonitis
Diagnosis
IFA (Gold Standard, Paired Sera)
PCR (Blood/Eschar)
Weil-Felix (Obsolete)
Treatment
Doxycycline 100mg BD (All)
Chloramphenicol (Pregnancy/Children)
Ceftriaxone (CNS)
Complications
Vasculitis, Encephalitis, Renal Failure, ARDS, DIC
Scrub: Pneumonitis, Meningoencephalitis
RMSF: High Mortality if Delayed Doxy

14. 📋 One-Page Revision Card

RICKETTSIAL INFECTIONS – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
Unifying Rx: Doxycycline 100mg BD PO/IV (All Rickettsial)
Pregnancy/Children <8y: Chloramphenicol 500mg QID
Spotted Fever Group (SFG): Eschar (Tache Noire) Present
- RMSF: R. rickettsii, No Eschar, Centrifugal Rash, Palmoplantar, Severe
- MSF: R. conorii, Eschar (Tache Noire), Mediterranean, Benign
- ATBF: R. africae, Multiple Eschars, Lymphadenopathy, Mild
Typhus Group: NO ESCHAR
- Epidemic: R. prowazekii, Louse, Crowded, Severe, CNS
- Murine: R. typhi, Flea (Rat), Mild, Worldwide
Scrub Typhus: Orientia tsutsugamushi, Mite, Eschar + Lymphadenopathy + Pneumonitis, Asia-Pacific
Diagnosis: IFA (Gold Standard, Paired Sera), PCR (Eschar/Blood), Weil-Felix (Obsolete)
Treatment: Doxycycline 100mg BD ×7-14d; Pregnancy/Child: Chloramphenicol
Complications: Vasculitis, Encephalitis, Renal Failure, ARDS, DIC, Myocarditis
Weil-Felix: OX19/OX2 = Typhus/SFG; OXK = Scrub Typhus (Obsolete)
Key Differentiator: Eschar = SFG/Scrub; No Eschar = Typhus

15. 📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScore (1-5)Next Review
Day 12025-06-172025-06-18
Day 3
Day 7
Day 15

16. 🎯 Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

LevelContent
Must KnowDoxycycline universal 1st line, Eschar = Spotted Fever/Scrub vs No Eschar = Typhus, RMSF vs MSF vs ATBF, Scrub typhus eschar/lymphadenopathy/pneumonitis, IFA gold standard, Weil-Felix obsolete, Doxycycline dosing, Pregnancy = Chloramphenicol
Should KnowSpecific vector for each (Dermacentor, Rhipicephalus, Amblyomma, Ixodes, Louse, Flea, Mite), Geographic distribution, RMSF palmoplantar rash, Scrub typhus pneumonitis, Anaplasma/Ehrlichia (HGA/HME) differentiation, IFA methodology, Weil-Felix antigen reactivity patterns
Nice to KnowRickettsial genomics, Vacuole escape mechanism, Actin-based motility, Host immune evasion, Vaccine development (historical failures), Bioterrorism potential (R. prowazekii), Climate change impact on vector distribution, Co-infections (Babesia, Borrelia), Antimicrobial resistance in Rickettsia

SectionScore (0-10)Notes
Classification & Vector

Eschar Significance
Diagnosis (IFA, PCR, Weil-Felix)
Treatment (Doxycycline, Chloramphenicol)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.41 Schistosomiasis
[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Parasites (Helminths) → Schistosomiasis
FCPS/MRCP Focus: S. haematobium (Urinary), S. mansoni/japonicum (Intestinal/Hepatic), acute Katayama fever, chronic hepatic/intestinal/urinary, praziquantel, egg detection, hepatosplenic disease

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Identify Schistosome Species: S. haematobium (Urinary), S. mansoni (Intestinal/Hepatic), S. japonicum (Intestinal/Hepatic, Zoonotic), S. intercalatum, S. mekongi
  • [ ] Understand Life Cycle: Snail Intermediate Host → Cercariae → Skin Penetration → Schistosomula → Portal Venous System → Adult Worms → Egg Migration
  • [ ] Recognise Acute (Katayama Fever): Fever, Cough, Hepatosplenomegaly, Eosinophilia (Weeks Post-Exposure)
  • [ ] Recognise Chronic Intestinal (S. mansoni/japonicum): Abdominal Pain, Bloody Diarrhoea, Hepatosplenomegaly, Periportal Fibrosis (Pipestem Fibrosis), Portal Hypertension
  • [ ] Recognise Chronic Urinary (S. haematobium): Terminal Haematuria, Bladder Pathology, SCC Risk, Hydronephrosis, Renal Failure
  • [ ] Diagnose: Egg Detection (Urine/Stool), Serology (ELISA/IHA), PCR, Ultrasound (Liver/Spleen/Bladder), Cystoscopy
  • [ ] Manage: Praziquantel 40mg/kg Single Dose (1st Line All Species), Oxamniquine (S. mansoni Alternative), Corticosteroids (Acute Katayama)
  • [ ] Recognise Complications: Portal Hypertension, Variceal Bleeding, Splenomegaly, Bladder Cancer (SCC), Glomerulonephritis, Pulmonary Hypertension

2. 📖 Life Cycle & Transmission

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Eggs in Water (Urine/Stool)
C
E
↓
Miracidium Hatches] --> C[Snail Intermediate Host (Specific per Species)
Sporocyst → Cercariae Released] --> E[Cercariae Penetrate Human Skin
Schistosomula → Lung → Liver → Portal Vein
↓
Adult Worms Mate in Mesenteric/Pelvic Veins
↓
& H2
S. mansoni/japonicum → Mesenteric Veins → Eggs in Stool
↓
Eggs → Tissue Trapping (Granulomas) OR Excreted in Urine/Stool
SpeciesAdult LocationEgg ExcretionGeographic Distribution
S. haematobiumVesical/Pelvic Venous PlexusUrine (Terminal Haematuria)Africa, Middle East
S. mansoniInferior Mesenteric VeinsStoolAfrica, Middle East, South America, Caribbean
S. japonicumSuperior Mesenteric VeinsStoolChina, Philippines, Philippines, Indonesia
S. intercalatumMesenteric VeinsStoolCentral/West Africa
S. mekongiMesenteric VeinsStoolMekong River Basin (Laos/Cambodia)
[!tip] S. haematobium = Urinary (Terminal Haematuria); S. mansoni/japonicum = Intestinal/Hepatic (Stool Eggs). Snail = Intermediate Host (Species-Specific).

3. 📖 Clinical Syndromes

1. Acute Schistosomiasis (Katayama Fever)

FeatureDetails
Timing4-8 Weeks Post-Exposure (Coincides with Egg Laying)
SymptomsHigh Fever, Cough, Hepatosplenomegaly, Eosinophilia (Marked), Rash, Abdominal Pain, Diarrhoea
PathophysiologyImmune Complex Reaction to Egg Antigens (Type III Hypersensitivity)
SpeciesS. japonicum > S. mansoni > S. haematobium

2. Chronic Intestinal / Hepatic Schistosomiasis (S. mansoni, S. japonicum)

FeatureDetails
IntestinalAbdominal Pain, Bloody/Mucus Diarrhoea, Tenesmus, Rectal Prolapse (Heavy Infection)
Hepatic (Hepatosplenic)Periportal Fibrosis (Pipestem Fibrosis) → Portal Hypertension, Splenomegaly, Oesophageal Varices, Ascites, Thrombocytopenia
S. japonicumMore Severe, Higher Egg Output, More Hepatic Involvement, Zoonotic (Cattle/Water Buffalo)

3. Urinary Schistosomiasis (S. haematobium)

FeatureDetails
Cardinal SignTerminal Haematuria (Painless, End of Micturition)
Bladder PathologySandy Patches, Granulomas, Ulceration, Calcification, Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) Risk ↑
Upper TractUreteric Strictures, Hydronephrosis, Renal Failure
GenitalSeminal Vesicle/Prostate Involvement, Haematospermia, Infertility

4. 🔬 Diagnosis

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected Schistosomiasis
↓
**Species / Clinical Syndrome**
↓
**Urine Microscopy (Terminal Urine)**
**Stool Microscopy (Kato-Katz)**
**Serology (ELISA/IHA/IFA)**
**Imaging**
↓
**Eggs with Terminal Spine** (S. haematobium)
**Lateral Spine Egg** (S. mansoni) / **Lateral Knob** (S. japonicum)
**US Liver/Spleen** (Portal Hypertension, Bladder Wall Thickening)
**Cystoscopy** (Bladder Lesions)

Diagnostic Methods

MethodSampleSensitivity/SpecificityRole
Urine Microscopy (Terminal)Terminal UrineHigh (S. haematobium)1st Line (Urinary)
Stool Microscopy (Kato-Katz)StoolQuantitative (EPG)1st Line (Intestinal)
Urine FiltrationUrineConcentrates EggsField-Friendly
Serology (ELISA/IHA/IFA)SerumHigh Sens/SpecTravellers, Low Parasite Load
PCR (Urine/Stool/Blood)VariousHigh Sens/SpecLow Parasite Load, Species ID
Ultrasound (Liver/Spleen/Bladder)—Portal Hypertension, Bladder WallMorbidity Assessment
CystoscopyBladderVisualize LesionsBladder Pathology Assessment
[!tip] Urine Microscopy (Terminal) = S. haematobium. Stool Kato-Katz = S. mansoni/japonicum. Serology = Travellers/Low Burden. Ultrasound = Morbidity Assessment (Portal Hypertension, Bladder).

5. 💊 Management

Praziquantel — Drug of Choice for ALL Species

SpeciesDoseSchedule
S. haematobium / S. mansoni / S. intercalatum40mg/kg Single DoseSingle Dose
S. japonicum / S. mekongi60mg/kg (Divided 2-3 Doses)Single Day
Acute Katayama FeverPraziquantel 40-60mg/kg + Prednisolone 1mg/kg (Anti-Inflammatory)Single Dose + Steroids 5-7 Days
[!warning] Praziquantel = Drug of Choice for ALL Schistosome Species. Single Dose (40-60mg/kg). Give with Food (↑ Absorption). Corticosteroids for Acute Katayama (Anti-Inflammatory).

Oxamniquine — Alternative for S. mansoni Only

IndicationDoseNotes
S. mansoni (Praziquantel Failure/Allergy)15mg/kg Single Dose (Up to 40mg/kg)Only for S. mansoni

Chronic Complications Management

ComplicationManagement
Portal Hypertension / VaricesBeta-Blockers, Endoscopic Band Ligation, TIPS, Splenectomy
Bladder Cancer (SCC)Cystoscopic Surveillance, TURBT, Radical Cystectomy
GlomerulonephritisACEi/ARB, Immunosuppression (If Nephrotic), Praziquantel
Pulmonary HypertensionAnticoagulation, PDE5 Inhibitors, Prostacyclin, Lung Transplant (End-Stage)
NeuroschistosomiasisPraziquantel + Corticosteroids (Dexamethasone)

6. 🔬 Diagnostic Summary Table

SpeciesEgg MorphologySampleKey Feature
S. haematobiumTerminal SpineUrine (Terminal)Terminal Haematuria
S. mansoniLateral SpineStoolHepatosplenic Disease
S. japonicumLateral Knob / Small SpineStoolMore Severe, Zoonotic
S. intercalatumTerminal Spine (Longer)StoolMild Intestinal
S. mekongiSmall Lateral SpineStoolMekong Basin
[!tip] Egg Morphology = Species ID. Terminal Spine = S. haematobium (Urine). Lateral Spine = S. mansoni (Stool). Lateral Knob = S. japonicum (Stool).

7. 🩺 Morbidity Assessment (WHO Ultrasound Grading)

OrganFindings
LiverPeriportal Fibrosis (Pipestem), Portal Vein Dilatation, Splenomegaly
SpleenSplenomegaly (Size, Texture)
BladderWall Thickening, Sandy Patches, Polyps, Masses (SCC), Calcification
KidneyHydronephrosis, Ureteric Dilatation

8. 💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Life CycleSnail → Cercaria → Skin Penetration → Schistosomula → Portal Vein → Adult Worms → Eggs
S. haematobiumUrinary, Terminal Haematuria, Terminal Spine Egg, Bladder Cancer (SCC) Risk
S. mansoniIntestinal/Hepatic, Lateral Spine Egg, Pipestem Fibrosis, Portal HTN
S. japonicumIntestinal/Hepatic, More Severe, Zoonotic, Lateral Knob Egg
Acute (Katayama)Fever, Cough, Hepatosplenomegaly, Eosinophilia (Weeks Post-Exposure) → Praziquantel + Prednisolone
Chronic IntestinalAbdominal Pain, Bloody Diarrhoea, Hepatosplenomegaly, Portal HTN
Chronic UrinaryTerminal Haematuria, Bladder Pathology, SCC Risk
DiagnosisEgg Microscopy (Urine/Stool/Kato-Katz), Serology/PCR, Ultrasound (Morbidity)
TreatmentPraziquantel 40mg/kg Single Dose (haematobium/mansoni), 60mg/kg (japonicum)
Acute KatayamaPraziquantel + Prednisolone
ComplicationsPortal HTN, Variceal Bleeding, Bladder SCC, Glomerulonephrosis, Pulm HTN

9. ❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the drug of choice for all Schistosome species?
  • Praziquantel 40mg/kg Single Dose (60mg/kg for S. japonicum).
  1. How do you differentiate Schistosome species by egg morphology?
  • S. haematobium = Terminal Spine (Urine); S. mansoni = Lateral Spine (Stool); S. japonicum = Lateral Knob/Small Spine (Stool).
  1. What is Katayama Fever and how is it managed?
  • Acute Serum Sickness-Like Illness (Fever, Cough, Hepatosplenomegaly, Eosinophilia) 4-8 Weeks Post-Exposure → Praziquantel + Prednisolone 1mg/kg.
  1. What is the clinical presentation of S. haematobium infection?
  • Terminal Haematuria (Painless, End of Micturition), Bladder Pathology (Sandy Patches, Granulomas), SCC Risk.
  1. What is the pathology of chronic S. mansoni infection?
  • Periportal Fibrosis (Pipestem Fibrosis) → Portal Hypertension → Splenomegaly, Oesophageal Varices, Ascites.
  1. What is the egg morphology of S. haematobium and where is it found?
  • Terminal Spine, Found in Urine (Terminal Specimen Best).
  1. What are the complications of chronic S. haematobium infection?
  • Bladder Cancer (SCC), Ureteric Stricture → Hydronephrosis → Renal Failure, Glomerulonephritis.
  1. How do you differentiate S. mansoni from S. japonicum clinically?
  • S. japonicum = More Severe, Higher Egg Output, Zoonotic (Cattle/Water Buffalo), Lateral Knob Egg.
  1. What is the treatment for Katayama Fever?
  • Praziquantel 40-60mg/kg + Prednisolone 1mg/kg/day × 5-7 Days.
  1. What is the diagnostic method of choice for S. haematobium?
  • Urine Microscopy (Terminal Urine) for Terminal Spine Eggs; Filtration Concentrates Eggs.
  1. What is the treatment for S. japonicum infection?
  • Praziquantel 60mg/kg (Divided 2-3 Doses) Single Day.
  1. What is the "Pipestem Fibrosis" in Schistosomiasis?
  • Periportal Fibrosis radiating from Portal Vein → Portal Hypertension → Splenomegaly, Varices.

10. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
S. haematobium vs S. mansoni vs S. japonicumHaematobium = Urine + Terminal Spine; Mansoni = Stool + Lateral Spine; Japonicum = Stool + Lateral Knob
Katayama vs Acute SchistosomiasisSame — Acute Serum Sickness-Like Reaction ~4-8 Weeks Post-Infection
Praziquantel vs OxamniquinePraziquantel = All Species; Oxamniquine = S. mansoni Only
S. mansoni vs S. japonicum EggLateral Spine vs Lateral Knob
S. haematobium vs Bladder CancerChronic Infection → SCC Risk; Cystoscopy Surveillance
MnemonicMeaning
"Haematobium = Haematuria + Terminal Spine"Urinary Schisto
"Mansoni = Mansoni = Stool + Lateral Spine"Intestinal Schisto
"Japonicum = Japan/Asia + Lateral Knob + Severe"Japanese Schisto
"Praziquantel = 40mg/kg (All), 60mg/kg (Japonicum)"Dosing
"Katayama = Fever + Cough + Hepatosplenomegaly + Eosinophilia"Acute Schisto
"Pipestem Fibrosis = Portal Hypertension"Chronic Hepatic

11. 🗺️ Mind Map

Schistosomiasis
Life Cycle
Eggs in Water → Miracidium → Snail → Cercaria
Skin Penetration → Schistosomula → Lung → Liver → Portal Vein
Adult Worms → Mesenteric (Mansoni/Japonicum) / Vesical (Haematobium)
Eggs → Veins → Intestine/Bladder → Water
Species
S. haematobium: Urine, Terminal Spine, Terminal Haematuria, Bladder Cancer
S. mansoni: Stool, Lateral Spine, Pipestem Fibrosis, Portal HTN
S. japonicum: Stool, Lateral Knob, Zoonotic, Severe
S. intercalatum/meongi: Minor
Clinical
Acute: Katayama Fever (Fever, Cough, Hepatosplenomegaly, Eosinophilia)
Chronic Intestinal: Pain, Bloody Diarrhoea
Chronic Hepatic: Pipestem Fibrosis, Portal HTN, Varices
Chronic Urinary: Terminal Haematuria, Bladder Pathology, SCC
Diagnosis
Urine Microscopy (Terminal) → Haematobium
Stool Kato-Katz → Mansoni/Japonicum
Serology/PCR
US Liver/Spleen/Bladder
Treatment
Praziquantel 40mg/kg (All except Japonicum)
Praziquantel 60mg/kg (Japonicum)
Katayama: Praziquantel + Prednisolone
Complications
Portal HTN, Varices, Ascites
Bladder SCC
Glomerulonephritis
Pulm HTN
Neuro (Rare)

12. 📋 One-Page Revision Card

SCHISTOSOMIASIS – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
S. haematobium: Urine, Terminal Spine, Terminal Haematuria, Bladder SCC
S. mansoni: Stool, Lateral Spine, Pipestem Fibrosis, Portal HTN
S. japonicum: Stool, Lateral Knob, Zoonotic, Severe, Meningitis Risk
Katayama Fever: 4-8w Post-Exposure, Fever/Cough/HSM/Eosinophilia, Praziquantel + Prednisolone
Chronic Intestinal: Abdominal Pain, Bloody Diarrhoea, Hepatosplenomegaly
Chronic Hepatic: Pipestem Fibrosis → Portal HTN → Varices/Ascites
Chronic Urinary: Terminal Haematuria, Bladder Pathology, SCC Risk
Diagnosis: Urine Microscopy (Terminal) for haematobium; Stool Kato-Katz for mansoni/japonicum
Treatment: Praziquantel 40mg/kg Single Dose (All except Japonicum 60mg/kg)
Acute Katayama: Praziquantel + Prednisolone
Complications: Portal HTN, Varices, Bladder SCC, Glomerulonephritis, Pulm HTN

14. 🎯 Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

LevelContent
Must KnowSpecies differentiation by egg morphology, Clinical syndromes (Katayama, Intestinal, Hepatic, Urinary), Praziquantel dosing (40 vs 60mg/kg), Katayama management, Complications (Portal HTN, Bladder SCC, Glomerulonephritis), Egg morphology (Terminal/Lateral Spine/Knob)
Should KnowSnail intermediate hosts, Life cycle details, Kato-Katz technique, Ultrasound morbidity grading, Oxamniquine alternative for S. mansoni, S. intercalatum/meongi, Neuroschistosomiasis, Pregnancy management, Control programs (MDA), Vaccine development
Nice to KnowHybrid schistosomes, Hybridization zones, Snail control (Molluscicides), Genetic susceptibility, Vaccine candidates (Sm14, Sh28GST), Climate change impact on distribution, Economic burden, Elimination targets (WHO 2030), One Health approach, Schistosomiasis-HIV co-infection

Species Differentiation (Egg Morphology)

Complications & Management
