▸ 14.1.42 Sepsis And Septic Shock

Related: [[Fever and Septic Syndrome Approach]], [[Acute Bacterial Meningitis]], [[Brain Abscess]], [[Infective Endocarditis]], [[Post-Transplant Infections]]

[!important]
Sepsis = life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection. Septic shock = sepsis + persisting hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥65mmHg + lactate >2mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. TIME IS TISSUE — sepsis bundle within 1 hour. Source control is as important as antibiotics.

Learning Objectives

  • Apply Sepsis-3 definitions (SOFA/qSOFA) and identify septic shock
  • Execute 1-hour sepsis bundle: lactate, blood cultures, broad antibiotics, fluid resuscitation, vasopressors
  • Select empirical antibiotics by suspected source and local epidemiology
  • Manage fluid resuscitation, vasopressor escalation (norepinephrine → vasopressin → adrenaline), and adjuncts
  • Recognise and treat lactic acidosis, coagulopathy, AKI, ARDS
  • Implement source control (drainage, debridement, device removal)
  • Understand corticosteroids in septic shock (hydrocortisone 200mg/day)
  • Monitor dynamic indices of fluid responsiveness

Definitions (Sepsis-3, 2016)

TermDefinition
InfectionSuspected/documented pathogen invasion
SepsisLife-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection = SOFA score increase ≥2 points from baseline
Septic shockSubset of sepsis with circulatory, cellular, metabolic abnormalities substantially increasing mortality: vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65mmHg + serum lactate >2mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation
qSOFAQuick SOFA (screening tool, NOT diagnostic): RR ≥22, AMS (GCS<15), SBP ≤100mmHg — ≥2 points = high risk for poor outcome
[!tip]
SOFA ≥2 = sepsis diagnosis. qSOFA ≥2 = screen for ICU referral/early intervention. Do NOT use qSOFA to rule out sepsis.

SOFA Score (Sepsis-related Organ Failure Assessment)

System01234
Respiratory (PaO₂/FiO₂)≥400<400<300<200 (vent)<100 (vent)
Coagulation (Platelets)≥150<150<100<50<20
Liver (Bilirubin)<1.21.2–1.92.0–5.96.0–11.9>12.0
CardiovascularMAP≥70MAP<70Dopamine ≤5 or DobutamineDopamine >5 or Epi ≤0.1 or NorEpi ≤0.1Dopamine >15 or Epi >0.1 or NorEpi >0.1
CNS (GCS)1513–1410–126–9<6
Renal (Creatinine/UO)<1.21.2–1.92.0–3.43.5–4.9 / <500mL/d>5.0 / <200mL/d
[!tip]
Baseline SOFA assumed 0 if unknown. Increase ≥2 = sepsis. Cardiovascular SOFA drives septic shock definition.

Pathophysiology — Dysregulated Host Response

PhaseMechanism
HyperinflammatoryPAMPs/DAMPs → TLRs → NF-κB → cytokine storm (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IFN-γ) → vasodilation, capillary leak, myocardial depression
Immunosuppressive (CARS)Anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-10, TGF-β), HLA-DR downregulation, lymphocyte apoptosis, endotoxin tolerance → secondary infections
CoagulopathyTissue factor → thrombin → fibrin deposition → DIC; Protein C/S depletion, antithrombin consumption
MetabolicMitochondrial dysfunction → aerobic glycolysis (Warburg effect) → lactate ↑ (not just hypoperfusion); cytopathic hypoxia

1-Hour Sepsis Bundle (Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

ActionTarget
1. Measure lactateInitial + repeat if >2mmol/L
2. Blood cultures (×2 sets, aerobic/anaerobic)BEFORE antibiotics (but do NOT delay abx >45min for cultures)
3. Broad-spectrum IV antibioticsWithin 1 HOUR of recognition
4. Fluid resuscitation30mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4mmol/L
5. VasopressorsIf hypotensive after fluids → Norepinephrine first-line to MAP ≥65mmHg
6. Source controlIdentify and intervene within 6–12 hours (drainage, debridement, device removal)

Sepsis-3 Septic Shock Criteria (Clinical)

ALL 3 must be present:

  1. Sepsis (SOFA ≥2 / suspected infection + organ dysfunction)
  2. Vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65mmHg
  3. Lactate >2mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation (30mL/kg crystalloid)
[!warning]
Mortality: Sepsis ~25–30%, Septic shock ~40–50%. Every hour of antibiotic delay ↑ mortality ~7–8%.

qSOFA (Bedside Screen — NOT for Diagnosis)

ParameterPoints
Respiratory rate ≥22/min1
Altered mental status (GCS <15)1
Systolic BP ≤100mmHg1
Total ≥2High risk for in-hospital mortality / ICU admission
[!tip]
qSOFA ≥2 → activate sepsis protocol, consider ICU, measure lactate, start bundle. qSOFA sensitivity ~60%, specificity ~80% for sepsis.

Empirical Antibiotic Selection — Source-Based

Suspected SourceEmpirical RegimenKey Coverage
Unknown source (community)Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV 6h OR Meropenem 1g IV 8h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h (if MRSA risk)Gram-negatives (including Pseudomonas), anaerobes, Gram-positives
Pneumonia (CAP severe)Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12h + Azithromycin 500mg IV/PO OD OR Levofloxacin 750mg IV ODS. pneumoniae, atypicals
Pneumonia (HAP/VAP)Pip-tazo 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h OR Cefepime 2g 8h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h (+/- Aztreonam if Pseudomonas)Pseudomonas, MRSA, Gram-negatives
Intra-abdominalPip-tazo 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h + Vancomycin if MRSA riskEnterobacteriaceae, anaerobes (Bacteroides), Enterococcus
UTI / PyelonephritisCeftriaxone 2g 12h OR Pip-tazo 4.5g 6h + Vancomycin if enterococcal/MRSA riskE. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, Pseudomonas
Skin/Soft tissue (necrotising)Pip-tazo 4.5g 6h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h + Clindamycin 900mg 8hMSSA/MRSA, streptococci, anaerobes, Gram-neg; clindamycin = toxin suppression
CNS (meningitis)See Acute Bacterial Meningitis note
Endocarditis (native valve)See Infective Endocarditis note
Febrile neutropeniaPip-tazo 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h (+ Vancomycin if catheter/HSV/mucositis)Pseudomonas, Gram-negatives
[!important]
Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h (trough 15–20) for MRSA coverage. Add if: MRSA risk factors (colonisation, prior MRSA, skin/soft tissue, prosthesis, IVDU, hospital exposure). De-escalate at 48–72h based on cultures.

Fluid Resuscitation

PrincipleDetail
Fluid typeBalanced crystalloid (Plasma-Lyte, Ringer's lactate) preferred over 0.9% saline (less hyperchloraemic acidosis, less AKI)
Initial bolus30mL/kg over 1–3 hours (e.g., 2–3L for 70kg)
ReassessmentDynamic indices after each bolus: passive leg raise (PLR), stroke volume variation (SVV), pulse pressure variation (PPV), echocardiographic IVC variation
Stopping pointNo fluid responsiveness (ΔSV <10–15% with PLR/500mL bolus) OR signs of fluid overload (pulmonary oedema, rising CVP/JVP)
AlbuminConsider if large-volume resuscitation (>4–5L crystalloid) or persistent hypoalbuminaemia
AvoidHydroxyethyl starch (HES) — ↑ AKI, mortality; gelatin — similar

Vasopressor Escalation

AgentDoseRoleKey Points
Norepinephrine0.01–3μg/kg/minFirst-lineα₁ > β₁; ↑ MAP, ↑ SVR, mild ↑ CO; start peripherally if central line delayed
Vasopressin0.03U/min (fixed)Second-line (add-on)V₁ receptors; spares catecholamines; ↓ norepinephrine dose; do NOT titrate
Adrenaline (Epinephrine)0.01–3μg/kg/minThird-line / alternativeα₁+β₁+β₂; ↑ CO, ↑ HR, ↑ lactate (β₂), arrhythmia risk
Dopamine2–20μg/kg/minAvoid (arrhythmia, ↑ mortality)β₁ > α₁ at low dose; α₁ at high; not recommended
Phenylephrine0.5–10μg/kg/minAlternative if tachycardia/arrhythmiaPure α₁; ↓ CO, ↓ splanchnic perfusion
[!tip]
MAP target ≥65mmHg (individualise: chronic HTN may need 70–75mmHg). Norepinephrine first, add vasopressin 0.03U/min to reduce NE dose, then adrenaline if needed.

Adjunctive Therapies

TherapyIndicationDose / Detail
HydrocortisoneSeptic shock requiring vasopressors (NE >0.25–0.5μg/kg/min)200mg/day IV continuous infusion OR 50mg IV 6h ×7–10d taper; do NOT give bolus
Vitamin C / Thiamine / Hydrocortisone (HAT)Not routinely recommendedEvidence inconsistent (VITAMINS, VICTAS trials negative); not standard

...truncated from 578 to 120 lines

Topic Overview

Learning Objectives
Definitions (Sepsis-3, 2016)
SOFA Score (Sepsis-related Organ Failure Assessment)
Pathophysiology — Dysregulated Host Response
1-Hour Sepsis Bundle (Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)
Sepsis-3 Septic Shock Criteria (Clinical)
qSOFA (Bedside Screen — NOT for Diagnosis)
Empirical Antibiotic Selection — Source-Based
Fluid Resuscitation
Vasopressor Escalation
Adjunctive Therapies
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.43 Sexually Transmitted Infections Stis

Related: [[HIV Infection and AIDS]], [[Pelvic Inflammatory Disease]], [[Epididymo-orchitis]], [[Reactive Arthritis]], [[Dermatology (Genital Ulcers)]]

[!important]
STI = infection transmitted predominantly through sexual contact. Syndromic management: urethral discharge, vaginal discharge, genital ulcer, inguinal bubo, scrotal swelling, neonatal conjunctivitis. Partner notification and treatment essential. Screening: chlamydia/gonorrhoea (NAAT), syphilis (serology), HIV, HBV, HCV. Expedited partner therapy (EPT) for chlamydia/gonorrhoea.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Diagnose common STIs using clinical syndromes and laboratory tests
  • Apply syndromic management algorithms
  • Select appropriate antimicrobial therapy for each STI
  • Manage complications (PID, epididymo-orchitis, neonatal infection)
  • Conduct partner notification and expedited partner therapy
  • Screen appropriately (chlamydia, gonorrhoea, syphilis, HIV, HBV, HCV)
  • Recognise antimicrobial resistance (gonorrhoea, mycoplasma genitalium)

2. Syndromic Classification & Common Aetiology

SyndromeCommon Pathogens
Urethral discharge (male)Chlamydia trachomatis (40–50%), Neisseria gonorrhoeae (30–40%), M. genitalium (15–25%), Ureaplasma, Trichomonas
Vaginal discharge (female)C. trachomatis, N. gonorrhoeae, T. vaginalis, Candida, BV, M. genitalium
Genital ulcerHSV-1/2 (most common), Treponema pallidum (syphilis), Haemophilus ducreyi (chancroid), Klebsiella granulomatis (donovanosis), LGV
Inguinal buboLGV (C. trachomatis L1–L3), chancroid, syphilis
Scrotal swellingC. trachomatis, N. gonorrhoeae (epididymo-orchitis)
Neonatal conjunctivitisC. trachomatis (5–14 days), N. gonorrhoeae (2–5 days), HSV

3. Key STIs — Clinical Features, Diagnosis, Treatment

1. Chlamydia trachomatis

AspectDetails
ClinicalOften asymptomatic (70% women, 50% men); urethritis, cervicitis, PID, epididymitis, LGV (L1–L3), neonatal conjunctivitis/pneumonia
DiagnosisNAAT (urine, swab) — gold standard; culture rarely used
Treatment (Uncomplicated)Doxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×7 days (1st line)
AlternativeAzithromycin 1g PO single dose (if adherence concern, pregnancy)
LGVDoxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×21 days
PregnancyAzithromycin 1g PO single dose (or Amoxicillin 500mg PO 8h ×7d)
Partner notificationAll partners last 6 months (or last partner if >6m)

2. Neisseria gonorrhoeae (Gonorrhoea)

AspectDetails
ClinicalUrethritis, cervicitis, pharyngitis, proctitis, PID, DGI (arthritis-dermatitis), neonatal ophthalmia
DiagnosisNAAT (urine, swab); Culture + AST essential (resistance monitoring)
Treatment (Uncomplicated)Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose (if <150kg) / 1g IM if ≥150kg
PLUSDoxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×7d (if chlamydia not excluded) OR Azithromycin 1g PO (if doxy contraindicated)
PharyngealCeftriaxone 1g IM single dose (higher failure at pharynx)
DGICeftriaxone 1g IV/IM 24h ×7d (switch oral after 24–48h improvement)
ResistanceRising MICs to ceftriaxone, azithromycin; ceftriaxone 1g IM if decreased susceptibility suspected
PregnancyCeftriaxone 500mg/1g IM + Azithromycin 1g PO

3. Syphilis (Treponema pallidum)

StageClinical FeaturesTreatment
PrimaryChancre: painless, clean-based, indurated ulcer at inoculation site (3–90d); regional lymphadenopathyBenzathine penicillin G 2.4MU IM single dose
SecondaryRash (palms/soles, trunk), condyloma lata (moist, infectious), mucous patches, generalised lymphadenopathy, fever, malaise (4–10w)Benzathine penicillin G 2.4MU IM single dose
Early Latent<1 year post-infection; seropositive, no symptomsBenzathine penicillin G 2.4MU IM single dose
Late Latent>1 year or unknown duration; seropositiveBenzathine penicillin G 2.4MU IM weekly ×3 doses
TertiaryGummatous (skin, bone, liver), Cardiovascular (aortitis, aneurysm), NeurosyphilisSee Neurosyphilis
NeurosyphilisMeningitis, stroke, tabes dorsalis, general paresis, ocular/otic syphilisPenicillin G 18–24MU/day IV ÷4h ×14d (or Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12h ×14d)
Ocular/Otic SyphilisUveitis, optic neuritis, hearing lossTreat as neurosyphilis (IV penicillin ×14d)
Special SituationsTreatment
Penicillin allergy (non-pregnant)Doxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×14d (early) / ×28d (late); Ceftriaxone 1g IM/IV daily ×10–14d
PregnancyPenicillin ONLY (desensitise if allergic); Benzathine penicillin G per stage
HIV coinfectionSame regimens; closer follow-up (RPR titres); consider neurosyphilis if CD4<350
Jarisch-HerxheimerFever, rigors, hypotension, worsening rash 2–12h post-treatment; self-limiting; prednisolone not routine
SerologyInterpretation
Non-treponemal (RPR, VDRL)Quantitative; correlates with disease activity; ↓4-fold = treatment response; can be negative in early primary/late tertiary
Treponemal (TPHA, TPPA, EIA, FTA-ABS)Qualitative; remains positive for life; confirms exposure
AlgorithmTraditional: Screen treponemal → confirm non-treponemal; Reverse: Screen non-treponemal → confirm treponemal

4. Genital Herpes (HSV-1/2)

AspectDetails
ClinicalPrimary: painful vesicles/ulcers, inguinal lymphadenopathy, systemic symptoms (fever, malaise); Recurrent: milder, shorter, prodrome (tingling, pain)
DiagnosisNAAT (swab from vesicle/ulcer) — gold standard; Tzanck smear (multinucleated giant cells) — low sensitivity; Type-specific serology (HSV-1/2 IgG) — for counselling, asymptomatic
Treatment (Episodic)Acyclovir 400mg PO 12h ×5d (or 800mg 12h ×5d); Valacyclovir 500mg 12h ×5d; Famciclovir 250mg 12h ×5d
Treatment (Suppressive)Acyclovir 400mg PO 12h; Valacyclovir 500mg PO OD; Famciclovir 250mg PO 12h — if ≥6 recurrences/year or severe psychosocial impact
Severe/ImmunocompromisedAcyclovir 5mg/kg IV 8h ×5–7d → oral step-down
PregnancySuppressive from 36 weeks (Acyclovir 400mg 12h or Valacyclovir 500mg OD) to reduce neonatal HSV; C-section if active lesions at delivery
Neonatal HSVAcyclovir 20mg/kg IV 8h ×14d (SEM) / ×21d (CNS/Disseminated)

5. Mycoplasma genitalium

AspectDetails
ClinicalUrethritis (often non-gonococcal, non-chlamydial), cervicitis, PID; increasing macrolide resistance
DiagnosisNAAT (with macrolide resistance detection if available)
Treatment (Resistance unknown / sensitive)Doxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×7d → Azithromycin 1g PO day 1, then 500mg daily ×3d (total 2.5g)
Treatment (Macrolide-resistant)Moxifloxacin 400mg PO OD ×7–14d (after doxycycline lead-in)
Resistance testingRecommended before treatment if available; guides macrolide vs fluoroquinolone choice

6. Trichomonas vaginalis

AspectDetails
ClinicalVaginitis (frothy yellow-green discharge, odour, pruritus, strawberry cervix); urethritis (often asymptomatic in men)
DiagnosisNAAT (gold standard); wet mount (motile trichomonads, sensitivity ~60%); culture
TreatmentMetronidazole 2g PO single dose (or 400mg 12h ×7d); Tinidazole 2g single dose
Partner treatmentSimultaneous (single dose metronidazole)
PregnancyMetronidazole 2g single dose safe after 1st trimester

7. Lymphogranuloma Venereum (LGV)

AspectDetails
AetiologyC. trachomatis serovars L1, L2, L3
ClinicalProctocolitis (MSM): rectal pain, discharge, bleeding, tenesmus, ulceration; Inguinal syndrome: bubo, fistulae; Genital ulcer (rare)
DiagnosisNAAT + genotyping (L1–L3) or rectal swab for LGV PCR
TreatmentDoxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×21 days
AlternativeErythromycin 500mg PO 6h ×21d (pregnancy)

8. Chancroid (Haemophilus ducreyi)

AspectDetails
ClinicalPainful, soft, purulent ulcer with ragged edges; painful inguinal lymphadenopathy (bubo)
DiagnosisClinical + PCR (specialised); culture difficult
TreatmentAzithromycin 1g PO single dose OR Ceftriaxone 250mg IM single dose OR Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO 12h ×3d OR Erythromycin 500mg PO 6h ×7d

9. Donovanosis (Klebsiella granulomatis)

AspectDetails
ClinicalProgressive, painless, beefy-red, granulomatous ulcers (pseudobuboes); genital, perineal, perianal
DiagnosisTissue smear (Donovan bodies — intracytoplasmic rods in macrophages); PCR
TreatmentAzithromycin 1g PO weekly ×3 weeks OR Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×3w OR TMP-SMX DS 12h ×3w

10. Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID)

AspectDetails
ClinicalLower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness, adnexal tenderness, fever, abnormal discharge, irregular bleeding
DiagnosisClinical (high index of suspicion); empirical treatment if criteria met; laparoscopy gold standard
Treatment (Outpatient, mild-moderate)Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose + Doxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×14d + Metronidazole 400mg PO 12h ×14d
Treatment (Inpatient, severe)Ceftriaxone 2g IV 24h + Doxycycline 100mg PO/IV 12h + Metronidazole 500mg IV 8h → step-down oral ×14d total
Alternative (if cephalosporin allergy)Ofloxacin 400mg PO 12h + Metronidazole 400mg PO 12h ×14d (NOT if gonorrhoea suspected)

11. Epididymo-orchitis

AspectDetails
<35 years / STI riskCeftriaxone 500mg IM single dose + Doxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×10–14d (covers C. trachomatis, N. gonorrhoeae)
>35 years / low STI risk / urinary tractCiprofloxacin 500mg PO 12h ×10–14d OR Ofloxacin 200mg PO 12h ×10–14d (covers Enterobacteriaceae)
Mumps orchitisSupportive (scrotal elevation, NSAIDs); no antibiotics

4. Partner Notification & Expedited Partner Therapy (EPT)

PrincipleDetails
Partner notificationAll sexual partners in relevant lookback period
Lookback periodsChlamydia: 6 months; Gonorrhoea: 2 months (or last partner); Syphilis: primary/secondary/early latent = 3m/6m/12m; HIV: all partners since last negative test
EPTPatient-delivered partner therapy for chlamydia (azithromycin 1g) and gonorrhoea (ceftriaxone 500mg IM + azithromycin 1g) where legal/approved
AbstinenceUntil both treated + 7 days after completion

5. STI Screening Recommendations

PopulationScreen For
All sexually active <25Annual Chlamydia (NAAT urine/swab); Gonorrhoea if risk factors
MSM3-site testing: pharyngeal, rectal, urethral for CT/NG; Syphilis (serology); HIV; HBV/HCV
PregnancySyphilis (booking, 28w, delivery if risk); HIV (booking); HBV (booking); Chlamydia/Gonorrhoea if <25 or risk; HCV if risk
New partner / symptomaticFull screen (CT, NG, Syphilis, HIV, HBV, HCV)
PrEP users3-monthly: HIV, Syphilis, CT/NG (3-site MSM), Renal function

6. Antimicrobial Resistance Concerns

PathogenResistance Issue
N. gonorrhoeaeRising ceftriaxone MICs (ceftriaxone 1g IM if decreased susceptibility); azithromycin resistance; MDR/XDR strains reported
M. genitaliumHigh macrolide resistance (mutations in 23S rRNA); moxifloxacin resistance emerging
C. trachomatisRare resistance to doxycycline/azithromycin
T. pallidumNo documented penicillin resistance

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Urethral discharge: CT (doxy 7d) → NG (ceftriaxone IM) → Mgen (doxy + azithro/moxi)
  • Vaginal discharge: CT/NG/T trichomonas/Candida/BV
  • Genital ulcer: HSV (NAAT, acyclovir) → Syphilis (RPR/TPPA, penicillin) → Chancroid/LGV/Donovanosis
  • Syphilis stages: Primary/Secondary/Early Latent = 2.4MU BPG ×1; Late Latent/Tertiary = 2.4MU BPG ×3 weekly; Neurosyphilis = Pen G IV 18-24MU/day ×14d

...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Syndromic Classification & Common Aetiology
3. Key STIs — Clinical Features, Diagnosis, Treatment
1. Chlamydia trachomatis
2. Neisseria gonorrhoeae (Gonorrhoea)
3. Syphilis (Treponema pallidum)
4. Genital Herpes (HSV-1/2)
5. Mycoplasma genitalium
6. Trichomonas vaginalis
7. Lymphogranuloma Venereum (LGV)
8. Chancroid (Haemophilus ducreyi)
9. Donovanosis (Klebsiella granulomatis)
10. Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID)
11. Epididymo-orchitis
4. Partner Notification & Expedited Partner Therapy (EPT)
5. STI Screening Recommendations
6. Antimicrobial Resistance Concerns
7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.44 Skin And Soft Tissue Infections Ssti

Related: [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Diabetic Foot Infection]], [[Bone and Joint Infections]], [[Clostridioides difficile Infection]], [[Antimicrobial Stewardship]]

[!important]
SSTI = infection of skin, subcutaneous tissue, fascia, muscle. Classification: non-purulent (cellulitis, erysipelas) vs purulent (abscess, furuncle, carbuncle). Severity: mild, moderate, severe (sepsis, necrotising). Non-purulent: β-haemolytic streptococci (Group A) → Penicillin/Cefazolin/Ceftriaxone. Purulent: S. aureus (MSSA/MRSA) → drainage + anti-staph. Necrotising fasciitis = SURGICAL EMERGENCY (LRINEC ≥6); broad antibiotics + urgent debridement. Diabetic foot: PEDIS classification; osteomyelitis probe-to-bone; offloading + vascular assessment.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify SSTI: non-purulent vs purulent; mild/moderate/severe/necrotising
  • Diagnose using clinical features, LRINEC score for necrotising fasciitis
  • Select empirical antibiotics by type, severity, MRSA risk
  • Manage abscess: incision & drainage primary; antibiotics adjunct
  • Recognise and urgently manage necrotising fasciitis (LRINEC, surgery)
  • Classify and manage diabetic foot infections (PEDIS, IDSA/IWGDF)
  • Determine treatment duration and IV→oral switch

2. Classification

CategorySubtypesTypical Pathogens
Non-purulentCellulitis, Erysipelasβ-haemolytic streptococci (Group A, C, G), S. aureus (less common)
PurulentAbscess, Furuncle, Carbuncle, Infected woundS. aureus (MSSA/MRSA), Streptococci
NecrotisingNecrotising fasciitis, Myonecrosis (clostridial/non-clostridial)Polymicrobial (Type I) or Group A Strep / S. aureus / Clostridium (Type II)
Device-relatedIV site infection, Surgical site infection (SSI)S. aureus, CoNS, Gram-neg, Enterococcus
SpecialDiabetic foot, Animal/human bite, Burn wound, ImmunocompromisedVaries

3. Clinical Features & Diagnosis

Non-purulent Cellulitis/Erysipelas

FeatureCellulitisErysipelas
DepthSubcutaneous tissueSuperficial dermis + lymphatics
AppearanceDiffuse, poorly demarcated erythema, warmth, swellingWell-demarcated, raised, shiny erythema
LymphaticsNot prominentLymphangitic streaking
SystemicVariableOften high fever, rigors
Common siteLower legFace, lower leg

Purulent (Abscess)

  • Fluctuant, tender mass with surrounding erythema
  • Incision & drainage = PRIMARY treatment; antibiotics adjunct

Necrotising Fasciitis — SURGICAL EMERGENCY

Clinical ClueSignificance
Pain out of proportion to examEarly hallmark
Rapid progression (hours)
Skin changes: erythema → dusky → bullae → necrosis
Crepitus (gas in tissues)Clostridial / polymicrobial
Systemic toxicity: sepsis, delirium, hypotension
LRINEC score ≥6High probability (see below)

LRINEC Score (Laboratory Risk Indicator for Necrotising Fasciitis)

ParameterPoints
CRP >150 mg/L4
WCC >25 ×10⁹/L2
WCC <15 ×10⁹/L1
Hb <11 g/dL1
Na <135 mmol/L2
Creatinine >1.6 mg/dL (141 μmol/L)2
Glucose >10 mmol/L (180 mg/dL)1
ScoreProbability
≥6High (>75%) — Urgent surgical exploration
4–5Moderate
≤3Low
[!critical]
LRINEC ≥6 = URGENT SURGICAL EXPLORATION. Do NOT delay for MRI/CT if clinical suspicion high. Time to debridement = survival.

4. Empirical Antibiotic Therapy

Non-purulent Cellulitis/Erysipelas (β-haemolytic Strep dominant)

SeverityRegimenDuration
Mild (afebrile, no sepsis, small area)Flucloxacillin 500mg PO 6h OR Cephalexin 500mg PO 6h OR Cefazolin 1g IV 8h (if IV needed)5–7 days
Moderate (febrile, spreading, comorbidities)Cefazolin 2g IV 8h OR Ceftriaxone 2g IV 24h OR Penicillin G 1.2MU IV 4–6h7–10 days (IV→oral at stability)
Severe (sepsis, rapid progression, immunocompromised)Ceftriaxone 2g IV 24h + Flucloxacillin 2g IV 6h (covers strep + staph)10–14 days
Penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis)Clindamycin 600mg IV/PO 6h OR Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h7–10 days

Purulent SSTI (Abscess, MSSA/MRSA)

StepAction
1. Incision & Drainage (I&D)PRIMARY treatment — adequate drainage, culture purulent material
2. AntibioticsAdjunct if: severe/extensive, rapid progression, comorbidities, immunocompromised, failed I&D alone, SIRS/sepsis, extremes of age
Mild/Moderate (post-I&D, no systemic signs)Often NO antibiotics needed if adequate drainage
If Antibiotics IndicatedRegimenDuration
Low MRSA risk (community, no risk factors)Flucloxacillin 500mg PO 6h OR Cefazolin 1g IV 8h5–7 days
High MRSA risk (known MRSA, prior MRSA, healthcare, incarceration, IVDU, sports teams)Doxycycline 100mg PO 12h OR TMP-SMX 160/800mg PO 12h OR Clindamycin 300mg PO 6h (if susceptible)5–7 days
Severe/Sepsis (IV needed)Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h (trough 15–20)7–10 days
[!key]
Abscess: I&D PRIMARY; antibiotics only if systemic signs/comorbidities. MRSA: Doxy/TMP-SMX/Clinda oral; Vancomycin IV.

Necrotising Fasciitis

TypePathogensEmpirical Regimen
Type I (Polymicrobial)Gram-neg + Anaerobes + Enterococcus ± StrepPip-tazo 4.5g IV 6h OR Meropenem 1g IV 8h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h + Clindamycin 600mg IV 6h (toxin suppression)
Type II (Monomicrobial)Group A Strep OR S. aureus (MSSA/MRSA) OR ClostridiumCeftriaxone 2g IV 24h + Flucloxacillin 2g IV 6h + Clindamycin 600mg IV 6h (if Strep/Staph) OR Pip-tazo + Vancomycin + Clindamycin (if uncertain)
Clostridial myonecrosis (gas gangrene)C. perfringens, C. septicumPenicillin G 4MU IV 4h + Clindamycin 600mg IV 6h + URGENT SURGERY
[!critical]
Necrotising fasciitis: CLINDAMYCIN ESSENTIAL (protein synthesis inhibitor → toxin suppression). Surgery = definitive; antibiotics adjunct. Multiple debridements often needed.

Diabetic Foot Infection — IDSA/IWGDF PEDIS Classification

GradeDefinition
1No infection
2 (Mild)Superficial (≥2 signs inflammation: erythema, warmth, swelling, pain, purulent), <2cm, no systemic signs
3 (Moderate)Deeper (abscess, osteomyelitis, septic arthritis, fasciitis) OR >2cm OR systemic signs (fever, leukocytosis) but stable
4 (Severe)Systemic sepsis (hypotension, confusion, tachycardia, tachypnoea, fever/hypothermia)
SeverityPathogensEmpirical Antibiotics
Mild (Grade 2)MSSA, β-haemolytic Strep (usually monomicrobial)Flucloxacillin 500mg PO 6h OR Cephalexin 500mg PO 6h OR Clindamycin 300mg PO 6h
Moderate (Grade 3)Polymicrobial: MSSA/MRSA, Strep, Gram-neg (E. coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas), AnaerobesPip-tazo 4.5g IV 6h OR Ceftriaxone 2g IV 24h + Metronidazole 500mg IV 8h +/− Vancomycin (if MRSA risk)
Severe (Grade 4)Polymicrobial MDRMeropenem 1g IV 8h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h OR Pip-tazo + Vancomycin
[!tip]
Osteomyelitis diagnosis: Probe-to-bone test (positive = high likelihood); MRI gold standard; plain X-ray changes late.
Offloading (total contact cast) + Vascular assessment (ABI, TBI, referral) = ESSENTIAL non-antibiotic management.
Duration: Mild 1–2w; Moderate 2–4w; Severe 4–6w; Osteomyelitis 6w (if no resection) or 2w post-resection.

5. IV to Oral Switch Criteria

CriteriaThreshold
Afebrile≥24–48h
Haemodynamically stableNo vasopressors, HR <100, SBP >90
Improving clinical signsDecreasing erythema, pain, swelling
Ability to tolerate oralNo nausea/vomiting/ileus
Available oral agentWith appropriate spectrum

6. Treatment Duration

SyndromeDuration
Non-purulent cellulitis5–7 days (extend to 10–14d if slow response)
Abscess (post-I&D, antibiotics indicated)5–7 days
Necrotising fasciitis10–14 days post-debridement (adjust per clinical course)
Diabetic foot (mild)1–2 weeks
Diabetic foot (moderate)2–4 weeks
Diabetic foot (severe, osteomyelitis)4–6 weeks (6w if no resection; 2w post-resection)

7. Special Situations

ScenarioKey Points
Animal/Human biteAmoxicillin-clavulanate 625mg PO 8h (covers Pasteurella, Capnocytophaga, Eikenella, anaerobes)
Burn wound infectionPseudomonas, Staph, Strep; topical + systemic; excision + graft
Surgical site infection (SSI)Superficial: open, drain; Deep/Organ space: re-explore, drain
ImmunocompromisedBroader coverage (Pseudomonas, fungi, Nocardia, mycobacteria); MRI for depth
IVDU (injection site)S. aureus (MSSA/MRSA), Strep, Gram-neg; abscess → I&D + antibiotics
Water exposure (marine)Vibrio vulnificus (severe, necrotising); Aeromonas, Mycobacterium marinum
Necrotising fasciitis — ClostridialPenicillin + Clindamycin + Surgery; C. septicum → occult colonic malignancy (colonoscopy)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Non-purulent (cellulitis/erysipelas): β-haemolytic Strep → Penicillin/Cefazolin/Ceftriaxone
  • Purulent (abscess): S. aureus → I&D PRIMARY; antibiotics adjunct if systemic/comorbidities
  • MRSA risk factors: known MRSA, healthcare, incarceration, IVDU, sports teams → Doxy/TMP-SMX/Clinda oral; Vanco IV
  • Necrotising fasciitis: SURGICAL EMERGENCY; LRINEC ≥6 → urgent exploration; CLINDAMYCIN essential (toxin suppression)
  • Type I = polymicrobial (Pip-tazo/Mero + Vanco + Clinda); Type II = Group A Strep/Staph/Clostridium
  • Diabetic foot: PEDIS grades 1–4; Mild: Fluclox/Cephalexin; Moderate: Pip-tazo/Ceftriaxone+Metro ± Vanco; Severe: Mero + Vanco
  • Osteomyelitis: Probe-to-bone; MRI gold standard; offloading + vascular assessment essential
  • Duration: Cellulitis 5–7d; Abscess 5–7d; Necrotising 10–14d; Diabetic foot mild 1–2w, mod 2–4w, severe/osteo 4–6w
  • Bites: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (Pasteurella, Eikenella, anaerobes)
  • Clostridial myonecrosis: Penicillin + Clindamycin + Surgery; C. septicum → colonoscopy

9. Common Viva Questions

  1. Difference between cellulitis and erysipelas? Cellulitis = deeper, poorly demarcated; Erysipelas = superficial, well-demarcated raised border, lymphangitic streaking.
  2. Primary treatment of skin abscess? Incision & drainage. Antibiotics adjunct only if systemic signs/comorbidities.
  3. When do you suspect necrotising fasciitis? Pain out of proportion, rapid progression, skin changes (dusky/bullae), crepitus, sepsis, LRINEC ≥6.
  4. LRINEC score components? CRP>150 (4), WCC>25 (2), WCC<15 (1), Hb<11 (1), Na<135 (2), Cr>1.6 (2), Glucose>10 (1). ≥6 = high probability.
  5. Empirical antibiotics for necrotising fasciitis? Type I: Pip-tazo/Mero + Vanco + Clinda; Type II: Ceftriaxone + Fluclox + Clinda; Clostridial: Pen + Clinda.
  6. Why is clindamycin essential in necrotising fasciitis? Protein synthesis inhibitor → suppresses toxin production (streptococcal/staphylococcal).
  7. Diabetic foot infection classification (PEDIS)? Grade 1: none; 2: mild superficial <2cm; 3: moderate deep/>2cm/systemic stable; 4: severe sepsis.
  8. Mild diabetic foot empirical antibiotics? Flucloxacillin or Cephalexin (covers MSSA, Strep).
  9. Osteomyelitis diagnosis in diabetic foot? Probe-to-bone test (clinical); MRI (gold standard); X-ray late.
  10. Animal bite prophylaxis? Amoxicillin-clavulanate (covers Pasteurella, Capnocytophaga, Eikenella, anaerobes).

10. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Antibiotics primary for abscessI&D PRIMARY; antibiotics adjunct
Flucloxacillin covers Strep wellFlucloxacillin = anti-staph; poor Strep coverage. Cellulitis/erysipelas = Strep dominant → Penicillin/Cefazolin/Ceftriaxone
MRSA coverage for all purulentOnly if risk factors present
LRINEC ≥6 = diagnose NFLRINEC ≥6 = high probability; clinical suspicion + urgent surgery = diagnosis
MRI before surgery for NFClinical suspicion + LRINEC ≥6 = URGENT SURGERY; do NOT delay for imaging
Diabetic foot = always osteomyelitisProbe-to-bone + MRI for diagnosis; not all ulcers have osteomyelitis
Offloading optional in diabetic footOffloading (total contact cast) ESSENTIAL
Clindamycin for all cellulitisClindamycin = alternative for penicillin allergy; 1st line = Penicillin/Cefazolin/Ceftriaxone
Necrotising fasciitis = always polymicrobialType II = monomicrobial (Group A Strep, S. aureus, Clostridium)
C. septicum = only wound infectionC. septicum bacteraemia/myonecrosis → OCCULT COLONIC MALIGNANCY (colonoscopy)

11. Mnemonics

  • CELLULITIS vs ERYSIPELAS: Cellulitis = Confusing borders, Common leg; Erysipelas = Exquisite borders, Elevated, Enter lymphatics
  • ABSCESS: Incision & Drainage = PRIMARY; Abx = ADJUNCT
  • NF CLINICAL: Pain out of proportion, Rapid progression, Skin changes (dusky/bullae), Crepitus, Sepsis
  • LRINEC: CRP>150 (4), WCC>25 (2)/<15 (1), Hb<11 (1), Na<135 (2), Cr>1.6 (2), Glucose>10 (1) → ≥6 = HIGH
  • NF TYPES: Type I = PolyMicrobial (Pip-tazo/Mero); Type II = Mono (Strep/Staph/Clostridial)
  • CLINDA: Toxin Suppression (protein synthesis inhibitor) = ESSENTIAL in NF
  • PEDIS: Grade 1=None, 2=Mild (<2cm superficial), 3=Mod (deep/>2cm/systemic stable), 4=Severe (sepsis)
  • DF MILD: Fluclox/Cepha (Staph/Strep); MOD: Pip-tazo/Ceftriaxone+Metro ± Vanco; SEV: Mero+Vanco
  • OSTEOMYELITIS: Probe-to-Bone; MRI gold standard
  • BITES: Amoxicillin-Clavulanate (Pasteurella, Eikenella, Capno, anaerobes)

...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)

Non-purulent Cellulitis/Erysipelas (β-haemolytic Strep dominant)
Purulent SSTI (Abscess, MSSA/MRSA)

▸ 14.1.45 Soil Transmitted Helminths Strongyloides
[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Parasites (Helminths) → Soil-Transmitted Helminths
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Ascariasis, Hookworm, Trichuriasis, Strongyloidiasis, albendazole/mebendazole, ivermectin, hyperinfection syndrome, eosinophilia, mass drug administration

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Identify Four Main STH: Ascaris lumbricoides, Necator americanus/Ancylostoma duodenale (Hookworm), Trichuris trichiura (Whipworm), Strongyloides stercoralis
  • [ ] Recognise Clinical Presentations: Intestinal (Obstruction, Malnutrition), Pulmonary (Löffler's Syndrome), Cutaneous (Larva Migrans), Hyperinfection (Strongyloides)
  • [ ] Diagnose: Stool Microscopy (Ova/Parasites), Larva Detection (Strongyloides), Serology/PCR (Strongyloides), Eosinophilia (Marked)
  • [ ] Manage: Albendazole/Mebendazole (STH), Ivermectin (Strongyloides), Mass Drug Administration (MDA) Programs
  • [ ] Recognise Strongyloides Hyperinfection: Immunocompromised, Steroids, HTLV-1, Disseminated, High Mortality
  • [ ] Apply MDA: Albendazole 400mg / Mebendazole 500mg (Annual/Bi-Annual), Ivermectin (Strongyloides Areas)

2. 📖 Overview of Soil-Transmitted Helminths (STH)

ParasiteCommon NameSize (Adult)LocationEgg/Cyst in Stool
Ascaris lumbricoidesRoundworm15-35 cmSmall IntestineFertilised Egg (Mammillated) / Unfertilised
Necator americanusNew World Hookworm10 mmDuodenum/Proximal JejuneThin-Shelled Oval Egg (4-Cell Stage)
Ancylostoma duodenaleOld World Hookworm10-15 mmDuodenumThin-Shelled Oval Egg (4-8 Cell Stage)
Trichuris trichiuraWhipworm3-5 cmCaecum/ColonBarrel-Shaped (Lemon) Egg (Bipolar Plugs)
Strongyloides stercoralisThreadworm2 mmDuodenum/Proximal JejunumRhabditiform Larva (Not Egg) in Stool
HelminthTransmissionPrimary SiteKey Clinical Feature
AscarisIngestion of Embryonated EggsSmall IntestineIntestinal Obstruction, Löffler's Syndrome (Pulmonary)
Hookworm (Necator/Ancylostoma)Skin Penetration (Filariform Larva)DuodenumIron Deficiency Anaemia (Blood Loss)
TrichurisIngestion of Embryonated EggsCaecum/ColonDysentery, Rectal Prolapse (Heavy Infection), Trichuris Dysentery Syndrome
StrongyloidesSkin Penetration (Filariform Larva) + AutoinfectionDuodenumAutoinfection, Hyperinfection (Immunocompromised), Larva Currens

3. 📖 Ascariasis (Ascaris lumbricoides)

Life Cycle & Pathogenesis

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Ingestion of Embryonated Eggs
E
H
↓
Hatch in Duodenum: Larvae
Trachea → Swallowed → Small Intestine
[Embryonation in Soil (2-3 Weeks)] --> A
↓
Penetrate Intestinal Wall → Portal Circulation
Adult Worms Mature (2-3 Months)] --> H[Eggs in Stool → Environment
↓
Liver → Heart → Lungs] --> E[Löffler's Syndrome: Cough, Wheeze, Eosinophilia

Clinical Features

PhaseFeatures
Pulmonary (Löffler's Syndrome)Dry Cough, Wheeze, Fever, Eosinophilia, Transient Pulmonary Infiltrates (Migratory)
Intestinal (Adult Worms)Abdominal Pain, Malnutrition, Vitamin A Deficiency, Intestinal Obstruction (Worm Bolus), Biliary/Pancreatic Duct Obstruction, Appendicitis
ComplicationsIntestinal Obstruction (Children, Worm Bolus), Biliary Ascariasis (Cholangitis, Pancreatitis), Peritonitis (Perforation)

Diagnosis & Treatment

TestFinding
Stool MicroscopyFertilised Egg (Mammillated), Unfertilised Egg (Longer, Narrow)
EosinophiliaMarked (Pulmonary Phase > Intestinal)
Imaging (US/CT)Worms in Intestine/Biliary Tree, Obstruction
TreatmentDoseDuration
Albendazole400mg Single DoseSingle Dose
Mebendazole500mg Single Dose (or 100mg BD × 3 Days)Single / 3 Days
Pyrantel Pamoate10mg/kg (Max 1g) Single DoseSingle Dose
[!tip] Ascariasis = Löffler's Syndrome (Pulmonary) + Intestinal Obstruction. Albendazole 400mg Single Dose = 1st Line.

4. 📖 Hookworm Infection (Ancylostomiasis/Necatoriasis)

Pathogenesis

FeatureAncylostoma duodenale (Old World)Necator americanus (New World)
SiteDuodenumDuodenum/Proximal Jejunum
EntrySkin Penetration + Oral IngestionSkin Penetration Only
AnticoagulantPotentLess Potent
Blood Loss/Worm/Day0.2-0.5 mL0.03-0.05 mL
Egg in Stool4-8 Cell Stage4-Cell Stage

Clinical Features

PhaseFeatures
Cutaneous (Entry)Ground Itch (Pruritic Papules at Entry Site — Feet/Hands)
Pulmonary (Larval Migration)Cough, Wheeze, Eosinophilia (Löffler-like)
Intestinal (Chronic)Iron Deficiency Anaemia (Microcytic Hypochromic), Protein-Losing Enteropathy, Protein Malnutrition, Pica (Geophagia)

Diagnosis & Treatment

TestFinding
Stool MicroscopyThin-Shelled Oval Eggs (4-Cell Necator, 4-8 Cell Ancylostoma)
CBCMicrocytic Hypochromic Anaemia, Eosinophilia
Iron StudiesLow Ferritin, Low Serum Iron, High TIBC
TreatmentDose
Albendazole 400mg Single DosePreferred
Mebendazole 500mg Single DoseAlternative
Pyrantel Pamoate 10mg/kgAlternative
Iron SupplementationFerrous Sulphate 200mg TDS × 3 Months
[!tip] Hookworm = Iron Deficiency Anaemia + Eosinophilia + Ground Itch. Albendazole 400mg Single Dose = 1st Line.

5. 📖 Trichuriasis (Whipworm)

Clinical Features

IntensityFeatures
LightAsymptomatic
ModerateAbdominal Pain, Diarrhoea, Mucus/Blood in Stool, Growth Retardation
Heavy (Trichuris Dysentery Syndrome)Chronic Mucus/Bloody Diarrhoea, Rectal Prolapse (Tenesmus), Anaemia, Protein-Losing Enteropathy, Growth Failure
ComplicationsRectal Prolapse, Appendicitis, Severe Anaemia, Growth Failure

Diagnosis & Treatment

TestFinding
Stool MicroscopyBarrel-Shaped (Lemon) Egg with Bipolar Plugs (Trichuris)
EosinophiliaMild-Moderate
TreatmentDoseDuration
Albendazole 400mg Single DoseSingle DoseLow Cure Rate (Repeat in 2-4 Weeks)
Mebendazole 500mg Single Dose (or 100mg BD × 3 Days)1-3 DaysBetter Cure Rate
Combination (Albendazole + Ivermectin)Best for Heavy InfectionsSequential/Concurrent

6. 📖 Strongyloidiasis (Strongyloides stercoralis) — Unique: Autoinfection

Unique Features

FeatureSignificance
AutoinfectionRhabditiform → Filariform in Gut → Re-invasion (Perianal/Venous) → Lifelong Infection if Untreated
HyperinfectionImmunocompromised (Steroids, HTLV-1, Transplant, HTLV-1) → Massive Larval Burden → Disseminated Disease, High Mortality (80-90%)
Larva CurrensUrticarial, Serpiginous, Rapidly Moving (cm/min) — Pathognomonic (Perianal/Buttocks/Thighs)
Hyperinfection SyndromeImmunosuppressed → Massive Larval Burden → Sepsis, ARDS, Meningitis, Septicaemia (Gram -ve from Gut Flora)

Clinical Forms

FormFeatures
Asymptomatic / ChronicIntermittent Abdominal Pain, Diarrhoea, Larva Currens, Eosinophilia
Acute PulmonaryLöffler-like Syndrome (Cough, Wheeze, Eosinophilia, Infiltrates)
Chronic IntestinalIntermittent Diarrhoea, Abdominal Pain, Weight Loss
Hyperinfection / DisseminatedImmunocompromised, Sepsis (Gram -ve), Meningitis (Enteric Flora), ARDS, DIC, High Mortality (80-90%)

7. 🔬 Diagnosis — Strongyloides

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected Strongyloidiasis: Eosinophilia + Diarrhoea + Travel/Immunocompromised
↓
**Stool Microscopy (3 Samples)**
↓
**Larvae Seen?**
↓
**Rhabditiform Larvae** (Not Eggs) → **Diagnosis**
**Specialised Tests**
↓
**Baermann Funnel / Harada-Mori** (Larval Culture)
**Serology (IgG ELISA)** — **High Sens/Spec**
**PCR (Stool/Sputum/Blood)** — **High Sens/Spec**
**Duodenal Aspirate/Biopsy** — Larvae in Mucosa
TestSensitivitySpecificityRole
Stool Microscopy (x3)30-50% (Intermittent Shedding)HighRoutine, Low Sens
Larval Culture (Harada-Mori)80-90%HighIncreased Sensitivity
Serology (IgG ELISA)90-95%90-95%Screening / Confirmatory
PCR (Stool/Serum)>95%>95%Gold Standard (Increasing Use)
Duodenal BiopsyHighHighIf GI Symptoms + Negative Stool
[!tip] Strongyloides = Rhabditiform Larva in Stool (Not Egg) + Autoinfection + Hyperinfection Risk. Serology = Best Screening Test. Ivermectin = Drug of Choice.

8. 💊 Treatment — Strongyloides

Scenario1st LineAlternativeDuration
UncomplicatedIvermectin 200µg/kg PO Single Dose (Repeat Day 14)Albendazole 400mg BD × 3 Days1-2 Doses
Strongyloidiasis (Chronic)Ivermectin 200µg/kg Day 1 & 14Albendazole 400mg BD × 3-7 DaysExtended
Hyperinfection / DisseminatedIvermectin 200µg/kg/day PO/NG (Daily Until Parasite Clearance)Albendazole 400mg BD IV/PO + Subcutaneous Ivermectin (If GI Absorption Impaired)Until 2 Weeks Parasite-Free
Immunocompromised (Prophylaxis)Ivermectin 200µg/kg Monthly—Duration of Immunosuppression
[!warning] Hyperinfection = Medical Emergency. Ivermectin Daily Until Parasite Clearance. Broad-Spectrum ABX for Gram-Negative Sepsis (Gut Translocation). Meningitis Risk → Cover CNS.
[!warning] Corticosteroids = Major Trigger for Hyperinfection. Screen Before Immunosuppression (Serology/PCR).

9. 🛡️ Mass Drug Administration (MDA) — WHO Strategy

Target PopulationDrugDoseFrequency
Pre-School Children (1-4y)Albendazole 400mg (or Mebendazole 500mg)Single DoseAnnual (If Prevalence >20%) / Bi-Annual (>50%)
School-Age Children (5-14y)Albendazole 400mg (or Mebendazole 500mg)Single DoseAnnual / Bi-Annual
Women of Reproductive AgeAlbendazole 400mgSingle DoseAnnual (In Endemic Areas)
Strongyloides EndemicIvermectin 200µg/kgSingle DoseAnnual/Bi-Annual
[!warning] Pregnancy: Avoid 1st Trimester. Albendazole/Mebendazole Category C. Ivermectin: Avoid in Pregnancy.

10. 💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
AscarisLöffler's Syndrome (Pulmonary), Intestinal Obstruction, Albendazole 400mg Single Dose
HookwormIron Deficiency Anaemia, Ground Itch, Eosinophilia, Albendazole 400mg Single Dose
TrichurisTrichuris Dysentery Syndrome (Bloody Diarrhoea, Rectal Prolapse), Albendazole/Mebendazole (Low Cure Rate, Repeat)
StrongyloidesAutoinfection, Larva Currens (Pathognomonic), Hyperinfection (Immunocompromised, Steroids), Ivermectin 200µg/kg
HyperinfectionSteroids/HTLV-1/Transplant → Disseminated → Sepsis/Meningitis/ARDS, Ivermectin Daily Until Clear
EosinophiliaMarked in All STH (Except Heavy Trichuris), Strongyloides = Persistent
MDAAlbendazole 400mg / Mebendazole 500mg Annual/Bi-Annual, Ivermectin for Strongyloides
Drug of ChoiceAlbendazole 400mg (Ascaris, Hookworm, Trichuris), Ivermectin (Strongyloides)

11. ❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the drug of choice for Strongyloides stercoralis?
  • Ivermectin 200µg/kg PO Single Dose (Repeat Day 14).
  1. What is the hallmark clinical feature of Strongyloides hyperinfection?
  • Disseminated Disease in Immunocompromised → Sepsis (Gram -ve), Meningitis, ARDS, High Mortality.
  1. What is the difference between Hookworm and Strongyloides skin penetration?
  • Hookworm = Ground Itch (Pruritic Papules); Strongyloides = Larva Currens (Urticarial, Serpiginous, Rapidly Moving).
  1. Why is Strongyloides unique among helminths?
  • Autoinfection Cycle → Lifelong Infection Possible, Hyperinfection Risk in Immunocompromised.
  1. What is the drug of choice for Trichuriasis?
  • Mebendazole 500mg Single Dose (Better than Albendazole) or Albendazole + Ivermectin for Heavy Infection.
  1. What is the drug of choice for Ascariasis?
  • Albendazole 400mg Single Dose (or Mebendazole 500mg Single Dose).
  1. How do you diagnose Strongyloides if stool microscopy is negative?
  • Serology (IgG ELISA), Larval Culture (Baermann/Harada-Mori), PCR, Duodenal Biopsy.
  1. What is the management of Strongyloides Hyperinfection?
  • Ivermectin 200µg/kg Daily Until Parasite Clearance, Broad-Spectrum ABX for Gram-Negative Sepsis, Stop Steroids/Immunosuppressants.
  1. What is the drug of choice for Hookworm?
  • Albendazole 400mg Single Dose (Alternative: Mebendazole 500mg).
  1. What is the MDA strategy for STH?
  • Albendazole 400mg / Mebendazole 500mg Annual/Bi-Annual for Pre-School & School-Age Children in Endemic Areas.
  1. What is the characteristic finding in Hookworm infection?
  • Iron Deficiency Anaemia (Microcytic Hypochromic) + Ground Itch + Eosinophilia.
  1. How does Strongyloides autoinfection occur?
  • Rhabditiform Larvae → Filariform Larvae in Gut → Penetrate Colonic Mucosa/Perianal Skin → Systemic Migration → Duodenum.

12. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Hookworm vs StrongyloidesHookworm: Eggs in Stool, Ground Itch, Anaemia; Strongyloides: Larvae in Stool, Larva Currens, Autoinfection
Ascaris vs HookwormAscaris: Obstruction, Löffler's Syndrome, Large Worms; Hookworm: Anaemia, Ground Itch, Blood Loss
Trichuris vs OtherTrichuris: Whipworm, Barrel Egg, Rectal Prolapse, Dysentery Syndrome
Strongyloides AutoinfectionFilariform Larva Penetrates Colonic Mucosa/Perianal Skin → Re-enters Circulation
Hyperinfection TriggerCorticosteroids, HTLV-1, Transplant, Immunosuppression
MnemonicMeaning
"Ascaris = Löffler's + Obstruction"Ascariasis
"Hookworm = Anaemia + Ground Itch"Hookworm
"Trichuris = Whipworm + Dysentery + Rectal Prolapse"Trichuriasis
"Strongyloides = Larva Currens + Autoinfection + Hyperinfection"Strongyloides
"Ivermectin = Strongyloides Drug"Treatment
"Steroids = Strongyloides Hyperinfection Trigger"Danger

13. 🗺️ Mind Map

Soil-Transmitted Helminths
Ascaris lumbricoides
Löffler's Syndrome (Pulmonary)
Intestinal Obstruction
Albendazole 400mg Single Dose
Hookworm (Ancylostoma/Necator)
Ground Itch (Entry)

Albendazole 400mg Single Dose
Trichuris trichiura

Autoinfection (Unique!)
Larva Currens (Pathognomonic)
Hyperinfection (Immunosuppressed)
Ivermectin 200µg/kg (Drug of Choice)

MDA Strategy
Albendazole 400mg / Mebendazole 500mg
Annual/Bi-Annual
Ivermectin for Strongyloides Endemic

14. 📋 One-Page Revision Card

SOIL-TRANSMITTED HELMINTHS – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
Ascaris: Löffler's Syndrome, Obstruction, Albendazole 400mg Single Dose
Hookworm: Iron Deficiency Anaemia, Ground Itch, Albendazole 400mg
Trichuris: Dysentery Syndrome, Rectal Prolapse, Mebendazole > Albendazole
Strongyloides: Autoinfection, Larva Currens, Hyperinfection (Steroids/HTLV-1)
Strongyloides Rx: Ivermectin 200µg/kg (Single/Repeat Day 14)
Hyperinfection: Ivermectin Daily Until Clear + Broad ABX + Stop Steroids
Eosinophilia: All STH (Except Heavy Trichuris)
MDA: Albendazole 400mg / Mebendazole 500mg Annual/Bi-Annual
Egg/Larva ID: Ascaris (Mammillated), Hookworm (Thin Oval), Trichuris (Barrel+Bipolar), Strongyloides (Rhabditiform Larva)

16. 🎯 Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

LevelContent
Must KnowFour STH + Clinical Features, Egg/Larva Identification, Drug of Choice (Albendazole vs Ivermectin), Strongyloides Autoinfection/Hyperinfection, Ivermectin for Strongyloides, MDA Strategy, Eosinophilia Patterns
Should KnowLife Cycles, Pulmonary Migration Phases (Löffler's), Diagnostic Methods (Microscopy, Serology, PCR, Culture), Heavy Infection Complications, MDA Implementation Challenges, Drug Resistance, Paediatric/Pregnancy Dosing, Co-infections
Nice to KnowGenomic Epidemiology, Anthelmintic Resistance Mechanisms, Vaccine Development (Hookworm, Ascaris), Integrated Control (WASH + MDA), Economic Impact, Elimination Targets (WHO 2030), Zoonotic Ascaris (A. suum), Strongyloides in HTLV-1, Coinfection Interactions (Malaria, TB, HIV)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.46 Toxoplasmosis
[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Parasites → Toxoplasma gondii
FCPS/MRCP Focus: AIDS-defining OI (CD4<100), ring-enhancing brain lesions, congenital toxoplasmosis, Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Identify Toxoplasma gondii: obligate intracellular protozoan; definitive host = cat
  • [ ] Recognise Acute toxoplasmosis (immunocompetent): Asymptomatic (80%) or mononucleosis-like (lymphadenopathy, fever)
  • [ ] Recognise Cerebral toxoplasmosis (HIV, CD4<100): Headache, focal deficit, seizures, multiple ring-enhancing lesions
  • [ ] Recognise Ocular toxoplasmosis: Chorioretinitis (posterior pole, focal necrotising retinitis)
  • [ ] Recognise Congenital toxoplasmosis: Classic triad (chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, intracranial calcifications)
  • [ ] Diagnose: IgG/IgM serology (Sabin-Feldman dye test = gold), PCR (CSF, blood, aqueous), CT/MRI brain, fundoscopy
  • [ ] Manage: Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin × 6 weeks (acute), then ½ dose (suppression)
  • [ ] Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX when CD4<100 + IgG+ (covers both PCP and Toxo)

2. 📚 Microbiology

FeatureDetails
OrganismToxoplasma gondii (obligate intracellular apicomplexan protozoan)
Definitive hostCat (felids) — sexual reproduction in enterocytes → oocysts shed in faeces
Intermediate hostsHumans, rodents, livestock, birds — asexual reproduction in tissues (cysts)
TransmissionIngestion of oocysts (contaminated food/water/soil/cat litter), ingestion of tissue cysts (undercooked meat), transplacental, organ transplant, rare person-to-person
FormsTachyzoite (rapidly dividing, acute), Bradyzoite (slow, tissue cyst, chronic/latent)

3. 🩺 Clinical Syndromes

1. Immunocompetent Primary Infection

FeatureDetails
Asymptomatic~80%
SymptomaticMononucleosis-like: cervical lymphadenopathy (KEY), fever, malaise, sore throat, hepatosplenomegaly, atypical lymphocytes
CourseSelf-limited (weeks-months); lymphadenopathy may persist; chorioretinitis may develop later
RarePneumonitis, myocarditis, encephalitis, hepatitis

2. Cerebral Toxoplasmosis (HIV, CD4<100)

FeatureDetails
PresentationSubacute (days-weeks): headache, confusion, fever, focal neurological deficit (hemiparesis, aphasia), seizures (30%), ataxia
ImagingMultiple ring-enhancing lesions (basal ganglia, corticomedullary junction) with surrounding oedema ("eccentric target sign" pathognomonic but rare); solitary lesion possible (15%)
CSFMild pleocytosis, ↑protein, normal/low glucose; PCR (specificity >95%, sensitivity 50%)
DifferentialPrimary CNS lymphoma (solitary, periventricular, EBV+ in CSF), brain abscess, TB, PML
Therapeutic trialEmpirical anti-toxo × 2 weeks; clinical + radiological improvement at 2-3 weeks confirms diagnosis

3. Ocular Toxoplasmosis

FeatureDetails
PresentationFloaters, blurred vision, scotoma, photophobia; focal necrotising retinitis (yellow-white "headlight in fog" lesion)
LocationPosterior pole, adjacent to old chorioretinal scar (recurrent)
ComplicationsMacular scar, retinal detachment, optic neuritis, cataract

4. Congenital Toxoplasmosis

FeatureDetails
MaternalPrimary infection during pregnancy (reactivation usually not transmitted if immunocompetent)
Classic triadChorioretinitis + Hydrocephalus + Intracranial calcifications (diffuse)
OtherMicrocephaly, seizures, psychomotor retardation, hepatosplenomegaly, jaundice, thrombocytopenia
Asymptomatic at birth70-90%; develop chorioretinitis or learning disabilities later
SeverityWorse with first-trimester infection; later pregnancy = less severe fetal disease but higher transmission

5. Other

  • Lymphadenopathy (cervical, occipital) — most common presentation
  • Myocarditis, pneumonitis (immunocompromised)

4. 🔬 Diagnosis

TestSensitivity/SpecificityNotes
Sabin-Feldman dye test (IgG)Gold standard serology (reference labs only)Live tachyzoites; patient's antibodies lyse; dye doesn't penetrate
ELISA IgGHigh sensitivity/specificityStandard screening; seroprevalence 30-70% globally
ELISA IgMRecent infection (rises early, falls in months)False +ve possible; confirm with IgG avidity
IgG avidityLow avidity = recent (<3-4 months); high avidity = old (>4 months)Critical in pregnancy
PCR (CSF, blood, aqueous, BAL)High specificity; variable sensitivityCSF PCR for cerebral toxo (specificity >95%, sensitivity 50%)
Brain CT/MRIMultiple ring-enhancing lesions + oedema"Eccentric target sign" (asymmetric ring enhancement)
Brain biopsyDefinitive; rarely neededIf no response to empirical Rx → consider biopsy (PCNSL?)
FundoscopyChorioretinitis (active vs scar)All HIV with visual symptoms
AntenatalAmniocentesis + PCR (≥18w gestation)Confirms fetal infection
[!tip] Exam Pearl: HIV with multiple ring-enhancing brain lesions = treat as toxoplasmosis empirically. If no response in 2 weeks → biopsy (think PCNSL, EBV+).

5. 💊 Management

Cerebral Toxoplasmosis (HIV)

PhaseRegimenDuration
Acute (induction)Pyrimethamine 200 mg loading → 50-75 mg/day + Sulfadiazine 1-1.5 g q6h + Leucovorin (folinic acid) 10-25 mg/day≥6 weeks (until clinical/radiological response)
Suppression (secondary)Pyrimethamine 25-50 mg/day + Sulfadiazine 500 mg q6h + Leucovorin 10-25 mg/dayUntil CD4 >200 ×3-6 months on ART

Alternative (Sulfa Allergy / Intolerance)

DrugDoseNotes
Pyrimethamine + ClindamycinPyrimethamine 200mg loading → 50-75 mg/day + Clindamycin 600 mg q6h IV/POAdd Leucovorin; alternative to sulfadiazine
Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX)5 mg TMP/kg q12hLess effective than pyrimethamine regimen; option in resource-limited
Atovaquone + PyrimethamineAtovaquone 1500 mg bd + Pyrimethamine 50-75 mg/daySalvage; Leucovorin
[!critical] ALWAYS add Leucovorin (folinic acid) with pyrimethamine — prevents bone marrow suppression (folate antagonist).

Other Forms

SyndromeTreatment
Acute toxoplasmosis (immunocompetent)Usually self-limited; treat if severe: Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin × 2-4 weeks
Ocular toxoplasmosisPyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin × 4-6 weeks + Prednisolone (if vitritis/macular involvement); intravitreal clindamycin/dexamethasone for severe
Pregnancy (acute, <18w)Spiramycin 3 g/day (until amniocentesis); if fetal infection confirmed → Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin (after 1st trimester)
Pregnancy (acute, ≥18w)Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin directly
Congenital toxoplasmosisPyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin × 12 months

6. 🛡️ Prophylaxis

IndicationRegimenNotes
HIV CD4 <100 + Toxo IgG+TMP-SMX DS (160/800 mg) PO dailySame drug covers PCP (CD4<200); doubles as primary prophylaxis
Alternative (sulfa allergy)Dapsone 200 mg weekly + Pyrimethamine 75 mg weekly + Leucovorin 25 mg weeklyOR Atovaquone ± Pyrimethamine
DiscontinueCD4 >200 ×3 months on ART

7. 📋 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PathogenT. gondii; definitive host = cat; tissue cysts in meat
TransmissionOocysts (cat faeces), tissue cysts (undercooked meat), transplacental
ImmunocompetentAsymptomatic (80%) or mononucleosis-like with cervical lymphadenopathy
Cerebral (HIV CD4<100)Multiple ring-enhancing lesions + oedema; basal ganglia/corticomedullary
Empirical treatmentPyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin; response at 2 weeks = diagnostic
SteroidsONLY for significant mass effect (don't mask lymphoma diagnosis)
PregnancySpiramycin (early) → Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine (after 1st trimester)
Congenital triadChorioretinitis + Hydrocephalus + Diffuse intracranial calcifications
ProphylaxisTMP-SMX when CD4<100 + IgG+ (covers PCP and Toxo)
OcularPosterior pole retinitis adjacent to old scar; "headlight in fog"

8. ❓ Viva Questions (FCPS/MRCP)

  1. What is the definitive host for Toxoplasma gondii?
  2. A HIV+ patient with CD4 80 presents with hemiparesis and seizures. CT shows multiple ring-enhancing lesions. Next steps?
  3. How do you differentiate cerebral toxoplasmosis from primary CNS lymphoma on imaging?
  4. What is the empirical treatment for cerebral toxoplasmosis? Why add leucovorin?
  5. A pregnant woman has positive Toxo IgM and IgG. Next steps?
  6. What is the classic triad of congenital toxoplasmosis?
  7. Differentiate ocular toxoplasmosis from CMV retinitis in HIV.
  8. Why do you add steroids in toxoplasma encephalitis (and when not)?
  9. How long do you treat cerebral toxoplasmosis and when do you stop secondary prophylaxis?
  10. What is the role of PCR in toxoplasmosis diagnosis?

9. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Toxoplasma vs CMV retinitis (HIV)Toxo: Posterior pole, focal, adjacent to old scar, "headlight in fog"; CMV: Perivascular, diffuse, haemorrhagic ("pizza-pie" / "ketchup-cottage cheese")
Toxoplasma vs PCNSL (HIV)Toxo: Multiple lesions, basal ganglia, oedema, Toxo IgG+; PCNSL: Solitary (often), periventricular, EBV+ in CSF, homogeneously enhancing
Pyrimethamine vs Spiramycin in pregnancySpiramycin: Early pregnancy (prevention of transmission, no fetal treatment); Pyrimethamine+Sulfadiazine: After 1st trimester (fetal treatment, teratogenic early)
Sabin-Feldman test vs ELISASabin-Feldman: Reference (gold, live tachyzoites); ELISA IgG: Routine screening
TMP-SMX prophylaxis covers both PCP and ToxoYES — same drug (DS daily) covers both; no need to add when CD4 crosses <100 if already on for PCP

Mnemonic: "TOXOPLASMA" → Tissue cysts (meat), Oocysts (cat faeces), X-linked? No — autosomal, Obligate intracellular, Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin, AIDS (CD4<100), Sabin-Feldman, Multiple ring lesions, Ante-natal (spiramycin)

Mnemonic - Cerebral toxo Rx: "PSL" → Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin (folinic acid)

Mnemonic - Congenital triad: "CHIC" → Chorioretinitis, Hydrocephalus, Intracranial calcifications (diffuse)

10. 🗺️ Mind Map

Toxoplasma gondii
Definitive Host
Cat (felids)
Oocysts in faeces
Transmission
Oocysts (food/water/soil)
Tissue cysts (undercooked meat)
Transplacental
Organ transplant
Clinical
Immunocompetent
– Asymptomatic (80%)
– Mononucleosis-like
– Cervical lymphadenopathy
HIV (CD4<100)
– Cerebral (multiple ring-enhancing)
– Chorioretinitis
Congenital
– CHIC triad
– Chorioretinitis, Hydrocephalus, IC calcifications
Diagnosis
Serology (Sabin-Feldman, ELISA IgG/IgM)
Avidity (low = recent)
PCR (CSF, blood, aqueous)
CT/MRI (multiple ring-enhancing)
Treatment
Cerebral: Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin
Pregnancy: Spiramycin (early) → P+S+L (after 1st tri)
Congenital: P+S+L ×12mo
Alternative: Clindamycin, Atovaquone
Prophylaxis
TMP-SMX DS daily (CD4<100, IgG+)
Stop when CD4>200 ×3mo

11. 📄 One-Page Revision Card

ToxoplasmosisKey Facts
OrganismT. gondii; definitive host = cat
TransmissionOocysts (cat faeces), tissue cysts (meat), transplacental
ImmunocompetentMononucleosis-like; cervical lymphadenopathy
Cerebral (HIV CD4<100)Multiple ring-enhancing lesions; empirical Rx; response at 2w = diagnostic
TreatmentPyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin (folinic acid) × 6w (acute)
PregnancySpiramycin (early) → Pyrimethamine+Sulfadiazine (after 1st trimester)
Congenital triadChorioretinitis + Hydrocephalus + Intracranial calcifications (diffuse)
OcularPosterior pole, "headlight in fog", adjacent to old scar
ProphylaxisTMP-SMX DS daily when CD4<100 + IgG+

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.47 Travel Medicine Pre Travel Advice
[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Travel Medicine
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Pre-travel consultation, risk assessment, vaccination schedules, malaria prophylaxis, travellers' diarrhoea, altitude sickness, special populations

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Conduct Pre-Travel Consultation: Risk assessment, itinerary review, medical history, immunisation status
  • [ ] Apply Vaccination Schedules: Routine, required (Yellow Fever), recommended (Hepatitis A, Typhoid, Rabies, JE, Meningococcal)
  • [ ] Prescribe Malaria Prophylaxis: Geographic resistance patterns, drug choice (Atovaquone-Proguanil, Doxycycline, Mefloquine), duration
  • [ ] Manage Travellers' Diarrhoea: Prevention, Empirical therapy (Azithromycin, Rifaximin, Ciprofloxacin), Hydration
  • [ ] Advise on Altitude Sickness: AMS, HACE, HAPE, Acetazolamide, Gradual Ascent
  • [ ] Advise on Special Populations: Pregnancy, Children, Elderly, Immunocompromised, Chronic Disease
  • [ ] Document Travel Insurance & Medical Kit

2. 📖 Pre-Travel Consultation Framework

Consultation Timeline

Time Before TravelAction
6-8 WeeksIdeal — Vaccines (Multi-dose), Malaria Prophylaxis Start, Insurance
4-6 WeeksMinimum — Most Vaccines, Malaria Prophylaxis Start
2-4 WeeksAccelerated — Single-Dose Vaccines, Rapid Schedules
<2 WeeksLimited — Accelerated Schedules, Standby Treatment, Risk Avoidance

Structured Consultation (The "5 Ws")

ComponentKey Questions
WhoAge, Medical Conditions, Medications, Allergies, Pregnancy, Immunosuppression
WhereCountries, Regions, Urban/Rural, Altitude, Season
WhenDates, Duration, Season (Transmission Seasons)
WhatActivities (Adventure, Water, Animal Contact, Medical Tourism, Sexual)
WhyTourism, Business, VFR (Visiting Friends/Relatives), Medical Tourism, Humanitarian

3. 💉 Vaccination — Routine, Required, Recommended

Routine (Update Regardless of Travel)

VaccineScheduleNotes
Diphtheria, Tetanus, Pertussis (DTaP/Tdap)Booster Every 10 YearsTdap Once, Then Td
Measles, Mumps, Rubella (MMR)2 Doses (If Born After 1970, No Evidence of Immunity)Live — Contraindicated in Immunocompromised/Pregnancy
Varicella2 Doses (If No History/Immunity)Live
InfluenzaAnnualStrongly Recommended for All Travellers
Pneumococcal (PCV20/PPSV23)Age ≥65 / Risk Groups
COVID-19Per Current Guidelines

Required (International Health Regulations)

VaccineRequirementCertificate
Yellow FeverEndemic Countries (Africa, South America)ICVP (International Certificate of Vaccination/Prophylaxis) — Valid 10 Days Post-Vaccine, Lifelong
Polio (IPV)Endemic Countries (Afghanistan, Pakistan, Outbreak Areas)ICVP (If Stay >4 Weeks)
Meningococcal (ACWY)Saudi Arabia (Hajj/Umrah)ICVP (Quadrivalent Conjugate)

Recommended by Destination/Activity

VaccineIndicationSchedule
Hepatitis AAll Travellers to Endemic Areas2 Doses (0, 6-12 Months) — Single Dose Gives 1yr Protection
TyphoidEndemic Areas (South Asia, Africa, Latin America)Vi Polysaccharide (Single Dose, 2-3yr) OR Ty21a Oral (3 Doses, 5-7yr)
Hepatitis BLong Stay, Medical/Dental Work, Sexual Contact, Healthcare Workers3 Doses (0, 1, 6 Months) OR Accelerated (0, 1, 2, 12 Months)
RabiesHigh-Risk Activities (Caving, Animal Work), Long Stay Rural, ChildrenPre-Exposure: 3 Doses (0, 7, 21-28 Days) → Post-Exposure: 4 Doses (0, 3, 7, 14) + RIG
Japanese Encephalitis (JE)Rural Asia (Rice Paddies, Monsoon), Long Stay >1 MonthIXIARO: 2 Doses (0, 28 Days)
Meningococcal (ACWY)African Meningitis Belt (Dec-Jun), Hajj/UmrahConjugate ACWY Single Dose
Cholera (Oral)Humanitarian Workers, Outbreak Areas2 Doses (1-6 Weeks Apart)
Tick-Borne Encephalitis (TBE)Endemic Europe/Asia (Forest/Hiking, Spring-Autumn)3 Doses (0, 1-3 Months, 5-12 Months)
Yellow FeverEndemic Africa/South AmericaSingle Dose, Lifelong (ICVP Valid 10 Days Post-Vax)
[!tip] Live Vaccines (MMR, Varicella, Yellow Fever, Oral Ty21a, BCG, LAIV) Contraindicated in Immunocompromised/Pregnancy. Inactivated Safe.

4. 🦟 Malaria Prophylaxis

Geographic Resistance & Drug Choice

RegionPredominant SpeciesResistance Pattern1st Line Prophylaxis
Sub-Saharan AfricaP. falciparum (Dominant)Chloroquine Resistance Universal; Mefloquine Resistance (Border Areas)Atovaquone-Proguanil / Doxycycline / Mefloquine
South Asia (India, SE Asia)P. vivax > P. falciparumChloroquine Resistance (P. falciparum); P. vivax Chloroquine SensitiveAtovaquone-Proguanil / Doxycycline
South AmericaP. vivax > P. falciparumChloroquine Resistance (P. falciparum)Atovaquone-Proguanil / Doxycycline
Central America / CaribbeanP. vivaxChloroquine Sensitive (P. vivax)Chloroquine (If P. vivax Only) / Atovaquone-Proguanil
Middle EastP. vivaxChloroquine SensitiveChloroquine
Oceania (PNG, Solomon)P. falciparum, P. vivaxChloroquine Resistant (Both)Atovaquone-Proguanil / Doxycycline

Drug Regimens (Adults)

DrugDose (Adult)ScheduleKey Points
Atovaquone-Proguanil (Malarone)250/100mg ODStart 1-2 Days Before → Daily During → 7 Days After Return1st Line (Well Tolerated, Short Post-Travel, Paediatric Formulation)
Doxycycline100mg ODStart 1-2 Days Before → Daily During → 28 Days After ReturnCheap, Photosensitivity, Oesophagitis (Take with Food/Water, Upright)
Mefloquine (Lariam)250mg WeeklyStart 2-3 Weeks Before → Weekly During → 4 Weeks After ReturnNeuropsychiatric SE (Contraindicated: Psych History, Seizures, Cardiac Conduction)
Chloroquine300mg Base Weekly (5mg/kg)Start 1 Week Before → Weekly During → 4 Weeks AfterOnly Chloroquine-Sensitive Areas (P. vivax/ovale)
Tafenoquine (Kozenis)200mg Single DoseStart 3 Days Before → Single Dose After ReturnP. vivax/ovale Radical Cure (G6PD Test Required!)
[!warning] Mefloquine Contraindicated: Psychiatric History, Seizures, Cardiac Conduction Defects. Tafenoquine: G6PD Testing Mandatory (Haemolysis Risk). Doxycycline: Photosensitivity, Oesophagitis.

Duration & Timing

PhaseDuration
Pre-TravelAtovaquone-Proguanil/Doxycycline: 1-2 Days Before; Mefloquine: 2-3 Weeks Before; Chloroquine: 1 Week Before
During TravelDaily (Atovaquone/Doxy) / Weekly (Mefloquine/Chloroquine)
Post-TravelAtovaquone-Proguanil/Doxycycline: 7 Days; Mefloquine/Chloroquine: 4 Weeks

5. 💩 Travellers' Diarrhoea (TD)

Definition & Aetiology

SeverityDefinition
Mild1-2 Loose Stools/24h, No Distress
Moderate≥3 Loose Stools/24h + Cramps/Nausea/Fever
Severe≥6 Loose Stools/24h + Fever/Blood/Severe Dehydration
AetiologyFrequency
ETEC (Enterotoxigenic E. coli)60-80%
Campylobacter10-20%
Shigella5-10%
Salmonella5-10%
Viral (Norovirus, Rotavirus)10-20%
Parasites (Giardia, Crypto, Cyclospora)<5% (Usually >14 Days)

Management

SeverityManagement
MildOral Rehydration (ORS), Loperamide 4mg Stat → 2mg After Each Loose Stool (Max 8mg/24h), Bismuth Subsalicylate
ModerateAntibiotic + Loperamide → Azithromycin 500mg OD × 3 Days (1st Line) OR Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD × 3 Days (If Susceptible)
SevereIV Fluids, IV Antibiotics (Ceftriaxone/Azithromycin), Monitor Electrolytes
[!warning] Loperamide Contraindicated: Bloody Diarrhoea, High Fever, Suspected Shigella/Campylobacter (Risk Toxic Megacolon). Fluoroquinolone Resistance Rising (SE Asia) → Azithromycin Preferred.

6. 🏔️ Altitude Illness

Definitions & Presentation

SyndromeAltitudeFeatures
AMS (Acute Mountain Sickness)>2500mHeadache + ≥1: Anorexia, Nausea, Insomnia, Fatigue, Dizziness
HACE (High Altitude Cerebral Edema)>3000mAMS + Ataxia, Confusion, Altered Consciousness → Life-Threatening
HAPE (High Altitude Pulmonary Edema)>2500mDyspnoea at Rest, Cough (Pink Frothy Sputum), Cyanosis, Tachycardia → Life-Threatening

Prevention & Treatment

StrategyDetails
Gradual Ascent<500m/Day Sleep Altitude Gain Above 3000m, Rest Day Every 3-4 Days
Acetazolamide (Prophylaxis)125mg BD PO (Start 24h Before Ascent, Continue 2 Days at Max Altitude)
Acetazolamide (Treatment AMS)250mg BD (Until Symptoms Resolve)
Dexamethasone (HACE)8mg IV/PO Stat, Then 4mg Q6H + Immediate Descent
Nifedipine (HAPE)30mg MR BD PO + Oxygen, Descent
Oxygen / DescentImmediate Descent = Definitive Treatment for HACE/HAPE
[!warning] Descent = Definitive Treatment for HACE/HAPE. Do Not Delay. Acetazolamide = Prophylaxis + AMS Treatment.

7. 🛡️ Special Populations

PopulationKey Considerations
PregnancyLive Vaccines Contraindicated, Malaria Risk ↑ (Severe), Mefloquine 2nd/3rd Trimester Only, Doxycycline Contraindicated, TD: Azithromycin Preferred
ChildrenWeight-Based Dosing, Live Vaccines Contraindicated <12mo (MMR) / <6mo (Yellow Fever), Mefloquine <5kg Contraindicated, Doxycycline <8y Contraindicated
ElderlyComorbidities, Polypharmacy, ↑ Bleeding Risk (Anticoagulants), ↑ Fall Risk, Influenza/Pneumococcal Vaccines Critical
ImmunocompromisedLive Vaccines Contraindicated, Inactivated Safe, Higher Infection Risk, Prophylaxis (PJP, Fungal, CMV), Reduced Vaccine Response
Chronic Disease (Cardiac/Pulmonary/Renal/Diabetes)Medication Adjustments, Vaccines Priority, High Altitude Risk Assessment, Insulin Adjustment at Altitude

8. 🧳 Travel Medical Kit Essentials

CategoryItems
First AidPlasters, Dressings, Antiseptic, Steri-Strips, Safety Pins, Scissors, Tweezers
MedicationsParacetamol/Ibuprofen, Loperamide, Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS), Antihistamine, Antiemetic, Antacid, Antibiotic Standby (Azithromycin/Ciprofloxacin)
Travel-SpecificAntimalarials, Acetazolamide, Sunscreen (SPF30+), Insect Repellent (DEET 30-50%), Permethrin (Clothing), Water Purification
DocumentationVaccination Record (ICVP), Prescription Copies, Insurance Details, Emergency Contacts, Blood Group

9. 💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Consultation Timing6-8 Weeks Ideal (Vaccines, Malaria Prophylaxis); <2 Weeks = Limited
VaccinesRoutine (MMR, Tdap, Flu, Pneumo); Required: Yellow Fever (ICVP), Polio, MenACWY (Hajj); Recommended: Hep A/B, Typhoid, Rabies, JE, MenACWY, Cholera, TBE
Malaria ProphylaxisAtovaquone-Proguanil (1st Line), Doxycycline, Mefloquine; Region-Specific Resistance
Travellers' DiarrhoeaAzithromycin 500mg OD x3d (1st Line), Loperamide Adjunct, Avoid if Bloody/Fever
Altitude SicknessGradual Ascent, Acetazolamide 125mg BD Prophylaxis, HACE/HAPE = Immediate Descent + Dex/Nifedipine
Special PopulationsPregnancy: Live Vaccines No, Doxy No, Mefloquine 2nd/3rd Trimester Only
Medical KitAntimalarials, ORS, Loperamide, Azithromycin Standby, Acetazolamide, DEET, Sunscreen

10. ❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the recommended timing for a pre-travel consultation?
  • Ideally 6-8 weeks before travel; Minimum 4-6 weeks for vaccines/prophylaxis.
  1. Which vaccine is required under International Health Regulations?
  • Yellow Fever (Endemic Countries) — ICVP Valid 10 Days Post-Vaccine, Lifelong.
  1. What is the first-line malaria prophylaxis for Sub-Saharan Africa?
  • Atovaquone-Proguanil (Malarone) — Daily, Start 1-2 Days Before, Continue 7 Days After Return.
  1. Which antimalarial is contraindicated in psychiatric history?
  • Mefloquine (Risk of Neuropsychiatric Side Effects: Anxiety, Depression, Psychosis, Seizures).
  1. What is the first-line treatment for travellers' diarrhoea?
  • Azithromycin 500mg OD × 3 Days + Loperamide (Avoid if Bloody/Fever).
  1. How do you prevent altitude sickness?
  • Gradual Ascent (<500m/Day Sleep Altitude Gain >3000m), Acetazolamide 125mg BD Prophylaxis (Start 24h Before Ascent).
  1. What is the treatment for HACE?
  • Dexamethasone 8mg IV/PO Stat → 4mg Q6H + Immediate Descent + Oxygen.
  1. What is the drug of choice for malaria prophylaxis in pregnancy?
  • Mefloquine (2nd/3rd Trimester Only); Doxycycline Contraindicated; Atovaquone-Proguanil Limited Data.
  1. Which vaccine is contraindicated in immunocompromised patients?
  • Live Vaccines (MMR, Varicella, Yellow Fever, Oral Typhoid, BCG, LAIV).
  1. What is the schedule for Rabies Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis?
  • 3 Doses: Day 0, 7, 21-28 (Intradermal 0.1ml or IM 1ml).
  1. What is the first-line treatment for travellers' diarrhoea?
  • Azithromycin 500mg OD × 3 Days + Loperamide; Fluoroquinolones (Ciprofloxacin) 2nd Line (Resistance Rising).

11. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Mefloquine vs DoxycyclineMefloquine = Weekly, Neuropsychiatric SE; Doxycycline = Daily, Photosensitivity, Oesophagitis
Atovaquone-Proguanil vs DoxycyclineA-P: 1-2d Before, 7d After, Expensive; Doxy: 1-2d Before, 28d After, Cheap, Photosensitivity
Mefloquine in PregnancyContraindicated 1st Trimester; Safe 2nd/3rd Trimester
Acetazolamide Prophylaxis vs TreatmentProphylaxis: 125mg BD; Treatment: 250mg BD
HACE vs HAPEHACE = Cerebral (Ataxia, Confusion); HAPE = Pulmonary (Dyspnoea, Frothy Sputum)
Live vs Inactivated VaccinesLive: MMR, Varicella, Yellow Fever, Oral Ty21a, BCG, LAIV — Contra in Immunocompromised/Pregnant
MnemonicMeaning
"Malaria: A-P = Daily, 7d After; Doxy = Daily, 28d After; Meflo = Weekly, 4w After"Prophylaxis Duration
"Yellow Fever = ICVP = 10 Days Valid"YF Certificate
"TD = Azithro + Loperamide (No Blood/Fever)"Travellers' Diarrhoea
"HACE = Head (Brain), HAPE = Lungs"Altitude Illness
"Gradual Ascent = <500m/Day Sleep Gain >3000m"Prevention
"Pregnancy = No Live Vax, No Doxy, Meflo 2nd/3rd Tri Only"Pregnancy Restrictions

12. 🗺️ Mind Map

Travel Medicine
Pre-Travel Consult
6-8 Weeks Ideal
5 Ws: Who, Where, When, What, Why
Medical History, Immunisation Status
Vaccines
Routine: MMR, Tdap, Flu, Pneumo
Required: Yellow Fever (ICVP), Polio, MenACWY (Hajj)
Recommended: Hep A/B, Typhoid, Rabies, JE, MenACWY, Cholera, TBE, Yellow Fever
Malaria Prophylaxis
Atovaquone-Proguanil (1st Line)
Doxycycline (Cheap, Photosensitivity)
Mefloquine (Weekly, Neuropsychiatric SE)
Chloroquine (Resistant Areas Only)
Tafenoquine (Radical Cure P. vivax)
Travellers' Diarrhoea
ETEC Most Common
Azithromycin 500mg OD x3d (1st Line)
Loperamide Adjunct
Avoid if Bloody/Fever
Altitude Illness
AMS: Headache + GI/Insomnia
HACE: Ataxia, Confusion
HAPE: Dyspnoea, Frothy Sputum

Special Populations
Pregnancy: No Live Vax, No Doxy, Meflo 2nd/3rd Tri
Children: Weight-Based, Live Vax Age Limits
Immunocompromised: No Live Vax, Reduced Response
Elderly: Comorbidities, Polypharmacy
Medical Kit
Antimalarials, ORS, Loperamide, Azithromycin Standby
Acetazolamide, DEET, Sunscreen, Insurance

13. 📋 One-Page Revision Card

TRAVEL MEDICINE – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
Consult: 6-8 Weeks Ideal; 5 Ws (Who, Where, When, What, Why)
Vaccines: Routine (MMR, Tdap, Flu), Required: YF (ICVP), Polio, MenACWY (Hajj), Rec: Hep A/B, Typhoid, Rabies, JE, MenACWY, Cholera
Malaria: Atovaquone-Proguanil 1st Line (Daily, 1-2d Start, 7d After); Doxy 100mg OD (1-2d Start, 28d After); Mefloquine 250mg Weekly (2-3w Start, 4w After, Neuropsychiatric SE)
TD: Azithromycin 500mg OD x3d + Loperamide; Avoid if Bloody/Fever; Fluoroquinolone 2nd Line
Altitude: Gradual Ascent <500m/Day >3000m; AMS: Headache+GI; HACE: Ataxia/Confusion; HAPE: Dyspnoea/Frothy Sputum
Altitude Rx: Descent Definitive; Acetazolamide 125mg BD (Proph) / 250mg BD (Tx); HACE: Dex 8mg + Descent; HAPE: Nifedipine + Descent
Pregnancy: No Live Vax, No Doxy, Meflo 2nd/3rd Tri Only
Medical Kit: Antimalarials, ORS, Loperamide, Azithromycin Standby, Acetazolamide, DEET 30-50%, Sunscreen, Insurance

15. 🎯 Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

LevelContent
Must KnowPre-travel timeline (6-8 weeks), Yellow Fever ICVP, Malaria prophylaxis choices by region, TD treatment (Azithromycin + Loperamide), Altitude sickness (AMS/HACE/HAPE) prevention & treatment, Pregnancy/contraindications, Travel medical kit
Should KnowVaccine schedules (accelerated vs standard), Rabies pre-exposure (3 doses), JE/TBE indications, Malaria standby treatment, Standby antibiotics for TD, Altitude sickness pathophysiology (HIF, VEGF), Jet lag management, DVT prevention on long flights, Insurance/evacuation
Nice to KnowTravellers' diarrhoea aetiology by region, Malaria drug resistance patterns (kelch13, pfmdr1), Altitude illness genetics, Travel vaccine economics, Telemedicine for travellers, Space tourism medicine, Expedition medicine, Medical tourism risks, Climate change impact on disease distribution
