Generated for FCPS/MRCP exam preparation. Based on Davidson Medicine 24th Ed Chapter 11.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.48 Tuberculosis Pulmonary And Extrapulmonary

Related: [[Tuberculous Meningitis]], [[Lymph Node Tuberculosis]], [[Pleural Tuberculosis]], [[Abdominal Tuberculosis]], [[Genitourinary Tuberculosis]], [[Bone and Joint Tuberculosis]], [[HIV-Associated Opportunistic Infections]], [[Drug-Resistant TB]]

[!important]
TB = Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex. Pulmonary TB = most common (80%). Diagnosis: GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra (rapid, sensitivity ~85–95% smear+, 50–70% smear-), culture gold standard. Treatment: 2HRZE/4HR (6 months total) for drug-susceptible. MDR-TB: BPaLM (6 months) or 9–12 month regimen. TB/HIV: ART timing by CD4. Latent TB: treat high-risk.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Diagnose pulmonary TB: symptoms, CXR, GeneXpert, culture, LPA, sequencing
  • Diagnose extrapulmonary TB: site-specific approaches, GeneXpert on non-respiratory samples
  • Initiate standard 6-month regimen (2HRZE/4HR) with correct doses and monitoring
  • Manage drug-resistant TB (RR/MDR/XDR): BPaLM, longer regimens, monitoring
  • Manage TB/HIV coinfection: ART timing, IRIS, drug interactions
  • Diagnose and treat latent TB infection (LTBI)
  • Recognise and manage adverse drug reactions (hepatotoxicity, neuropathy, optic neuritis, hyperuricaemia)
  • Apply infection control (airborne precautions, UV, ventilation)

2. Epidemiology & Transmission

AspectDetail
Global burden~10.6M incident cases/year, 1.3M deaths (2022); leading infectious killer
TransmissionAirborne droplet nuclei (1–5μm) from sputum-positive pulmonary TB
InfectivitySmear-positive > smear-negative > extrapulmonary (non-pulmonary rarely infectious)
Risk factorsHIV (20–30×), diabetes (2–3×), malnutrition, silicosis, TNF-α inhibitors, steroids, transplant, CKD, alcohol, smoking, prisoners, healthcare workers
Latent TB (LTBI)~25% world population infected; 5–10% lifetime reactivation risk (↑ with HIV, TNF-α inhibitors)

3. Pathogenesis

  1. Inhalation → alveolar macrophages
  2. Primary infection → Ghon focus (subpleural) + hilar lymphadenopathy = primary complex
  3. Containment → caseous granuloma (latent TB) OR progression → primary progressive TB
  4. Reactivation (post-primary) → apical/posterior upper lobe / superior lower lobe (high O₂ tension)
  5. Haematogenous spread → miliary TB, extrapulmonary sites (meninges, bone, kidney, etc.)

4. Clinical Features

Pulmonary TB

SymptomFrequency
Cough >2 weeks90%+
Fever (often low-grade, evening)70–80%
Night sweats50–70%
Weight loss / anorexia70–90%
Haemoptysis20–30%
Chest pain (pleuritic)20–30%
DyspnoeaIf extensive disease/effusion

Extrapulmonary TB (EPTB) — Common Sites

SiteFeatures
Lymph nodes (cervical)Painless, matted, firm, rubbery; may suppurate (cold abscess)
PleuralExudative effusion, lymphocytic, high ADA, often paucibacillary
Meninges (TBM)Subacute, basilar exudates, cranial nerves, hydrocephalus — see TBM note
AbdominalAscites (high protein, lymphocytic), peritoneal nodules, ileocaecal mass, strictures
GenitourinarySterile pyuria, renal calculi, strictures, epididymal masses, infertility
Bone/Joint (Pott's spine)Thoracic/lumbar spine, gibbus, paraspinal abscess, kyphosis, neuro deficit
PericardialEffusion, constriction, tamponade
MiliaryDiffuse 1–3mm nodules, high fever, hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia, choroidal tubercles

5. Diagnosis

Pulmonary TB

TestSensitivitySpecificityRole
GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra (sputum)85–95% smear+; 50–70% smear->98%First-line rapid test; detects RIF resistance
Smear microscopy (ZN/fluorescence)40–60% (culture+)HighRapid, cheap; cannot distinguish MTBC/NTM
Culture (MGIT liquid)Gold standard (100%)100%DST, species ID; 2–4 weeks
LPA (Line Probe Assay)~95% on culture; ~85% on smear+ sputumHighRapid DST (RIF, INH, FQ, SLI)
Whole Genome SequencingDefinitiveDefinitiveComprehensive DST, transmission mapping
CXRVariableLowScreening, supportive; upper lobe cavitation, miliary, effusion
LAM Ag (urine)Low (CD4<100)ModerateHIV advanced disease only

Extrapulmonary TB

SiteBest Diagnostic Approach
Lymph nodeFNAC (granulomas, AFB), GeneXpert on aspirate, excisional biopsy
Pleural effusionADA >40 U/L (exudate, lymphocytic); GeneXpert on fluid (low yield); biopsy
MeningesCSF GeneXpert, AFB, culture, ADA — see TBM note
AbdominalAscitic ADA, GeneXpert, laparoscopy/biopsy, CT
GenitourinaryUrine GeneXpert ×3 (early morning), IVU/CT, biopsy
Bone/JointMRI (gold standard), CT-guided biopsy, GeneXpert on pus
PericardialEcho, pericardial fluid ADA/GeneXpert, biopsy
MiliaryCXR (miliary pattern), CXR, blood culture, liver biopsy, choroidal tubercles
[!tip]
GeneXpert on non-respiratory samples: sensitivity lower than sputum but still valuable. Always send for culture + DST. ADA supportive in lymphocytic exudates (pleural, pericardial, ascitic).

6. Standard Treatment — Drug-Susceptible TB (6 Months)

Regimen

PhaseDurationDrugsDoses (Daily)
Intensive2 monthsHRZEH 5mg/kg (4–6), R 10mg/kg (8–12), Z 25mg/kg (20–30), E 15mg/kg (15–20)
Continuation4 monthsHRSame H, R doses
[!important]
Total 6 months (2HRZE/4HR). Daily dosing preferred. Intermittent (3×/week) ONLY in continuation phase if DOT assured. Pyridoxine 25–50mg/day with INH. Ethambutol: baseline visual acuity + colour vision; monthly monitoring.

Drug Doses (Daily, Weight-Based)

DrugDose (mg/kg)Max DoseKey Toxicity
Isoniazid (H)5 (4–6)300mgPeripheral neuropathy, hepatotoxicity
Rifampicin (R)10 (8–12)600mgHepatotoxicity, orange secretions, drug interactions (CYP450 induction)
Pyrazinamide (Z)25 (20–30)2gHepatotoxicity, hyperuricaemia/gout, GI upset
Ethambutol (E)15 (15–20)1.2gOptic neuritis (colour vision, acuity)
Pyridoxine—25–50mg/dayPrevents INH neuropathy

Monitoring

ParameterSchedule
Sputum smear/cultureEnd of intensive (2m), end of treatment (6m); if +ve at 2m → extend intensive to 3m
LFTs (ALT, AST, bilirubin)Baseline, 2w, 4w, 8w, then monthly (more if abnormal)
Visual acuity + colour vision (E)Baseline, monthly
Uric acid (Z)Baseline, if symptomatic
Neuropathy screen (H)Each visit
WeightMonthly

7. Treatment Response Criteria

TimepointExpected ResponseAction if Not Met
2 monthsSmear/culture negativeExtend intensive phase to 3 months; review adherence, DST
5 monthsCulture negativeExtend continuation phase to 7 months (total 9m)
6 monthsClinical/radiological cureDeclare treatment success
[!key]
Sputum conversion at 2 months = best predictor of cure. Failure = smear/culture +ve at 5m or later.

8. Drug-Resistant TB

Definitions

TypeDefinition
RR-TBRifampicin-resistant (with or without other resistance)
MDR-TBResistant to Rifampicin + Isoniazid
Pre-XDR-TBMDR + resistant to any fluoroquinolone
XDR-TBMDR + resistant to fluoroquinolone + at least one Group A drug (bedaquiline, linezolid)

WHO 2022 Regimens (Preferred)

RegimenCompositionDurationIndication
BPaLMBedaquiline + Pretomanid + Linezolid + Moxifloxacin6 monthsPreferred for RR/MDR-TB (no FQ resistance)
BPaLBedaquiline + Pretomanid + Linezolid6 monthsMDR-TB + FQ resistance (or intolerance)
9–12 month all-oralBedaquiline + Linezolid + Levofloxacin/Moxifloxacin + Clofazimine + Cycloserine/Terizidone + Pyrazinamide + Ethambutol9–12 monthsIf BPaLM contraindicated
[!important]
BPaLM = 6 months, all-oral, high success (~90%). Linezolid 600mg/day (reduce to 300mg if toxicity); monitor CBC, neuropathy, optic neuritis. Bedaquiline: ECG monitoring (QTc). Pretomanid: teratogenic (contraception mandatory).

9. TB / HIV Co-infection

CD4 CountART Timing
<50 cells/μLWithin 2 weeks of TB treatment start
50–100 cells/μLWithin 2–8 weeks
>100 cells/μLWithin 8 weeks (can delay up to 12w if logistical)
[!important]
IRIS risk: highest with low CD4, early ART. Paradoxical TB-IRIS: worsening TB symptoms/signs 2–12w post-ART; Prednisolone 1.5mg/kg/day taper 2w if moderate-severe.
Rifampicin + ART interactions: Dolutegravir 50mg BD (double dose); Efavirenz 600mg OK; Avoid PIs (rifampicin reduces levels); Nevirapine avoid.

TB/HIV Key Points

  • TB treatment duration SAME (6 months) for drug-susceptible TB
  • Cotrimoxazole prophylaxis if CD4<350 or WHO stage 3/4
  • Pyridoxine mandatory (INH neuropathy ↑ with HIV)
  • GeneXpert sensitivity higher in HIV (higher bacillary load)

10. Latent TB Infection (LTBI)

Who to Test & Treat

High-Risk GroupsTestTreat if Positive
HIV+IGRA/TSTYes (regardless of BCG)
TNF-α inhibitor candidatesIGRA/TSTYes
Transplant candidates/recipientsIGRA/TSTYes
SilicosisIGRA/TSTYes
Close contacts of smear+ TBIGRA/TSTYes (especially <5y, HIV+)
Healthcare workers (high TB burden)Serial IGRA/TSTConsider
Prisoners, homeless, IVDUIGRA/TSTConsider

LTBI Regimens

RegimenDurationNotes
3HP (Isoniazid 15mg/kg + Rifapentine 15mg/kg) weekly ×12 weeks3 monthsPreferred (high completion, less hepatotoxicity); DOT
3HR (Isoniazid + Rifampicin daily)3 monthsAlternative
4R (Rifampicin daily)4 monthsPreferred if INH contraindicated
6H/9H (Isoniazid daily)6/9 monthsOlder regimens; 9H if high risk
[!tip]
Exclude active TB BEFORE LTBI treatment (symptom screen, CXR). IGRA preferred over TST if BCG vaccinated.

11. Adverse Drug Reactions — Major

DrugReactionManagement
IsoniazidPeripheral neuropathyPyridoxine 25–50mg/day prophylaxis; if established → pyridoxine 100mg/day
HepatotoxicitySee hepatotoxicity algorithm
Seizures (rare)Pyridoxine IV
RifampicinHepatotoxicitySee hepatotoxicity algorithm
Drug interactions (CYP450 induction)Adjust co-medications (ART, anticoagulants, contraceptives, etc.)
Flu-like syndrome, thrombocytopeniaUsually transient
PyrazinamideHepatotoxicitySee hepatotoxicity algorithm
Hyperuricaemia / goutAllopurinol if symptomatic; usually asymptomatic
GI intoleranceTake with food
EthambutolOptic neuritis (reversible if caught early)Baseline + monthly acuity/colour vision; STOP if deterioration
BedaquilineQTc prolongationECG baseline, 2w, 4w, 8w, 12w; avoid with other QTc drugs
LinezolidMyelosuppression (anaemia, thrombocytopenia)CBC weekly ×4, then 2w; hold if Hb<8, plt<50
Peripheral/optic neuropathyMonitor; reduce to 300mg/day if toxicity
ClofazimineSkin discolouration (pink-brown), GI intoleranceReversible; counseling

12. Hepatotoxicity Algorithm (ANTI-TB Drug-Induced Liver Injury - ATB-DILI)

ALT/ASTBilirubinAction

...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Epidemiology & Transmission
3. Pathogenesis
4. Clinical Features
Pulmonary TB
Extrapulmonary TB (EPTB) — Common Sites
5. Diagnosis
Pulmonary TB
Extrapulmonary TB
6. Standard Treatment — Drug-Susceptible TB (6 Months)
Regimen
Drug Doses (Daily, Weight-Based)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.49 Tuberculous Meningitis

Related: [[Tuberculosis (Pulmonary and Extrapulmonary)]], [[Acute Bacterial Meningitis]], [[Viral Encephalitis and Meningitis]], [[HIV-Associated Opportunistic Infections]]

[!important]
Tuberculous meningitis (TBM) is the most severe form of TB — mortality 20–40% even with treatment. Subacute onset (1–4 weeks), basilar exudates, cranial nerve palsies, hydrocephalus. Dexamethasone reduces mortality. GeneXpert on CSF has sensitivity ~50–80%. Treat 12 months (2HRZE/10HR).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise subacute clinical presentation: headache, fever, AMS, cranial nerve palsies
  • Interpret CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, very low glucose, high protein, opening pressure ↑
  • Use GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra on CSF (rapid, moderate sensitivity)
  • Initiate empirical anti-TB + dexamethasone immediately if high suspicion
  • Manage hydrocephalus (VP shunt), stroke (vasculitis), raised ICP
  • Understand TB/HIV co-management and IRIS in TBM

2. Definition

Tuberculous meningitis = Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection of the meninges. Subacute (days–weeks). Basilar exudates → cranial nerve palsies, hydrocephalus, vasculitis → stroke.

3. Pathogenesis

  1. Primary TB → haematogenous spread → Rich focus (subpial/ependymal granuloma in brain/spine)
  2. Rupture of Rich focus into subarachnoid space → meningeal inflammation
  3. Basilar exudate (thick, gelatinous) → entrapment of cranial nerves (III, VI, VII), vessels (vasculitis), CSF pathways (hydrocephalus)

4. Clinical Features

StageFeatures
Prodrome (1–3w)Low-grade fever, malaise, headache, personality change, irritability
Meningeal (1–2w)Severe headache, vomiting, neck stiffness, photophobia, fever
Neurological (late)Cranial nerve palsies (III → ptosis, dilated pupil; VI → diplopia; VII → facial weakness), AMS (confusion → coma), focal deficits (hemiparesis from vasculitis), seizures
ComplicationsHydrocephalus (communicating → non-communicating), stroke (MCA territory, basal ganglia), SIADH, hyponatraemia

5. Normal Values / Important Cut-offs

ParameterTBM CSFBacterial MeningitisViral Meningitis
Opening pressure↑↑ (often >30 cmH₂O)↑↑Normal/mild ↑
WBC50–500 (lymphocytes)Neutrophils ↑↑↑Lymphocytes 10–500
Protein↑↑ (100–500 mg/dL, often >200)↑↑Mild ↑
Glucose (CSF/serum)↓↓ (<0.5, often <2.2 mmol/L or <40 mg/dL)↓↓ (<0.4)Normal (>0.6)
GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra50–80% sensitivityN/AN/A
AFB smear10–20%N/AN/A
Culture50–70% (4–8 weeks)N/AN/A
ADA>10 U/L (supportive)N/AN/A
[!tip]
CSF glucose <2.2 mmol/L (40 mg/dL) or ratio <0.5 = TBM / bacterial / fungal. Lymphocytic pleocytosis + very low glucose + high protein + high pressure = classic TBM triad.

6. Approach / Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Subacute meningitis: headache 1-4w + fever + AMS/cranial nerve palsies
↓
LP if safe (contraindications: papilloedema, GCS<10, focal deficit)
↓
CSF: opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, AFB, GeneXpert, culture
↓
CT/MRI brain with contrast: basal exudates, hydrocephalus, infarcts, enhancement
↓
Empirical anti-TB + Dexamethasone IMMEDIATELY if high suspicion
↓
GeneXpert +ve → confirm; GeneXpert -ve but high suspicion → continue Rx, await culture
↓
Monitor: ICP, hydrocephalus (serial imaging), stroke, hyponatraemia, drug toxicity
↓
Duration: 12 months total (2HRZE/10HR)

7. Investigations

TestUtility
CSF GeneXpert MTB/RIF UltraRapid (2h), sensitivity 50–80%, specificity >98%; detects RIF resistance
CSF AFB smearLow sensitivity (10–20%); >1 smear increases yield
CSF culture (MGIT)Gold standard (50–70%); 2–4 weeks; allows DST
CSF LPA / WGSRapid DST if culture +ve
CSF ADA>10 U/L supportive (not diagnostic); high in lymphocytic exudates
CT/MRI brain (contrast)Basilar exudates (meningeal enhancement), hydrocephalus, infarcts (vasculitis), tuberculomas
Chest X-ray / CTActive pulmonary TB in 30–50%
IGRA / TSTSupportive; not diagnostic for active TBM
HIV testMandatory (co-infection changes Rx duration, ART timing, IRIS risk)

8. Treatment

Standard Regimen (12 Months Total)

PhaseDurationDrugsDoses
Intensive2 monthsHRZE (Rifampicin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol)R 10mg/kg, H 5mg/kg, Z 25mg/kg, E 15mg/kg
Continuation10 monthsHR (Rifampicin, Isoniazid)Same doses
[!important]
Total 12 months (not 6 months like pulmonary TB). Ethambutol essential in intensive phase (good CSF penetration). Pyridoxine 25mg/day mandatory with INH.

Adjunctive Dexamethasone — MORTALITY BENEFIT

PopulationDoseDuration
AdultsDexamethasone 0.3–0.4mg/kg/day IV (max 24mg/day)4–6 weeks taper (reduce every 1–2w)
ChildrenDexamethasone 0.6mg/kg/day IV (max 30mg/day)4–6 weeks taper
[!warning]
Start dexamethasone WITH or BEFORE first anti-TB dose. Reduces mortality by ~30%. Monitor glucose, GI bleed, psychiatric effects. Taper slowly to avoid rebound inflammation.

Drug Penetration into CSF (Inflamed Meninges)

DrugCSF PenetrationNotes
IsoniazidExcellent (100%)Core drug
PyrazinamideExcellent (100%)Core drug
EthambutolGood (50–70%)Include in intensive phase
RifampicinPoor (10–20%)High dose (10mg/kg) compensates; protein binding
StreptomycinPoorAvoid (otalgia, no CSF penetration)
Fluoroquinolones (Mfx/Lfx)Good (50–70%)MDR-TBM regimens
BedaquilinePoorNot for TBM
LinezolidGood (>80%)MDR-TBM if needed

9. HIV Co-infection

AspectManagement
ART timingDelay 8–12 weeks after starting TB Rx (IRIS risk high in TBM)
DexamethasoneStill indicated; monitor closely
Rifampicin + ARTDolutegravir 50mg BD (double dose); EFV 600mg OK; avoid PIs
IRISParadoxical worsening 2–12w post-ART; prednisolone 1.5mg/kg taper if moderate-severe

10. Complications & Management

ComplicationManagement
HydrocephalusVP shunt if symptomatic (GCS declining, papilloedema, imaging progression); EVD temporising
Vasculitis / StrokeAspirin 75–150mg/day (some evidence); anticoagulation controversial; continue anti-TB + dex
Raised ICPHead elevation, mannitol/hypertonic saline, hyperventilation (temp), dexamethasone
SIADH / HyponatraemiaFluid restriction, hypertonic saline if symptomatic; check cortisol (adrenal TB)
Cranial nerve palsiesUsually improve with treatment; III/VI most common
TuberculomasSame anti-TB regimen; surgery if mass effect/refractory
Drug-induced hepatotoxicityLFTs baseline, 2w, monthly; hold/reintroduce per guidelines

11. Red Flags / Emergencies

  • GCS declining → hydrocephalus, stroke, raised ICP → urgent imaging, VP shunt/EVD
  • Focal deficit / seizure → stroke (vasculitis) → aspirin, continue dex, imaging
  • Severe hyponatraemia (Na⁺<120) → hypertonic saline, fluid restriction, check cortisol
  • Drug-induced liver injury (ALT>5×ULN or >3×ULN+symptoms) → hold hepatotoxic drugs
  • Adrenal insufficiency (TB adrenalitis) → hydrocortisone replacement + fludrocortisone

12. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Bacterial meningitisAcute (hours–days), neutrophilic CSF, very low glucose, Gram stain +ve
Viral meningitisAcute, normal glucose, self-limiting, no cranial nerves/hydrocephalus
Fungal (Cryptococcal)Immunocompromised, ↑↑ opening pressure, India ink/CrAg +ve
Carcinomatous meningitisMalignancy known; CSF malignant cells, low glucose, high protein
Sarcoid / Behçet / autoimmuneSystemic features; CSF lymphocytic, low glucose possible; no AFB/GeneXpert
Partially treated bacterialHistory of antibiotics; mixed CSF picture

13. Special Situations

SituationAdjustment
PregnancyStandard 2HRZE/10HR safe; avoid streptomycin, fluoroquinolones, bedaquiline; pyridoxine mandatory; dexamethasone OK
Renal impairmentH, R, Z dose adjust; E contraindicated if CrCl<30; moxifloxacin/levofloxacin OK
Hepatic impairmentAvoid Z if severe; monitor LFTs weekly; R/H/E caution
MDR-TBMExpert consultation; fluoroquinolone + linezolid + clofazimine + cycloserine + bedaquiline (poor CNS) + high-dose INH; duration 18–24m
ChildrenWeight-based dosing; dexamethasone 0.6mg/kg/day; VP shunt threshold lower; monitor growth, development

14. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • TBM = subacute (1–4w), basilar exudates, cranial nerves III/VI/VII, hydrocephalus, vasculitis → stroke
  • CSF: lymphocytic, very low glucose (<2.2 mmol/L, ratio <0.5), high protein (>200), high pressure
  • GeneXpert on CSF: sensitivity 50–80%, specificity >98%, detects RIF resistance in 2h
  • Treatment: 12 months (2HRZE/10HR) — NOT 6 months
  • Dexamethasone 0.3–0.4mg/kg/day ×4–6w taper: MORTALITY BENEFIT — start WITH/BEFORE anti-TB
  • Ethambutol essential in intensive phase (good CSF penetration); pyridoxine mandatory
  • HIV: delay ART 8–12w (IRIS risk high); DTG 50mg BD with rifampicin
  • Hydrocephalus = VP shunt if symptomatic; stroke = aspirin consideration
  • MDR-TBM: prolonged regimen (18–24m), fluoroquinolone + linezolid + clofazimine + cycloserine

15. Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the duration of treatment for tuberculous meningitis? 12 months (2 months HRZE + 10 months HR).
  2. What is the role of dexamethasone in TBM? Reduces mortality by ~30%; 0.3–0.4mg/kg/day ×4–6w taper; start with/ before anti-TB.
  3. What is the CSF profile in TBM? Lymphocytic pleocytosis (50–500), very low glucose (<2.2 mmol/L, ratio <0.5), high protein (>100, often >200), high opening pressure.
  4. What is the sensitivity of GeneXpert on CSF for TBM? 50–80% (moderate); specificity >98%.
  5. When do you start ART in HIV/TBM co-infection? Delay 8–12 weeks after starting TB treatment (high IRIS risk).
  6. Which anti-TB drugs have good CSF penetration? INH, PZA, EMB, fluoroquinolones, linezolid. Rifampicin has poor penetration (compensated by high dose).
  7. How do you manage hydrocephalus in TBM? VP shunt if symptomatic (declining GCS, papilloedema, progressive imaging).

16. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
TBM duration = 6 months like pulmonary TB12 months (2HRZE/10HR) — continuation phase 10 months
Dexamethasone optional in TBMStrong mortality benefit — standard of care
GeneXpert negative = rule out TBMSensitivity only 50–80% — clinical suspicion + culture if -ve
Rifampicin penetrates CSF wellPoor (10–20%) — high dose 10mg/kg compensates
Streptomycin for TBMNo CSF penetration + ototoxicity — contraindicated
ART timing same as pulmonary TB/HIVTBM: delay 8–12w (pulmonary: CD4<50 → 2w)
VP shunt never neededSymptomatic hydrocephalus → VP shunt (EVD temporising)
Aspirin contraindicated in TBM strokeAspirin 75–150mg/day may reduce infarcts (some evidence)

17. Mnemonics

  • TBM CSF: Lymphocytes, Over 200 protein, Water (glucose) low, Pressure high = LOW P
  • TBM RX: 12 MONTHS = 2 HRZE / 10 HR (not 6 months)
  • DEX TBM: 0.3–0.4mg/kg/day IV ×4–6w taper — START WITH ANTI-TB
  • TBM COMPLICATIONS: Hydrocephalus (VP shunt), Stroke (vasculitis, aspirin), Cranial nerves (III, VI, VII), SIADH, ICPs raised
  • HIV TBM ART: DELAY 8–12 WEEKS (IRIS risk)

18. Mind Map

Tuberculous Meningitis
Pathogenesis
Haematogenous spread
Rich focus rupture
Basilar exudate
Clinical
Subacute 1-4w
Headache, fever, AMS
Cranial nerves (III, VI, VII)
Hydrocephalus
Stroke (vasculitis)
CSF
Lymphocytes 50-500
*...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)*
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.50 Typhoid And Paratyphoid Fever

Related: [[Fever in Returned Traveller and FUO]], [[Infective Diarrhoea and Food Poisoning]], [[Travel Medicine and Pre-Travel Advice]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Healthcare-Associated Infections (CLABSI, CAUTI, SSI, VAP)]]

[!important]
Enteric Fever = Typhoid (S. Typhi) + Paratyphoid A/B/C (S. Paratyphi). Faecal-oral transmission; chronic carriers (gallbladder). Step-ladder fever, relative bradycardia, rose spots, hepatosplenomegaly. Blood culture = gold standard (first week); Widal test = supportive. Ceftriaxone = 1st-line; Azithromycin for resistant/children; Ciprofloxacin (resistance common). Carriers: ciprofloxacin ×4–6w + cholecystectomy if persistent.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise clinical features of enteric fever (step-ladder fever, relative bradycardia, rose spots)
  • Apply diagnostic approach (blood culture, Widal test, PCR)
  • Select empirical therapy by resistance patterns and patient factors
  • Manage complications (intestinal perforation, haemorrhage, relapse, carrier state)
  • Apply vaccination and public health measures

2. Epidemiology & Transmission

AspectDetails
AgentsS. Typhi (typhoid); S. Paratyphi A, B, C (paratyphoid) — S. Paratyphi A most common paratyphoid
TransmissionFaecal-oral (contaminated food/water, chronic carriers); shellfish, raw vegetables, milk
ReservoirHumans only (acute cases, convalescent carriers, chronic carriers)
Incubation7–14 days (range 3–60 days); shorter for paratyphoid
RiskTravellers to endemic areas (South Asia, SE Asia, Africa, Latin America); poor sanitation; household contacts
[!tip]
Chronic carriers: 1–4% of untreated cases; gallbladder colonisation (biofilm on gallstones); shedding in stool >1 year; "Typhoid Mary" phenomenon.

3. Clinical Features

PhaseFeatures
Week 1Step-ladder fever (rising daily), headache, malaise, anorexia, relative bradycardia (pulse-temperature dissociation), cough, epistaxis, abdominal discomfort
Week 2Fever plateau (39–40°C), rose spots (faint salmon macules on trunk, 2–4mm, 10–20%), hepatosplenomegaly, abdominal distension, constipation > diarrhoea, relative bradycardia
Week 3Complications (if untreated): intestinal perforation, GI haemorrhage, encephalopathy, myocarditis, abscesses
Week 4Gradual defervescence (if survived); relapse in 10–20% (1–2 weeks after stopping antibiotics)

Key Clinical Signs

SignSignificance
Step-ladder feverTemp rises incrementally each day (classic but not always present)
Relative bradycardiaPulse not commensurate with fever (Faget's sign)
Rose spotsFaint salmon macules on trunk (pathognomonic but only 10–30%); blanch on pressure
HepatosplenomegalyCommon by week 2
Abdominal tendernessRight lower quadrant / diffuse

4. Diagnosis

TestTimingSensitivityUtility
Blood CultureWeek 1–2 (best first week)40–80% (increases with volume/repeats)Gold standard; allows AST
Bone Marrow CultureAny time80–95%Higher yield if prior antibiotics; invasive
Stool CultureWeek 2–330–40%Late; carrier detection
Urine CultureWeek 2–3LowAncillary
Widal TestWeek 2+VariableSupportive only; O ≥1:160, H ≥1:320 suggestive; false +/− common
Typhoid PCRWeek 1–280–95%Rapid; blood/stool/urine; not universally available
Typhidot (IgM/IgG)Week 1+70–90%Rapid; false +/− in endemic areas
[!key]
Blood culture = gold standard; collect 10–20mL before antibiotics. Widal = supportive only (not diagnostic alone). Bone marrow = highest yield if antibiotics given.

5. Complications

ComplicationFrequencyManagement
Intestinal Perforation1–3% (ileum, terminal ileum)Emergency laparotomy + broad antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam/meropenem)
GI Haemorrhage1–5%Blood transfusion, endoscopy, supportive; surgery if massive
Relapse10–20% (1–4w post-treatment)Repeat full course antibiotics (different class if resistance)
Chronic Carrier1–4% (if untreated/partial treatment)Ciprofloxacin 750mg BD ×4–6w + cholecystectomy if gallstones
Typhoid Encephalopathy<1%ICU support; high-dose steroids controversial; antibiotics
AbscessesRareLiver, spleen, lung, bone, brain; drainage + antibiotics

6. Treatment

Uncomplicated Enteric Fever

DrugDoseDurationNotes
Ceftriaxone2g IV/IM OD (children 50–75mg/kg)7–10 days1st-line (incl. resistant strains); IV preferred severe
Azithromycin500mg PO OD (children 15mg/kg)7 days1st-line oral (esp. children, ciprofloxacin-resistant); safe pregnancy
Ciprofloxacin500mg PO 12h (750mg if severe)7–10 daysOnly if susceptible (resistance common: South Asia >90%); avoid children/pregnancy
Cefixime20mg/kg PO 12h (max 400mg)7–14 daysAlternative oral; less reliable for resistant strains
[!tip]
Ceftriaxone = 1st-line (IV/IM); Azithromycin = 1st-line oral. Ciprofloxacin resistance >90% in South Asia — avoid empirical use there. Azithromycin preferred for children/pregnancy.

Severe/Complicated Enteric Fever

ScenarioRegimen
Severe sepsis / shockMeropenem 1g IV 8h +/− Azithromycin
Intestinal perforationEmergency laparotomy + Meropenem 1g IV 8h
Ciprofloxacin-resistantCeftriaxone 2g IV OD OR Azithromycin 500mg OD
RelapseFull course with different class (e.g., azithromycin if ceftriaxone used initially)

Chronic Carrier

RegimenDuration
Ciprofloxacin 750mg PO 12h4–6 weeks
+ CholecystectomyIf gallstones present (90% carriers have gallstones)

7. Vaccination

VaccineTypeScheduleEfficacyDuration
Typhoid Conjugate Vaccine (TCV)Vi polysaccharide-protein conjugateSingle dose IM80–85%3–5 years
Vi Polysaccharide (ViPS)Vi capsular polysaccharideSingle dose IM/SC60–70%2–3 years
Ty21a (Oral)Live attenuated (Ty21a strain)3 capsules alt days (4 total)50–70%3–5 years
[!tip]
TCV = preferred (single dose, higher efficacy, longer duration, usable <2y). Ty21a: live, contraindicated immunocompromised/pregnancy. Booster every 3 years if ongoing risk.

8. Public Health

MeasureAction
NotificationStatutory notifiable disease
Contact tracingHousehold/close contacts: stool culture ×3; prophylaxis (azithromycin) if carrier
Carrier managementExclude from food handling; ciprofloxacin ×4–6w + cholecystectomy
Water/food safetyBoiled water, cooked food, pasteurised milk
VaccinationTravellers to endemic areas; high-risk occupations

9. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Enteric fever: S. Typhi (typhoid) + S. Paratyphi A/B/C; faecal-oral; human reservoir
  • Step-ladder fever, relative bradycardia, rose spots, hepatosplenomegaly
  • Blood culture = gold standard (week 1); bone marrow = highest yield
  • Widal test = supportive only (O ≥1:160, H ≥1:320)
  • Ceftriaxone 2g IV OD = 1st-line; Azithromycin 500mg OD = 1st-line oral
  • Ciprofloxacin resistance >90% South Asia — avoid empirical
  • Complications: perforation, haemorrhage, relapse (10–20%), carrier state
  • Carrier: ciprofloxacin ×4–6w + cholecystectomy if gallstones
  • Vaccine: TCV single dose (preferred); boosters 3-yearly

10. Common Viva Questions

  1. Gold standard diagnosis of typhoid? Blood culture (first week); bone marrow if antibiotics given.
  2. Step-ladder fever pattern? Temperature rises incrementally each day during first week.
  3. Relative bradycardia (Faget's sign)? Pulse rate disproportionately low for degree of fever.
  4. Rose spots?* Faint salmon macules on trunk, 2–4mm, blanch on pressure; 10–30% of cases.
  5. First-line treatment? Ceftriaxone 2g IV OD (7–10d); Azithromycin 500mg PO OD (7d) if oral needed.
  6. Why avoid ciprofloxacin empirically in South Asia? >90% resistance.
  7. Complications of typhoid? Intestinal perforation, GI haemorrhage, relapse (10–20%), carrier state.
  8. Chronic carrier management? Ciprofloxacin 750mg BD ×4–6w + cholecystectomy if gallstones.
  9. Best typhoid vaccine? Typhoid Conjugate Vaccine (TCV) — single dose, 80–85% efficacy, from 6 months age.
  10. Relapse vs reinfection? Relapse = same strain, 1–4w post-treatment; reinfection = new exposure.

11. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Widal test = diagnosticSupportive only; not diagnostic alone
Ciprofloxacin 1st-line everywhereResistance >90% South Asia — use ceftriaxone/azithromycin
Rose spots = pathognomonicOnly 10–30%; blanch on pressure; not always present
Relapse = treatment failureRelapse = same strain 1–4w post-treatment; reinfection = new exposure
Carrier = always symptomaticAsymptomatic; excluded from food handling
Widal positive = typhoidFalse + in malaria, typhus, other infections; false - early
Cholecystectomy always needed for carrierOnly if gallstones present (90% have them); try antibiotics first
Azithromycin not for severe typhoidIV azithromycin available; ceftriaxone preferred severe
Typhoid vaccine 100% effectiveTCV 80–85%; hygiene still essential
Paratyphoid = milder typhoidSimilar severity; S. Paratyphi A most common paratyphoid

12. Mnemonics

  • TYPHOID: Typhi; Yield blood culture week 1; Paratyphi A/B/C; Human reservoir; Oral- faecal; Intestinal perforation; Drugs: Ceftriaxone/Azithromycin
  • CLINICAL: Step-ladder fever; Relative bradycardia; O spots; Plastosplenomegaly; Hepatosis; One-week blood culture; Ileal perforation; ID: blood culture
  • ROSE SPOTS: Red-Salmon; On trunk; Small (2-4mm); Ephemeral
  • TREATMENT: Ceftriaxone 1st-line IV; Azithromycin 1st-line PO; Cipro NOT empirical Asia

13. Mind Map

Enteric Fever
Agents
S. Typhi (Typhoid)
S. Paratyphi A/B/C
Transmission
Faecal-oral; Human reservoir
Chronic carriers (gallbladder)
Clinical
Step-ladder fever
Relative bradycardia
Rose spots (salmon macules)
Hepatosplenomegaly
Diagnosis
Blood culture (gold, week 1)
Bone marrow (if abx given)
Widal: O≥1:160, H≥1:320 (supportive)
PCR (rapid)
Complications
Intestinal perforation
GI haemorrhage
Relapse (10-20%)
Chronic carrier
Treatment
Ceftriaxone 2g IV OD (1st line)
Azithromycin 500mg PO OD (1st oral)
Ciprofloxacin only if susceptible
Carriers: Cipro 4-6w + cholecystectomy
Vaccines
*...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)*
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.51 Urinary Tract Infections Complicated Ca Uti Pyelonephritis

Related: [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Antimicrobial Stewardship]], [[Prosthetic Joint Infection]], [[Renal Transplant Infections]]

[!important]
UTI = significant bacteriuria + urinary symptoms. Uncomplicated cystitis: healthy non-pregnant women. Complicated UTI: structural/functional abnormality, male, pregnant, immunocompromised, catheter, recent instrumentation. CA-UTI: catheter ≥2 days + symptoms/signs. Pyelonephritis: flank pain, fever, CVA tenderness + bacteriuria. Empirical antibiotics by local resistance, severity, risk factors. Duration: cystitis 3–5d, pyelonephritis 7–14d, CA-UTI 7d (remove catheter if possible). Asymptomatic bacteriuria: treat ONLY in pregnancy, pre-urologic procedure, renal transplant (early).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify UTI: uncomplicated vs complicated, cystitis vs pyelonephritis, CA-UTI
  • Diagnose using symptoms, urinalysis, urine culture thresholds
  • Select empirical antibiotics by syndrome, local resistance, MDR risk
  • Determine appropriate treatment duration
  • Manage asymptomatic bacteriuria (when to treat vs not)
  • Recognise and manage complications: emphysematous pyelonephritis, renal abscess, sepsis
  • Apply CA-UTI prevention strategies

2. Classification

CategoryDefinition
Uncomplicated cystitisHealthy, non-pregnant, premenopausal women; no structural/functional abnormality
Complicated UTIANY of: male, pregnant, postmenopausal, immunocompromised, diabetes, structural abnormality (stones, obstruction, reflux), indwelling catheter, recent instrumentation, renal transplant, neurogenic bladder
PyelonephritisUTI + fever/flank pain/CVA tenderness (upper tract involvement)
CA-UTICatheter ≥2 days + symptoms/signs (fever, suprapubic pain, costovertebral angle tenderness, delirium) + ≥10³ CFU/mL from catheter specimen
Asymptomatic bacteriuria (ASB)≥10⁵ CFU/mL (clean-catch) or ≥10² CFU/mL (catheter) in ASYMPTOMATIC patient

3. Aetiology

SyndromeCommon Pathogens
Uncomplicated cystitisE. coli (75–90%), S. saprophyticus (5–15%), Klebsiella, Proteus
Complicated UTI / PyelonephritisE. coli (50–60%), Klebsiella, Proteus, Pseudomonas, Enterococcus, S. aureus
CA-UTIE. coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas, Enterococcus, Candida, Proteus, Morganella, Providencia (often polymicrobial)
Healthcare-associated / MDR riskESBL Enterobacteriaceae, Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter, Enterococcus (VRE), Serratia
[!tip]
Proteus → urease → struvite stones, alkaline urine. Pseudomonas → biofilm on catheters. Candida in catheterised/ICU patients — treat only if symptomatic + pyuria.

4. Diagnosis

Urinalysis

FindingSignificance
Nitrite +Specific for Enterobacteriaceae (reduces nitrate)
Leukocyte esterase +Pyuria (WBC in urine)
WBC >10/HPFPyuria
Bacteria on microscopy~10⁵ CFU/mL if ≥1 bacterium/HPF
[!warning]
Pyuria + symptoms = UTI. Pyuria alone (no symptoms) = ASB (do not treat except specific indications). Elderly: delirium may be ONLY symptom.

Urine Culture Thresholds (Significant Bacteriuria)

SpecimenThresholdNotes
Clean-catch midstream (female)≥10⁵ CFU/mL≥10² if symptomatic (high pre-test probability)
Clean-catch midstream (male)≥10⁴ CFU/mL
Straight catheter≥10² CFU/mL
Suprapubic aspiration≥10¹ CFU/mLGold standard
Indwelling catheter≥10³ CFU/mLFor CA-UTI diagnosis

5. Empirical Antibiotic Therapy

Uncomplicated Cystitis (Women)

AgentDoseDurationNotes
Nitrofurantoin100mg PO 12h (MR) or 50mg 6h3 days (MR: 5 days)1st line; avoid if CrCl <30, G6PD, pregnancy term
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX)160/800mg PO 12h3 days1st line if local E. coli resistance <20%
Fosfomycin3g PO single doseSingle doseAlternative; lower cure rate
Pivmecillinam400mg PO 8h3–5 daysAlternative (not in all countries)
Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin)Cipro 250mg 12h / Levo 250mg OD3 daysAvoid 1st line (resistance, tendinopathy, C. diff, aortic dissection risk)

Complicated UTI / Pyelonephritis (Outpatient / Mild Inpatient)

AgentDoseDuration
Ciprofloxacin500mg PO 12h (or 750mg XR OD)7 days (pyelo) / 5–7 days (complicated)
Levofloxacin750mg PO OD5–7 days
TMP-SMX160/800mg PO 12h7–14 days (if susceptible)
Cefpodoxime / Cefdinir200mg PO 12h / 300mg PO 12h7–14 days
IV → Oral switchCeftriaxone 1–2g IV 24h → oral fluoroquinolone/TMP-SMXWhen clinically stable

Complicated UTI / Pyelonephritis (Inpatient / Severe / MDR Risk)

ScenarioEmpirical RegimenDuration
No MDR risk, stableCeftriaxone 1–2g IV 24h7–14 days (IV→oral at stability)
MDR risk (prior abx, healthcare, travel, known colonisation)Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV 6h OR Meropenem 1g IV 8h OR Cefepime 2g IV 8h7–14 days
+ Enterococcus risk (catheter, instrumentation, abscess)Add Amoxicillin 2g IV 4h (if E. faecalis) OR Vancomycin (if VRE risk)
+ Pseudomonas riskAnti-pseudomonal β-lactam above
Sepsis / ShockMeropenem 1g IV 8h (or Pip-tazo) ± Vancomycin ± Aminoglycoside7–14 days

CA-UTI

StepAction
1. Remove/replace catheter if ≥2 days (send tip for culture if removed)
2. Empirical antibiotics (if symptomatic): Same as complicated UTI by severity
3. Duration7 days (if catheter removed); 10–14 days if catheter MUST remain
4. Catheter changeReplace catheter after 48–72h of antibiotics if remaining
[!key]
CA-UTI: REMOVE catheter if possible. If catheter must stay, change after 48–72h antibiotics. Duration 7d if removed, 10–14d if retained.

6. Asymptomatic Bacteriuria (ASB) — When to TREAT

PopulationTreat?
Pregnant womenYES (screen at 12–16 weeks; treat to prevent pyelonephritis)
Pre-urologic procedure with mucosal bleedingYES (pre-op)
Renal transplant (first 1–3 months)YES (some guidelines)
Neutropenic patientsControversial (some treat)
Healthy non-pregnant womenNO
Elderly / LTCF residentsNO
DiabeticsNO
Catheterised patients (chronic)NO
Spinal cord injury / neurogenic bladderNO
Pre-non-urologic surgeryNO
[!critical]
ASB treatment in non-indicated populations → ↑ antibiotic resistance, ↑ C. diff, NO clinical benefit. Pyuria in ASB does NOT indicate treatment.

7. Treatment Duration Summary

SyndromeDuration
Uncomplicated cystitis (women)3 days (Nitrofurantoin, TMP-SMX); Single dose (Fosfomycin)
Complicated cystitis5–7 days
Pyelonephritis (outpatient/mild)7 days (fluoroquinolone) / 10–14 days (β-lactam/TMP-SMX)
Pyelonephritis (severe/inpatient)10–14 days (IV→oral at stability)
CA-UTI (catheter removed)7 days
CA-UTI (catheter retained)10–14 days (change catheter at 48–72h)
Prostatitis (acute bacterial)4–6 weeks
Prostatitis (chronic bacterial)6–12 weeks

8. Complications

ComplicationFeaturesManagement
Emphysematous pyelonephritisGas in renal parenchyme/collecting system (diabetics); high mortalityEmergency nephrectomy + broad antibiotics (Pip-tazo/Meropenem)
Renal / Perinephric abscessFlank mass, persistent fever, failure to respondCT-guided drainage + antibiotics
Papillary necrosisDiabetics, sickle cell, analgesic abuse; flank pain, haematuriaSupportive, antibiotics, urology
Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritisChronic, staghorn stones, Proteus; mass mimics tumourNephrectomy
Sepsis / Septic shockSee Sepsis noteSepsis bundle + source control
Prostatitis (acute)Fever, perineal pain, dysuria, tender prostateFluoroquinolone 4–6 weeks (prostate penetration)

9. MDR Pathogens in UTI

PathogenPreferred TreatmentAlternatives
ESBL EnterobacteriaceaeMeropenem 1g IV 8h (or Ertapenem 1g IV 24h if no Pseudomonas risk)Fosfomycin IV, Ceftazidime-avibactam, Plazomicin
CRECeftazidime-avibactam, Meropenem-vaborbactam, Imipenem-relebactam, Plazomicin, PolymyxinFosfomycin, Tigecycline (poor urine levels)
MDR PseudomonasCeftolozane-tazobactam, Ceftazidime-avibactam, Imipenem-relebactam, Polymyxin + high-dose meropenemAminoglycoside + β-lactam
VRE (E. faecium)Linezolid 600mg IV/PO 12h OR Daptomycin 8–10mg/kg IV OD (NOT for urine — inactivated by surfactant)Tigecycline (poor urine levels)
Enterococcus faecalis (ampicillin-R)Vancomycin OR LinezolidDaptomycin
Candida (symptomatic + pyuria)Fluconazole 200–400mg PO/IV OD (if susceptible)Echinocandin (caspofungin/micafungin) — poor urine levels except micafungin
[!tip]
Fosfomycin IV excellent for MDR UTI (broad Gram-neg + Enterococcus). Daptomycin inactivated in urine — avoid for UTI. Tigecycline poor urine levels — avoid for UTI. Echinocandins poor urine levels (except micafungin) — fluconazole preferred for Candida UTI.

10. CA-UTI Prevention Bundle

ComponentAction
Avoid unnecessary catheterisationUse alternatives (condom catheter, intermittent catheterisation, nappies)
Aseptic insertionSterile technique, smallest appropriate catheter
Closed drainage systemMaintain unobstructed flow; no disconnections
Catheter securementPrevent traction/urethral trauma
Maintain drainage bag below bladderPrevent reflux
Daily review of necessityRemove as soon as possible (nurse-driven protocols)
Periurethral cleaningRoutine soap/water (no antiseptic benefit)
Antibiotic prophylaxis at insertionNOT routinely recommended
Catheter materialSilver-alloy or hydrogel-coated if prolonged (>7d) anticipated

11. Special Situations

SituationManagement
PregnancyScreen for ASB at 12–16w; treat positive. Cystitis: Nitrofurantoin (avoid term), Cephalexin, Fosfomycin. Pyelonephritis: IV Ceftriaxone → oral cephalexin. Avoid fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX (1st tri), Nitrofurantoin (term).
Renal transplantASB treat early (1–3m); UTI: adjust for immunosuppression interactions (fluconazole ↑ tacrolimus, TMP-SMX ↑ creatinine).
Neurogenic bladder / Spinal cordAsymptomatic bacteriuria universal — do NOT treat. Treat only if symptomatic (fever, autonomic dysreflexia, new incontinence).
ProstatitisAcute: fluoroquinolone 4–6w (prostate penetration). Chronic: fluoroquinolone/TMP-SMX 6–12w.
Urosepsis with obstructionURGENT decompression (nephrostomy/stent) + antibiotics — source control critical.

12. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • UTI classification: uncomplicated cystitis (healthy women) vs complicated (ALL others)
  • Diagnosis: symptoms + pyuria + culture (≥10⁵ clean-catch, ≥10² catheter, ≥10³ indwelling catheter)
  • Uncomplicated cystitis: Nitrofurantoin 3d (1st line), TMP-SMX 3d (if resistance <20%), Fosfomycin single dose
  • Complicated UTI/Pyelonephritis: Fluoroquinolone 7d (outpatient) or IV Ceftriaxone → oral
  • MDR risk: prior abx, healthcare, travel, colonisation → Pip-tazo/Meropenem/Cefepime
  • CA-UTI: catheter ≥2d + symptoms + ≥10³ CFU/mL → REMOVE catheter; 7d antibiotics if removed
  • Asymptomatic bacteriuria: TREAT only in pregnancy, pre-urologic procedure with bleeding, early renal transplant
  • Duration: cystitis 3d, pyelo 7–14d, CA-UTI 7d (removed) / 10–14d (retained)
  • ESBL: carbapenem (meropenem/ertapenem); CRE: newer β-lactamase inhibitors; VRE: linezolid (daptomycin fails in urine)
  • Emphysematous pyelonephritis: diabetics, gas on imaging → emergency nephrectomy + antibiotics
  • Prostatitis: fluoroquinolone 4–6w (penetration)

13. Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the urine culture threshold for CA-UTI? ≥10³ CFU/mL from indwelling catheter specimen.
  2. When do you treat asymptomatic bacteriuria? Pregnancy, pre-urologic procedure with mucosal bleeding, renal transplant (early).
  3. First-line for uncomplicated cystitis? Nitrofurantoin 100mg PO 12h ×3 days (or TMP-SMX if resistance <20%).
  4. Duration for pyelonephritis? 7 days (fluoroquinolone) or 10–14 days (β-lactam/TMP-SMX).
  5. CA-UTI management? Remove catheter if possible; antibiotics 7d if removed, 10–14d if retained; change catheter at 48–72h.
  6. ESBL UTI treatment? Carbapenem (meropenem/ertapenem).
  7. Why avoid daptomycin for VRE UTI? Inactivated by pulmonary surfactant in urine (actually — inactivated by calcium/surfactant; poor urinary penetration).
  8. Emphysematous pyelonephritis management? Emergency nephrectomy + broad antibiotics.
  9. Asymptomatic bacteriuria in elderly LTCF resident — treat? NO.
  10. Acute prostatitis treatment and duration? Fluoroquinolone 4–6 weeks (prostate penetration).

14. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Treat all asymptomatic bacteriuriaTreat ONLY: pregnancy, pre-urologic bleeding, early renal transplant
Pyuria = treatPyuria without symptoms = ASB (do not treat)
Fosfomycin 1st line for cystitisNitrofurantoin/TMP-SMX 1st line; Fosfomycin alternative (lower cure)
Fluoroquinolone 1st line for cystitisAvoid 1st line (resistance, tendinopathy, C. diff, aortic risk)

...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Classification
3. Aetiology
4. Diagnosis
Urinalysis
Urine Culture Thresholds (Significant Bacteriuria)
5. Empirical Antibiotic Therapy
Uncomplicated Cystitis (Women)
Complicated UTI / Pyelonephritis (Outpatient / Mild Inpatient)
Complicated UTI / Pyelonephritis (Inpatient / Severe / MDR Risk)
CA-UTI
6. Asymptomatic Bacteriuria (ASB) — When to TREAT
7. Treatment Duration Summary
8. Complications

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.52 Viral Encephalitis And Meningitis

Related: [[Acute Bacterial Meningitis]], [[Tuberculous Meningitis]], [[HIV-Associated Opportunistic Infections]], [[Fever and Septic Syndrome Approach]]

[!important]
Viral encephalitis = brain parenchymal inflammation (AMS, focal signs, seizures) — HSV-1 is the most common identifiable cause and the ONLY treatable one with specific antiviral (aciclovir). Viral meningitis = meningeal inflammation without parenchymal involvement — usually self-limiting (enteroviruses). Start aciclovir immediately if encephalitis suspected — do not wait for PCR.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Distinguish viral meningitis (self-limiting) from viral encephalitis (emergency)
  • Recognise HSV encephalitis clinical-radiological signature (temporal lobe, personality change, seizures)
  • Initiate empirical aciclovir within 1 hour for suspected encephalitis
  • Apply CSF interpretation: lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, normal/mildly ↑ protein
  • Know indications for MRI, EEG, PCR panel, autoantibody panels
  • Manage complications: raised ICP, seizures, long-term neurocognitive sequelae

2. Definition

SyndromeDefinitionKey Feature
Viral meningitisInflammation of leptomeninges by virusNo parenchymal involvement; AMS rare; self-limiting
Viral encephalitisInflammation of brain parenchyma by virusAMS, focal deficits, seizures, personality change; high morbidity/mortality
MeningoencephalitisCombined meningeal + parenchymal involvementOverlap features

3. Core Microbiology — Aetiology

CategoryPathogensKey Features
Herpesviruses (treatable)HSV-1 (adult encephalitis #1), HSV-2 (neonatal, genital, Mollaret's), VZV (shingles, Ramsay Hunt), EBV, CMV, HHV-6Aciclovir effective; HSV-1 = temporal lobe; VZV = vasculopathy
EnterovirusesCoxsackie, Echo, Enterovirus D68, PoliovirusMost common viral meningitis; summer/autumn; hand-foot-mouth, herpangina, acute flaccid myelitis (EV-D68)
Arboviruses (vector-borne)West Nile, Japanese encephalitis, St. Louis, La Crosse, dengue neurotropic, Zika, chikungunya, tick-borne encephalitisGeographic; Culex/Aedes mosquitoes; CSF IgM +ve
Respiratory virusesInfluenza, RSV, hMPV, parainfluenza, adenovirus, SARS-CoV-2Post-viral / para-infectious; acute necrotising encephalopathy (ANEC)
ZoonoticRabies (fatal), Nipah, Hendra, Lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus (LCMV)Animal exposure; rabies = hydrophobia, agitation, fatal
OtherMumps (pre-vaccine era), measles (SSPE), HIV (acute seroconversion, chronic)Vaccine-preventable; SSPE = subacute sclerosing panencephalitis

4. Normal Values / Important Cut-offs

ParameterViral MeningitisViral EncephalitisBacterial Meningitis
Opening pressureNormal or mildly ↑ (10–25)Normal/mildly ↑↑↑ (>25)
WBC count10–500 (lymphocytes)10–500 (lymphocytes)↑↑↑ neutrophils
ProteinNormal/mildly ↑ (45–150)Mildly ↑ (50–200)↑↑ (100–500)
Glucose (CSF/serum)Normal (>0.6)Normal (>0.6)↓↓ (<0.4)
Gram stainNegativeNegativePositive 60–80%
PCR panelEnterovirus, HSV, VZV, parechovirusHSV-1/2, VZV, enterovirus, arbovirusesBacterial targets
CSF IgM (arboviruses)—Positive (diagnostic)—
Autoantibody panel—Anti-NMDA, LGI1, CASPR2, etc.—
[!tip]
CSF glucose NORMAL = viral (or TB/fungal if low); CSF glucose LOW = bacterial/TB/fungal. Normal glucose essentially rules out typical bacterial meningitis.

5. Clinical Differentiation

FeatureViral MeningitisViral Encephalitis
OnsetAcute (hours–days)Subacute (days–weeks)
FeverYesYes
HeadacheSevereModerate-severe
Neck stiffnessProminentVariable (may be absent)
PhotophobiaCommonVariable
Altered mental statusAbsent/mildProminent (confusion, agitation, coma)
Focal neurological deficitsAbsentPresent (aphasia, hemiparesis, cranial nerves)
SeizuresRareCommon (30–50%)
Personality/behaviour changeAbsentProminent (HSV: temporal lobe → personality, memory)
OutcomeFull recovery (days–2w)Mortality 10–30%; sequelae 30–50%

6. Approach / Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected CNS infection: fever + headache + neck stiffness +/- AMS/seizures
↓
Encephalitis features? AMS, focal signs, seizures, personality change
↓
EMERGENCY: Start IV Aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h IMMEDIATELY
Supportive care; LP if no contraindications
↓
LP if safe (same contraindications as bacterial)
↓
CSF: cell count, protein, glucose, PCR panel (HSV, VZV, enterovirus), HSV PCR specific
↓
MRI brain with contrast (DWI, FLAIR, T2) — temporal lobe = HSV
↓
EEG if seizures suspected — temporal periodic complexes = HSV
↓
Autoantibody panel if PCR negative (anti-NMDA, LGI1, CASPR2, etc.)
↓
Continue aciclovir 14–21d if HSV PCR +ve; stop if -ve and alternative dx

7. Investigations

TestIndicationInterpretation
CSF HSV PCRAll suspected encephalitisGold standard for HSV; sensitivity 95–98%, specificity >99%; may be -ve <72h
Multiplex PCR panel (FilmArray)All CNS infectionsRapid (1h): HSV-1/2, VZV, enterovirus, parechovirus, HHV-6, bacteria
CSF VZV PCRShingles + CNS, Ramsay HuntSensitivity 70–90%
CSF enterovirus PCRSuspected viral meningitisHigh sensitivity; seasonal
Arbovirus IgM (CSF/serum)Travel, geographic exposureWest Nile, JE, dengue, Zika, TBE; IgM in CSF = CNS infection
MRI brain (contrast, DWI, FLAIR)All encephalitis; meningitis if atypicalHSV: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity medial temporal lobes ± insula ± cingulate; DWI restricted
EEGSeizures, encephalopathyHSV: periodic lateralised epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) / temporal periodic complexes
Autoantibody panel (CSF/serum)PCR-negative encephalitisAnti-NMDAR, LGI1, CASPR2, AMPAR, GABA-B, DPPX — treatable immune encephalitis
Serology (paired acute/convalescent)Arboviruses, specific viruses4-fold rise = acute infection
Blood PCR / NAATHIV, CMV, EBV, enterovirusSupplementary

8. Treatment

Specific Antivirals

VirusDrugDose (Adult)DurationNotes
HSV-1/2IV Aciclovir10mg/kg IV 8h (adjust renal)14–21 daysStart IMMEDIATELY — do not wait for PCR; renal adjust essential
VZVIV Aciclovir10–15mg/kg IV 8h10–14 daysHigher dose for encephalitis/vasculopathy
CMVGanciclovir / Valganciclovir5mg/kg IV 12h / 900mg PO 12h2–3w inductionImmunocompromised; monitor neutrophils
HHV-6Ganciclovir / Foscarnet5mg/kg IV 12h / 90mg/kg IV 8h2–3wPost-transplant encephalitis
EnterovirusPleconaril (investigational)——No licensed antiviral; supportive; IVIG for severe
ArbovirusesSupportive——No specific antiviral; IVIG sometimes used (West Nile)
RabiesPost-exposure prophylaxis ONLYRabies vaccine + HRIG—No treatment once symptomatic — fatal

Adjunctive / Supportive

MeasureIndication
Seizure controlLevetiracetam preferred (minimal interactions); phenytoin/valproate alternatives
Raised ICPHead elevation, mannitol/hypertonic saline, hyperventilation (temp), ICP monitoring
IVIGPost-infectious encephalomyelitis (ADEM), anti-NMDAR encephalitis, severe enterovirus
CorticosteroidsAutoimmune encephalitis (high-dose methylprednisolone); VZV vasculopathy; ADEM; NOT routine for viral
Plasma exchangeSevere autoimmune encephalitis, ADEM
RituximabRefractory anti-NMDAR, LGI1, CASPR2 encephalitis

9. Herpes Simplex Encephalitis (HSE) — THE MUST-KNOW

FeatureDetail
PathogenHSV-1 (>90% adult); HSV-2 (neonatal, immunocompromised)
PathogenesisReactivation from trigeminal ganglion → olfactory/temporal lobes
Peak ageBimodal: <3y and >50y
Classic triadFever, personality/behaviour change, seizures (focal temporal)
Other featuresAnosmia, aphasia, memory loss, hallucinations, psychosis
CSFLymphocytic pleocytosis (50–500), normal glucose, ↑ protein, RBCs common (haemorrhagic necrosis), HSV PCR +ve
MRIT2/FLAIR hyperintensity medial temporal lobes ± insula ± cingulate; haemorrhage on GRE/SWI; DWI restriction
EEGPeriodic lateralised epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) / temporal periodic complexes (specific but not sensitive)
TreatmentIV Aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h ×14–21d (renal adjust: CrCl 10–25 → 10mg/kg 12h; CrCl <10 → 10mg/kg 24h; HD → 10mg/kg post-dialysis)
MortalityUntreated 70%; treated 15–20%
SequelaeMemory impairment, personality change, epilepsy (30–50%)
Relapse10–25% at 1–3m (immune-mediated) — repeat MRI/LP, consider steroids

10. Autoimmune Encephalitis — Mimics Viral

SyndromeAntibodyClinical CluesTreatment
Anti-NMDAR encephalitisAnti-NMDARYoung women; psychiatric prodrome → seizures, dyskinesias, autonomic instability, catatonia; ovarian teratomaIVIG + methylprednisolone + rituximab; tumour removal
LGI1 encephalitisAnti-LGI1Older men; faciobrachial dystonic seizures (FBDS), hyponatraemia, limbic encephalitisIVIG + steroids; good response
CASPR2 encephalitisAnti-CASPR2Morvan syndrome (neuromyotonia, insomnia, autonomic), limbic encephalitisIVIG + steroids
Anti-GABA-BAnti-GABA-BLimbic encephalitis, seizures; SCLC associationTumour treatment + immunotherapy
ADEMPost-infectious / post-vaccinalChildren; multifocal demyelination; encephalopathy + polyfocal deficitsHigh-dose methylprednisolone + IVIG/plasma exchange

11. Red Flags / Emergencies

  • Suspected HSV encephalitis → Aciclovir within 1h — delay increases mortality
  • Raised ICP / herniation → mannitol, hypertonic saline, hyperventilation, neurosurgery
  • Status epilepticus → benzodiazepines → levetiracetam/phenytoin/phenobarbital → ICU
  • Autonomic instability (anti-NMDAR) → ICU monitoring
  • Rabies exposure → immediate PEP — once symptomatic, 100% fatal

12. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Bacterial meningitisCSF: neutrophils, low glucose, high protein; Gram stain +ve; rapid progression
TB meningitisSubacute >1w; CSF: lymphocytes, low glucose, high protein; basal exudates; GeneXpert
Fungal (Cryptococcal)Immunocompromised; India ink/CrAg +ve; ↑ opening pressure
Autoimmune encephalitisSubacute; psychiatric features; autoantibodies +ve; MRI may show limbic T2 hyperintensity
NeurosyphilisArgyll Robertson pupil, tabes dorsalis; CSF VDRL +ve; serology +ve
Malignancy (paraneoplastic, lymphoma)Subacute; systemic cancer signs; autoantibodies (Hu, Yo, Ma2); CSF cytology
Stroke (limbic infarct)Acute; DWI restriction territorial; no fever/pleocytosis
Metabolic / toxicHyponatraemia, hepatic encephalopathy, alcohol withdrawal, drugs

13. Special Situations

SituationManagement
PregnancyAciclovir safe (category B); HSV encephalitis = treat aggressively; VZV = treat
NeonatesHSV-2 encephalitis: aciclovir 20mg/kg IV 8h ×21d; CSF PCR, liver function, eye exam
ImmunocompromisedBroader differential: CMV, HHV-6, EBV, JC virus (PML), fungi, TB, toxoplasma; aciclovir still first-line for HSV
Renal impairmentAciclovir renal adjust critical — crystalluria, AKI risk; dose by CrCl
Post-HSCTHHV-6 encephalitis (ganciclovir/foscarnet); CMV; adenovirus

14. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Encephalitis vs meningitis: AMS + focal signs + seizures = encephalitis (emergency); meningitis = meningeal signs only, no parenchymal involvement
  • HSV-1 = most common identifiable cause of sporadic encephalitis — temporal lobe, personality change, seizures
  • Start IV aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h IMMEDIATELY — do NOT wait for PCR/LP/MRI
  • Aciclovir renal adjustment: CrCl 10–25 → 10mg/kg 12h; CrCl <10 → 10mg/kg 24h; HD → 10mg/kg post-HD
  • HSV PCR sensitivity 95–98%; may be negative <72h — repeat at 48–72h if high suspicion
  • MRI hallmark: bilateral medial temporal lobe T2/FLAIR hyperintensity ± haemorrhage ± DWI restriction
  • EEG hallmark: PLEDs / temporal periodic complexes
  • Autoimmune encephalitis mimics viral: anti-NMDAR (young women, psychiatric, ovarian teratoma), LGI1 (FBDS, hyponatraemia), CASPR2, ADEM
  • Enterovirus = most common viral meningitis (summer/autumn); self-limiting, supportive
  • Arboviruses: West Nile (acute flaccid paralysis), JE (rural Asia), dengue/Zika/chikungunya (travel)
  • Rabies: fatal once symptomatic; PEP = vaccine + HRIG (pre-exposure: vaccine only)

15. Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the most common cause of sporadic viral encephalitis? HSV-1.
  2. When do you start aciclovir in suspected encephalitis? Immediately — within 1 hour, do not wait for LP/MRI/PCR.
  3. What is the classic MRI finding in HSV encephalitis? Bilateral medial temporal lobe T2/FLAIR hyperintensity ± insula ± cingulate; haemorrhage common.
  4. How do you distinguish viral meningitis from encephalitis clinically? Encephalitis = AMS, focal deficits, seizures, personality change; meningitis = meningeal signs only, no parenchymal involvement.
  5. What is the aciclovir dose for HSV encephalitis and renal adjustment? 10mg/kg IV 8h ×14–21d; CrCl 10–25: 10mg/kg 12h; CrCl <10: 10mg/kg 24h; HD: 10mg/kg post-dialysis.
  6. Name three autoimmune encephalitis syndromes that mimic viral encephalitis. Anti-NMDAR, LGI1 (FBDS), CASPR2, ADEM.
  7. What is the most common cause of viral meningitis? Enteroviruses (coxsackie, echo) — summer/autumn.
  8. When do you suspect anti-NMDAR encephalitis? Young woman, psychiatric prodrome → seizures, dyskinesias, autonomic instability, catatonia; check for ovarian teratoma.

16. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Wait for HSV PCR before starting aciclovirNEVER — start aciclovir immediately; delay = mortality
Viral meningitis has low CSF glucoseNormal glucose in viral; low glucose = bacterial/TB/fungal
Aciclovir dose same for all indicationsEncephalitis: 10mg/kg 8h; herpes zoster: 10mg/kg 8h (or 800mg PO 5×/d); neonatal: 20mg/kg 8h
MRI normal rules out HSV encephalitisEarly MRI may be normal (first 24–48h); repeat at 48–72h; PCR is more sensitive early
All encephalitis is viralAutoimmune encephalitis common mimic — antibody panel if PCR negative
Arboviruses only in tropicsWest Nile in US/Europe; TBE in Europe/Asia; travel history key
IVIG for all viral encephalitisOnly for specific indications: anti-NMDAR, ADEM, post-infectious, severe enterovirus
Rabies treatable once symptomatic100% fatal — only PEP works

17. Mnemonics

  • HSV ENCEPHALITIS: HSV-1, Start aciclovir Very fast, ENCEPHALITIS = Emergency; Medial Temporal on MRI; PLEDs on EEG; ACiclovir 10mg/kg 8h; Lymphocytic CSF; Immediate start; Treat 14–21d; ICU if needed; Sequelae memory/seizures
  • AUTOIMMUNE ENCEPH: Anti-NMDAR (young F, teratoma), Unresponsive? FBDS = LGI1, Tumour check, Older M = LGI1/CASPR2, IVIG + steroids + rituximab, Memory limbic, Morvan = CASPR2, Unusual abs, NMDA receptor, EEG extreme delta brush

...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.53 Viral Haemorrhagic Fevers

Related: [[Fever in Returned Traveller and FUO]], [[Travel Medicine and Pre-Travel Advice]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Infection in Immunocompromised Host (Transplant, Biologics)]], [[Infection Control]]

[!important]
VHF = group of viral infections causing fever + bleeding diathesis + multi-organ failure. Ebola, Marburg, Lassa, CCHF, Dengue (severe), Yellow Fever. High case fatality (25-90%). Transmission: contact with infected body fluids (Ebola, Marburg), aerosols (Lassa), vectors (CCHF, Dengue, Yellow Fever). ISOLATE IMMEDIATELY on suspicion. PPE mandatory. Public health notification.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify VHF by virus family, vector, geography
  • Recognise clinical syndrome: fever + bleeding + multi-organ involvement
  • Apply immediate isolation and infection control measures
  • Initiate supportive management and specific antivirals where available
  • Apply contact tracing and infection control measures
  • Recognise when to suspect VHF in returned traveller

2. Classification

Virus FamilyVirusesVector/ReservoirGeography
FiloviridaeEbola virus (EBOV)Fruit bats; human-to-humanCentral/West Africa (DRC, Guinea, Sierra Leone, Liberia)
Marburg virus (MARV)Fruit bats; human-to-humanCentral/East/Southern Africa
ArenaviridaeLassa virus (LASV)Multimammate rat (Mastomys); aerosol/excretaWest Africa (Nigeria, Sierra Leone, Liberia, Guinea)
Lujo virusUnknown rodentSouthern Africa
Bunyaviridae (Nairoviridae)Crimean-Congo Haemorrhagic Fever (CCHF) virusHyalomma ticks; livestock; human-to-humanAfrica, Middle East, Asia, Eastern Europe
Rift Valley Fever (RVF) virusMosquitoes; livestockAfrica, Arabian Peninsula
FlaviviridaeDengue virus (DENV) — severe dengue/DHF/DSSAedes mosquitoesTropical/subtropical worldwide
Yellow Fever (YFV)Aedes/Haemagogus mosquitoesAfrica, South America
Kyasanur Forest Disease (KFD) virusTicks; monkeysIndia (Western Ghats)
Omsk Haemorrhagic FeverTicks; rodentsRussia (Western Siberia)
Alkhurma Haemorrhagic FeverTicks; camelsSaudi Arabia, Egypt

3. Clinical Syndrome

FeatureTypical Presentation
Incubation2–21 days (Ebola 2–21; Lassa 6–21; CCHF 1–13; Dengue 4–10)
ProdromeFever, myalgia, headache, fatigue, anorexia, nausea/vomiting
Haemorrhagic ManifestationsPetechiae, ecchymoses, mucosal bleeding (gums, nose), GI bleeding, haematuria, vaginal bleeding, injection site bleeding
Organ InvolvementHepatitis (↑ AST/ALT), renal failure, encephalopathy, myocarditis, pancreatitis, ARDS, DIC, shock
LaboratoryThrombocytopenia, leukopenia (Ebola, Lassa), ↑ AST/ALT, ↑ creatinine, prolonged PT/aPTT, fibrinogen ↓, D-dimer ↑

4. Key Viruses — Clinical Pearls

Ebola Virus Disease (EVD)

AspectDetails
TransmissionDirect contact with blood/body fluids of symptomatic patient; fomites; bushmeat; sexual (persistent in semen)
ClinicalSudden fever, severe headache, myalgia → vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain → haemorrhage (day 5–7), multi-organ failure
CFR50–90% (Zaire ebolavirus); Sudan ebolavirus ~50%
TreatmentInmazeb (atoltivimab/maftivimab/odesivimab) OR Ebanga (ansuvimab) — monoclonal antibodies; supportive care
VaccinerVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo) — single dose, ring vaccination

Marburg Virus Disease (MVD)

AspectDetails
TransmissionSimilar to Ebola; bat exposure (caves/mines)
ClinicalIndistinguishable from Ebola; severe MACULOPAPULAR RASH (day 5)
CFR24–88%
TreatmentSupportive; no licenced antiviral/vaccine (experimental monoclonal)

Lassa Fever

AspectDetails
TransmissionAerosol/excreta of Mastomys rats; person-to-person (body fluids); nosocomial
ClinicalInsidious onset; fever, pharyngitis, retrosternal pain, facial oedema, pleural effusions, sensorineural hearing loss (common sequela), encephalopathy
CFR1–15% (hospitalised); higher in pregnancy (3rd trimester)
TreatmentRibavirin IV (early = better outcome); supportive; convalescent plasma (experimental)
VaccineNone licenced (candidates in development)

Crimean-Congo Haemorrhagic Fever (CCHF)

AspectDetails
TransmissionHyalomma tick bite; contact with infected livestock blood/tissues; nosocomial
ClinicalSudden fever, myalgia, headache, dizziness → haemorrhage (day 3–5), hepatomegaly, confusion, multiorgan failure
CFR10–40%
TreatmentRibavirin IV (early = better outcome); supportive
VaccineNone licenced (inactivated vaccine in limited use)

Dengue Haemorrhagic Fever / Dengue Shock Syndrome (DHF/DSS)

AspectDetails
TransmissionAedes aegypti/albopictus mosquito
ClinicalDHF: fever, thrombocytopenia (<100k), haemorrhagic manifestations, plasma leakage (pleural effusion, ascites); DSS: shock, narrow pulse pressure
CFR<1% with proper fluid management; >20% untreated
TreatmentFluid management (crystalloids, colloids; avoid overload); no antiviral
VaccineDengvaxia — ONLY for seropositive individuals (9–45y)

5. Diagnosis

TestUtility
RT-PCR (blood)Gold standard; rapid (hours); available for Ebola, Marburg, Lassa, CCHF, Dengue, YF
IgM/IgG ELISAAcute/convalescent; Lassa, CCHF, Dengue, YF
Antigen Detection (Rapid Test)Dengue NS1; Ebola VP40 (field use)
Virus IsolationBSL-4; definitive; not for acute management
Routine LabsThrombocytopenia, ↑ AST/ALT, prolonged PT, DIC screen
[!critical]
VHF SUSPICION → IMMEDIATE ISOLATION + PPE + PUBLIC HEALTH NOTIFICATION. Do NOT send samples routine lab — use dedicated VHF pathway.

6. Management

Immediate (Hour 0)

ActionDetails
1. ISOLATENegative pressure room; PPE: double gloves, impermeable gown, N95/FFP3, face shield, hood, boots; trained buddy system for donning/doffing
2. NOTIFYPublic health authorities immediately (statutory notification); hospital IPC team
3. SUPPORTIVEFluid resuscitation (crystalloids, avoid overload), blood products (platelets, FFP, cryoprecipitate for DIC), vasopressors, renal replacement, mechanical ventilation as needed
4. SPECIFIC ANTIVIRALSEbola: Inmazeb (atoltivimab/maftivimab/odesivimab) OR Ebanga (ansuvimab); Lassa/CCHF: Ribavirin IV (30mg/kg load → 16mg/kg q6h ×4d → 8mg/kg q8h ×6d); Dengue: Fluid management only; Marburg: Supportive only

Infection Control (IPC)

MeasureStandard
IsolationNegative pressure room; anteroom; dedicated equipment
PPEDouble gloves; impermeable gown; N95/FFP3; face shield; hood; boots; buddy system
WasteCategory A infectious; autoclave/incinerate
DecontaminationHypochlorite 10,000ppm (1%) for surfaces; 0.5% chlorine for skin
Contact Tracing21 days (Ebola/Marburg); 17 days (Lassa); 14 days (CCHF)
Dead Body ManagementTrained team; minimal handling; sealed body bag; cremation/deep burial

Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP)

VirusPEP
EbolaMonoclonal antibodies (Inmazeb/Ebanga) ± rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine (ring vaccination)
Lassa/CCHFRibavirin (controversial; limited evidence)
DengueNone (no PEPreventative vaccine for seronegative)
MarburgNone licenced

7. VHF in Returned Traveller

ScenarioAction
Fever + travel to VHF-endemic area + bleeding/contact historyISOLATE IMMEDIATELY; notify IPC/public health; send blood for RT-PCR (Ebola, Marburg, Lassa, CCHF, Dengue, YF)
HCW needlestick from VHF patientImmediate washing; baseline labs; PEP per virus (Ebola: vaccine ± mAb; Lassa/CCHF: ribavirin controversial); monitor 21 days

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • VHF = fever + bleeding + multi-organ failure; high CFR
  • Ebola/Marburg = Filoviridae; contact fluids; CFR 50-90%; monoclonal antibodies (Inmazeb/Ebanga); rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine
  • Lassa = Arenaviridae; Mastomys rat; aerosol; facial oedema, hearing loss; Ribavirin IV early
  • CCHF = Nairoviridae; Hyalomma tick; livestock; Ribavirin IV early
  • Dengue DHF/DSS = plasma leakage, thrombocytopenia; fluid management; Dengvaxia seropositive only
  • ISOLATE IMMEDIATELY on suspicion; PPE; negative pressure; public health notification
  • RT-PCR = gold standard; send via dedicated VHF pathway
  • Ribavirin for Lassa/CCHF (early = better)
  • Ebola: Inmazeb/Ebanga monoclonals; rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine
  • No person-to-person for CCHF except nosocomial; Ebola/Marburg = body fluids; Lassa = aerosol/fluid

9. Common Viva Questions

  1. First action on suspecting VHF? Isolate immediately (negative pressure), PPE, notify public health.
  2. Which VHF has specific antiviral? Lassa & CCHF = Ribavirin; Ebola = monoclonal antibodies (Inmazeb/Ebanga).
  3. Lassa fever characteristic features? Facial oedema, pharyngitis, retrosternal pain, sensorineural hearing loss.
  4. CCHF transmission? Hyalomma tick bite; livestock contact; nosocomial.
  5. Ebola treatment? Inmazeb or Ebanga (monoclonal antibodies); supportive care; rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine for prevention.
  6. Dengue DHF vs DSS? DHF = plasma leakage + haemorrhage; DSS = shock (narrow pulse pressure).
  7. First action on VHF suspicion? ISOLATE (negative pressure), PPE, notify public health.
  8. Ribavirin use? Lassa fever & CCHF (early = better outcome).
  9. Ebola vaccine? rVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo) single dose.
  10. VHF in pregnancy? Lassa severe in 3rd trimester; Ebola high mortality; Ribavirin teratogenic (avoid in pregnancy).

10. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Ribavirin for all VHFOnly Lassa & CCHF (early); NOT for Ebola/Marburg/Dengue
Ebola = airborneContact with body fluids; NOT airborne (except lab aerosols)
Dengue vaccine for allDengvaxia ONLY seropositive (9-45y); seronegative = ↑ severe dengue
Ribavirin safe in pregnancyTeratogenic (Category X) — avoid in pregnancy; Lassa in pregnancy = high mortality
Ebola = always haemorrhageOnly 50% develop overt bleeding; fever + GI symptoms common presentation
Marburg = same as EbolaClinically similar but distinct virus; maculopapular rash more prominent in Marburg
CCHF = only AfricaAlso Middle East, Asia, Eastern Europe
Lassa = only ruralUrban transmission occurs (rat infestation); nosocomial outbreaks common
Vaccine for all VHFOnly Ebola (rVSV-ZEBOV), YF, Dengue (seropositive only) have licenced vaccines
VHF = always fatalSupportive care + early specific therapy ↓ mortality significantly

11. Mnemonics

  • VHF FIRST: Isolate Negative Pressure; PPE Buddy; Notify Public Health
  • EBOLA: Ebolavirus; Body fluids; Outbreak (Africa); Lethal (50-90%); Antiviral mAbs
  • LASSA: Lassa; Arenavirus; Sensorineural Hearing Loss; Aerosol (rat); Ribavirin
  • CCHF: Crimean-Congo; Hyalomma Tick; Ribavirin; Haemorrhage
  • DENGUE: Dengue; Epidemic; Ng Glucose Uea Electrolytes; Aedes mosquito; Haemorrhagic Fever (DHF) → Shock (DSS)
  • RIBAVIRIN: Lassa + CCHF (early) = Ribavirin; NOT Ebola/Marburg/Dengue
  • VHF ISOLATE: Immediate Single Occupancy Legative Pressure Anteroom Trained Expert

12. Mind Map

Viral Haemorrhagic Fevers
Filoviridae
Ebola: Contact fluids, CFR 50-90%, mAbs (Inmazeb/Ebanga), rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine
Marburg: Bats/caves, CFR 24-88%, maculopapular rash, supportive only
Arenaviridae
Lassa: Mastomys rat, aerosol, facial oedema, hearing loss, Ribavirin
Lujo: Rare, Southern Africa
Nairoviridae
*...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)*
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.54 Viral Hepatitis

Related: [[HIV Infection and AIDS]], [[Liver Disease]], [[Fever in Returned Traveller and FUO]], [[Travel Medicine and Pre-Travel Advice]], [[Infection in Pregnancy and Neonates]], [[Antimicrobial Prescribing Principles]]

[!important]
Viral hepatitis = liver inflammation from hepatotropic viruses (A, B, C, D, E). Hep A/E = faecal-oral; acute self-limiting. Hep B/C/D = blood-borne; chronic infection risk. Hep D = defective virus requiring Hep B. Hep E = severe in pregnancy (genotype 1). Hep B/C = leading causes of cirrhosis/HCC. DAA cure Hep C (>95%); Hep B suppressible not curable. Vaccines: Hep A, Hep B (also prevents Hep D).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Differentiate hepatitis viruses by transmission, clinical course, and chronicity
  • Interpret serology and molecular markers for each hepatitis virus
  • Stage Hepatitis B and C (fibrosis, cirrhosis, HCC risk)
  • Select and monitor antiviral therapy for Hepatitis B and C
  • Manage complications (cirrhosis, HCC, reactivation)
  • Apply vaccination and prevention strategies
  • Manage special populations (pregnancy, HIV coinfection, immunosuppression, transplant)

2. Overview of Hepatitis Viruses

FeatureHAVHBVHCVHDVHEV
FamilyPicornaviridaeHepadnaviridaeFlaviviridaeDeltaviridae (defective)Hepeviridae
GenomeRNA (+)DNA (partially ds)RNA (+)RNA (-)RNA (+)
TransmissionFaecal-oralBlood, sexual, verticalBlood (mainly), sexual, verticalBlood, sexual (requires HBV)Faecal-oral (waterborne)
ChronicityNever5–10% adults; 90% neonates70–85%Only with HBVRare (except immunosuppressed)
Incubation15–50 days60–150 days14–180 days30–180 days15–60 days
VaccineYesYesNoHep B vaccine preventsYes (China only)
TreatmentSupportiveNUCs (suppress), PegIFNDAA cure >95%PegIFN (limited)Supportive (Ribavirin if chronic)

3. Hepatitis A (HAV)

AspectDetails
TransmissionFaecal-oral (contaminated food/water, person-to-person); shellfish; outbreaks in institutions
Risk GroupsTravellers to endemic areas, MSM, PWID, household contacts, institutionalised
ClinicalAcute self-limiting; prodrome (fever, malaise, anorexia, nausea) → jaundice, dark urine, pale stools; no chronic infection; fulminant rare (<0.5%)
DiagnosisAnti-HAV IgM (acute); Anti-HAV IgG (past immunity); HAV RNA (research)
ManagementSupportive; no specific antiviral; hospitalise if severe/fulminant; avoid hepatotoxins
PreventionHep A vaccine (inactivated): 2 doses (0, 6–12m); Post-exposure: vaccine ± IgG (within 2w); hygiene
SpecialPregnancy: increased severity in 3rd trimester; vaccine safe

4. Hepatitis B (HBV)

Natural History

PhaseHBsAgHBeAgHBV DNAALTHistology
Immune Tolerant++Very high (>10⁷)NormalMinimal inflammation
Immune Active (HBeAg+)++High (>10⁵)ElevatedModerate-severe inflammation
Inactive Carrier+−Low (<2000)NormalMinimal
HBeAg-Negative Chronic Hepatitis+−Intermittent >2000ElevatedModerate-severe
Resolved−−UndetectableNormalResolved (anti-HBs+, anti-HBc+)

Serology Interpretation

MarkerMeaning
HBsAgCurrent infection (acute or chronic)
Anti-HBsImmunity (vaccine or resolved)
Anti-HBc IgMAcute/recent infection (<6m)
Anti-HBc IgGPast or chronic infection
HBeAgHigh replicative, high infectivity
Anti-HBeLower replicative (usually)
HBV DNAViral load (quantifies replication)

Fibrosis Staging & HCC Surveillance

ToolIndication
Transient Elastography (FibroScan)Non-invasive fibrosis staging (kPa)
APRI / FIB-4Serum-based fibrosis scores
Liver BiopsyGold standard (if noninvasive discordant)
HCC Surveillance6-monthly US ± AFP if cirrhosis OR high-risk chronic HBV (Asian male >40, female >50, family HCC, cirrhosis)

HBV Treatment Indications (EASL/AASLD/APASL)

IndicationCriteria
Immune Active (HBeAg+)HBV DNA >20,000 IU/mL + ALT >2×ULN (≥2× over 3–6m)
HBeAg-Negative Chronic HepatitisHBV DNA >2,000 IU/mL + ALT >ULN (≥2× over 3–6m)
Cirrhosis (Compensated)Any detectable HBV DNA
Decompensated CirrhosisAny detectable HBV DNA
Reactivation RiskImmunosuppression/chemo (prophylactic NUCs)

HBV Treatment — Nucleos(t)ide Analogues (NUCs)

DrugDosePotencyResistanceRenal AdjustNotes
Tenofovir Disoproxil Fumarate (TDF)245mg ODHighExtremely low↓ if CrCl <50Bone/renal toxicity; 1st-line
Tenofovir Alafenamide (TAF)25mg ODHighExtremely lowSafer renal/bonePreferred over TDF
Entecavir (ETV)0.5mg OD (1mg if NUC-experienced)HighLow (naïve); higher if LAM-experienced↓ if CrCl <501st-line
Lamivudine (LAM)100mg ODLowHigh (70% at 5y)↓ if CrCl <50Not 1st-line
Adefovir10mg ODLowModerate↓ if CrCl <50Nephrotoxic
Telbivudine600mg ODModerateModerate↓ if CrCl <50Myopathy, neuropathy
[!critical]
TAF/TDF/ETV = 1st-line (high barrier, potent). Lifelong therapy usually needed. Stop criteria: HBeAg+ → HBeAg seroconversion + consolidation 12m; HBeAg− → sustained suppression 3y + HBsAg loss (rare). PegIFN-α: finite duration (48w); lower SVR; side effects.

Special HBV Situations

SituationManagement
PregnancyTDF/TAF safe; avoid ETV (limited data); TDF 3rd trimester if HBV DNA >200,000 (reduce vertical transmission); infant HBIG + vaccine 12h
HIV CoinfectionTDF/TAF + 3TC/FTC covers both; DTG preferred; avoid 3TC monotherapy
Immunosuppression/ChemoProphylactic NUCs (TDF/TAF/ETV) if HBsAg+ or anti-HBc+ (reactivation risk)
HBV ReactivationNUCs immediately; monitor HBV DNA; continue ≥12m post-immunosuppression
Hep D CoinfectionPegIFN-α 48w only effective therapy; NUCs alone insufficient

5. Hepatitis C (HCV)

Natural History

StageOutcome
Acute HCV15–25% spontaneous clearance; 75–85% chronic
Chronic HCV20–30% cirrhosis over 20–30y; risk factors: age >50, male, alcohol, HIV, obesity, steatosis
CirrhosisDecompensation (ascites, varices, encephalopathy), HCC (1–4%/yr)

HCV Genotypes & Treatment Selection

GenotypePrevalencePreferred DAA RegimenDuration
GT140–50%Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir (GLE/PIB) 300/120mg OD OR Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir (SOF/VEL)8w (no cirrhosis) / 12w (cirrhosis)
GT215–20%GLE/PIB OR SOF/VEL8w / 12w
GT330–40%GLE/PIB (preferred) OR SOF/VEL8w (no cirrhosis) / 16w (cirrhosis)
GT410–15%GLE/PIB OR SOF/VEL12w
GT5/6RareGLE/PIB12w
[!key]
Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir (GLE/PIB) = pan-genotypic, 8w for non-cirrhotic, 12w cirrhotic. Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir (SOF/VEL) = pan-genotypic alternative. SVR >95% all genotypes.

HCV Treatment — Key Principles

AspectDetail
SVR DefinitionHCV RNA undetectable 12 weeks post-treatment (SVR12) = cure
Pre-treatmentGenotype, Fibrosis (FibroScan/APRI/FIB-4), NS5A RAS testing (GT3/1a if cirrhosis), Drug interactions, Renal function
MonitoringHCV RNA at week 4 (rapid virological response), end of treatment, SVR12
Retreatment FailureGLE/PIB + SOF + RBV (16–24w) OR SOF/VEL/VOX (Vosevi) 12w

Special HCV Situations

SituationManagement
HIV CoinfectionSame DAA regimens; check interactions (DTG/GLE/PIB OK); same SVR
Renal Impairment (CrCl<30)GLE/PIB (no renal adjust) OR SOF/VEL (avoid SOF if <30); Grazoprevir/Elbasvir alternative
Decompensated Cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B/C)SOF/VEL + RBV 12w (Child-Pugh B); avoid protease inhibitors (GLE/PIB contraindicated)
Post-Liver TransplantSOF/VEL 12w (preferred); GLE/PIB alternative; monitor interactions with CNI
Acute HCVDAA if persists >12w; treat as chronic

6. Hepatitis D (HDV)

AspectDetails
Defective VirusRequires HBsAg for assembly/transmission
TransmissionCo-infection (simultaneous HBV+HDV) or superinfection (HDV on chronic HBV)
ClinicalSuperinfection = more severe; rapid cirrhosis, high HCC risk
DiagnosisAnti-HDV IgM/IgG; HDV RNA (quantitative)
TreatmentPegIFN-α 48 weeks (only effective therapy); SVR ~25%; NUCs alone insufficient
New TherapiesBulevirtide (entry inhibitor) — approved EU; Lonafarnib (assembly inhibitor) in trials

7. Hepatitis E (HEV)

AspectDetails
GenotypesGT1/2 (human, faecal-oral, waterborne, endemic Asia/Africa); GT3/4 (zoonotic, pigs, undercooked pork, Europe/USA)
ClinicalAcute self-limiting (GT1/2); GT3/4 = chronic in immunosuppressed (transplant, HIV, chemo)
Severe in PregnancyGT1: 20–30% mortality in 3rd trimester (fulminant hepatic failure)
DiagnosisAnti-HEV IgM (acute); HEV RNA (blood/stool); Anti-HEV IgG (past)
TreatmentAcute: supportive; Chronic (GT3/4 in immunosuppressed): Ribavirin 600mg OD ×3m (reduce immunosuppression); PegIFN-α if ribavirin fails

8. Reactivation & Special Populations

HBV Reactivation

RiskProphylaxis
HBsAg+ (any immunosuppression)TDF/TAF/ETV prophylaxis (start before/during, continue ≥12m post)
HBsAg−/anti-HBc+ (rituximab, high-dose steroids, HSCT)TDF/TAF/ETV prophylaxis or close monitoring (HBV DNA q1–3m)
Resolved HBV (anti-HBc+ only)Low risk; monitor HBV DNA if high-risk (rituximab, HSCT, alemtuzumab)

HCV Reinfection

RiskManagement
Post-SVR reinfectionRe-treat with DAA; same SVR rates; harm reduction (PWID: OAT, needle exchange)

9. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • HAV/HEV = faecal-oral, acute only; HBV/HCV/HDV = blood-borne, chronic risk
  • HBV: immune tolerant → immune active → inactive carrier → HBeAg-negative CHB
  • HBV treatment: TDF/TAF/ETV 1st-line (high barrier); lifelong usually; stop if HBeAg seroconversion + 12m consolidation
  • HCV: DAA cure >95%; GLE/PIB pan-genotypic 8w (non-cirr) / 12w (cirr); GT3 cirrhosis = 16w SOF/VEL or 12w GLE/PIB
  • HDV: requires HBsAg; superinfection worse; PegIFN only effective therapy; bulevirtide emerging
  • HEV: GT1/2 acute; GT3/4 chronic in immunocompromised; pregnancy GT1 = 20-30% mortality; chronic = ribavirin
  • HBV reactivation: HBsAg+ = prophylactic NUCs; anti-HBc+ (rituximab/HSCT) = prophylactic NUCs
  • Pregnancy: HAV/HEV severe in 3rd tri; HBV TDF 3rd tri if DNA>200k; HCV treat postpartum; HEV severe in pregnancy
  • HCC surveillance: 6-monthly US ± AFP if cirrhosis or high-risk chronic HBV

10. Common Viva Questions

  1. HBV serology interpretation? HBsAg = infection; Anti-HBs = immunity; Anti-HBc IgM = acute; Anti-HBc IgG = past/chronic; HBeAg = high replication.
  2. HBV treatment indications? Immune active (DNA>20k + ALT>2×ULN), HBeAg-neg CHB (DNA>2k + ALT>ULN), cirrhosis (any DNA).
  3. HCV treatment — pan-genotypic regimen? GLE/PIB 300/120mg OD ×8w (non-cirr) / 12w (cirr); SOF/VEL alternative.
  4. HCV GT3 cirrhosis treatment? SOF/VEL 16w OR GLE/PIB 12w (with RBV if prior treatment failure).
  5. HDV treatment? PegIFN-α 48w only proven; bulevirtide emerging; NUCs alone insufficient.
  6. HEV in pregnancy? GT1 3rd trimester = 20-30% mortality; supportive only.
